syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements to hold an aviation document, as laid down in the CA Act 2023, sections 55 and 78
concept: To hold any aviation document--whether it's a licence, certificate of airworthiness, or operational approval--you need to meet the legislative requirements set out in the Civil Aviation Act 2023. Section 55 establishes the fundamental requirement: you must have a current, valid document issued by the Director. Section 78 covers the application process and what the Director needs to be satisfied with before issuing. Think of it this way: just meeting the technical qualifications isn't enough--you've got to have the paperwork, it's got to be current, and the Director's had to formally approve and issued it. This is why checking your certificate's validity date before every flight matters.
exam_tip: You'll likely be asked to identify what makes a document legally valid. The key is formal issue by the Director under the CA Act--self-assessment or informal approval doesn't cut it.
key_facts: Aviation documents must be issued by the Director under CA Act 2023, section 55
You cannot legally operate without a current, valid document
Section 78 outlines the Director's powers in approving applications
The document must be in your possession during flight operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 sections 55 and 78

syllabus_desc: Describe the criteria for the fit and proper person test, as laid down in the CA Act 2023, section 80
concept: The 'fit and proper person' test is the Director's gate-keeper tool for deciding whether you can hold an aviation document. Section 80 of the CA Act 2023 sets out the criteria: you've got to be competent to perform the functions, you've got to be trustworthy, and you've got to be physically and mentally fit. What does that mean in practice? Competence means you've got the training and experience for the role. Trustworthy means you don't have a history of serious breaches of the CARs or safety management failures. Physically and mentally fit means you've got a valid medical certificate and no undisclosed conditions. The Director can refuse or cancel your document if you fail any of these criteria.
exam_tip: Be ready to give examples: medical unfitness, a history of safety violations, or lack of training all fail the test. The three pillars are competence, trustworthiness, and fitness.
key_facts: Fit and proper person test has three criteria: competence, trustworthiness, and fitness
Competence requires appropriate training and experience for the role
Trustworthiness assessed on history of compliance with aviation law and safety standards
Fitness requires valid medical certificate and no undisclosed medical conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 section 80

syllabus_desc: Describe the duties of the PIC, as laid down in the CA Act 2023, sections 14, 15 and 16
concept: As PIC, you've got significant legal responsibilities under the CA Act 2023. Section 14 establishes your core duty: you've got to operate the aircraft in a way that doesn't endanger persons or property. Section 15 extends this--you've got to ensure the aircraft is airworthy, properly maintained, and crewed appropriately. Section 16 gives the Director powers to regulate how you operate. Your duties aren't just 'fly safely'--they're enforceable legal obligations. You're responsible for pre-flight checks, ensuring crew competence, complying with weight and balance limits, and aborting the flight if something's unsafe. The CAA takes these duties seriously: breaching them can result in prosecution, cancellation of your licence, or civil liability if anyone's harmed.
exam_tip: Remember: PIC duty is absolute. You cannot be overruled by ATC, company procedures, or pressure--safety is your call. Exam questions often test your understanding that you're ultimately responsible.
key_facts: Section 14: PIC must not operate an aircraft in a way that endangers persons or property
Section 15: PIC must ensure aircraft is airworthy, properly maintained, and appropriately crewed
Section 16: Director has powers to regulate PIC operations and impose conditions
PIC duty is non-delegable--you cannot pass this responsibility to anyone else
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 sections 14, 15, and 16

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to changes in their medical condition, as laid down in the CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 8
concept: Schedule 2, clause 8 of the CA Act 2023 places a clear responsibility on you: if your medical condition changes--whether it's a new diagnosis, medication, or even a persistent symptom--you've got to notify the Director. You can't wait until your next medical exam and hope it passes. If you develop a condition that might affect your fitness to fly--say, diabetes, heart arrhythmia, or even a new medication with dizziness as a side effect--you've got to declare it. The clause makes it your responsibility to keep the Director informed. Failure to disclose is serious: it's potentially a criminal offence under the CA Act, and it means your insurance and certificates are invalid if an incident occurs. The medical examiners expect you to proactively mention changes.
exam_tip: Expect questions about timing: you must notify when the condition develops, not when you remember or when you next see a doctor. Any condition affecting safety must be reported.
key_facts: You must notify the Director of any change in medical condition affecting your fitness
Notification must be made promptly--not delayed until your next medical exam
Failure to disclose a medical condition is a breach of your licence conditions
New medications, diagnoses, or symptoms must be assessed for safety impact before flying
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 Schedule 2, clause 8

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to the surrender of a medical certificate as laid down in the CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 18
concept: Schedule 2, clause 18 of the CA Act 2023 sets out your obligations regarding surrendering your medical certificate. If your medical certificate is suspended or cancelled, or if you become aware that you no longer meet the medical standards, you must surrender the certificate to the Director. This isn't optional--it's a legal requirement. The surrender requirement ensures that pilots who are unfit to fly don't continue to hold documents that suggest they're medically cleared. If you've had a serious medical event, been diagnosed with a disqualifying condition, or been directed by the Director to surrender, you must comply promptly. Failure to surrender when required is a breach of the CA Act.
exam_tip: Know the trigger events for surrender: suspension, cancellation, or failure to meet medical standards. The requirement is in Schedule 2, clause 18 of the CA Act 2023.
key_facts: You must surrender your medical certificate if suspended or cancelled
Surrender required if you no longer meet the medical standard
Obligation is in CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 18
Failure to surrender is a breach of the CA Act
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 Schedule 2, clause 18

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to safety offences, as laid down in the CA Act 2023, sections 40, 41 and 103
concept: The CA Act 2023 creates specific safety offences that licence holders need to understand. Section 40 deals with operating an aircraft in a manner that endangers persons or property. Section 41 covers performing aviation activities while impaired by alcohol or drugs. Section 103 sets out the penalties framework for these offences. These aren't minor regulatory breaches--they're criminal offences that can result in fines and imprisonment. As a licence holder, you're expected to know what constitutes a safety offence and understand that the consequences go beyond losing your licence. The Act takes a firm stance: if your actions endanger safety, you face criminal prosecution.
exam_tip: Know the three key sections: 40 (endangering safety), 41 (impairment), and 103 (penalties). These are criminal offences, not just regulatory breaches.
key_facts: Section 40: offence to operate aircraft endangering persons or property
Section 41: offence to perform aviation activities while impaired by alcohol or drugs
Section 103: penalties framework for safety offences
Safety offences are criminal and can result in fines and imprisonment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 sections 40, 41 and 103

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the following definitions from CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted): accelerate-stop distance available, accident, Act, aerobatic flight, aerodrome control service, aerodrome traffic circuit, aeronautical information circular, AIP supplement, aircraft category and category of aircraft, air traffic control service, air transport operation, air operation, airworthiness certificate, airworthiness directive, airworthy condition, alerting service, altitude, area control service, ATC clearance, ATC instruction, aviation event, AWIB service, basic weather report, ceiling, certificated organisation, Class 3.1A Flammable liquid, Class 3.1C Flammable liquid, Class 3.1D Flammable liquid, Class B cargo or baggage compartment, clearance limit, clearway, command practice, commercial transport operation, controlled airspace, controlled flight, co-pilot, crew member, dangerous goods, day, dual flight time, emergency locator transmitter, enhanced visual imaging system, final reserve fuel, fit and proper person, flight examiner, flight attendant, flight crew member, flight information service, flight level, flight manual, flight plan, flight time, height, heliport (Helicopter candidates only), introductory flight, incident, landing area, landing distance available, night, night vision goggles, night vision imaging systems, NOTAM, NVG operation, passenger, pilot-in-command (PIC), rating, regular air transport passenger service, SARTIME, serious incident, simultaneous operations -- visual conditions (AIP GEN), synthetic vision system, take-off distance available, take-off run available, take-off weight, Technical Instructions, threshold (rule 121.3), transition altitude (AIP GEN), transition layer (AIP GEN), transition level (AIP GEN), type, unlawful interference, UNICOM service, VFR flight, vicinity of an aerodrome (AIP GEN), visibility, visual meteorological conditions, visual reference (AIP GEN)
concept: CAR Part 1 contains the regulatory definitions that underpin all of New Zealand's civil aviation rules. Rev 38 has updated and restructured these definitions to align with the CA Act 2023. Some definitions have been added (like accelerate-stop distance available, Act, aerobatic flight, aerodrome control service, enhanced visual imaging system, synthetic vision system, NVG operation), while others from older revisions have been removed or reorganised. These aren't just words--they're legal terms with precise meanings. For example, 'air operation' and 'air transport operation' have distinct definitions that determine which regulatory regime applies to your flying. 'Airworthy condition' is legally defined and determines whether your aircraft can legally fly. Time-based definitions like 'day' and 'night' are based on civil twilight. Note that some definitions now reference AIP GEN rather than CAR Part 1. You need to know each definition precisely--getting them wrong means misunderstanding the rules themselves.
exam_tip: Rev 38 has significantly restructured the definitions list. Focus on the new additions (accelerate-stop distance available, Act, aerobatic flight, etc.) and note which definitions now reference AIP GEN. The exam tests these definitions both directly and indirectly through scenario questions.
key_facts: 83 definitions listed in Rev 38 (significantly restructured from earlier revisions)
Some definitions now reference AIP GEN rather than CAR Part 1
New definitions include: accelerate-stop distance available, Act, aerobatic flight, enhanced visual imaging system, synthetic vision system, NVG operation
Many abbreviation-style items (DME, ELT, ATC etc.) from older revisions have been removed from definitions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: accelerate-stop distance available. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Accelerate-stop distance available means the length of the take-off run available plus the length of any stopway.
exam_tip: This is one of the four declared distances. Don't confuse ASDA with TODA -- ASDA adds the stopway, TODA adds the clearway.
key_facts: ASDA = TORA + stopway
Stopway is a defined area beyond the runway end suitable for decelerating
Used for calculating accelerate-stop performance in the event of a rejected take-off
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: accident. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Accident means an occurrence associated with the operation of an aircraft that -- (a) in the case of an aircraft intended to be flown with any person on board, takes place between the time that any person boards the aircraft with the intention of flight and the time that all persons on board have disembarked and the engine or any propellers or rotors have come to rest; or (b) in the case of an aircraft intended to be flown without any person on board, takes place between the time that the aircraft is ready to move with the purpose of flight and the time that the aircraft comes to rest at the end of the flight and the primary propulsion system is shut down. The occurrence must be one in which -- (a) a person is fatally or seriously injured as a result of being in the aircraft, or direct contact with any part of the aircraft including any part that has become detached from the aircraft, or direct exposure to jet blast; or (b) the aircraft sustains damage or structural failure that adversely affects the structural strength, performance, or flight characteristics of the aircraft and would normally require major repair or replacement of the affected component; or (c) the aircraft is missing or is completely inaccessible.
exam_tip: The CA Act 2023 defines accident in section 6. Know the distinction between accident and incident -- accidents involve death, serious injury, or substantial aircraft damage.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 6
Involves death, serious injury, or substantial aircraft damage
Must be reported under CAR Part 12
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s6

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Act. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Act means the Civil Aviation Act 2023.
exam_tip: Straightforward -- whenever you see 'the Act' in the CARs, it means the Civil Aviation Act 2023. This replaced the 1990 Act.
key_facts: Act means the Civil Aviation Act 2023
Replaced the Civil Aviation Act 1990
All CAR references to 'the Act' point to this legislation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerobatic flight. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Aerobatic flight means -- (1) an intentional manoeuvre in which the aircraft is in sustained inverted flight or is rolled from upright to inverted or from inverted to upright position; or (2) manoeuvres such as rolls, loops, spins, upward vertical flight culminating in a stall turn, hammerhead or whip stall, or a combination of such manoeuvres.
exam_tip: The key word is 'intentional.' A steep turn in the circuit isn't aerobatic flight. Know the two limbs: sustained inverted flight, or specific manoeuvres like rolls, loops, and spins.
key_facts: Two-part definition: (1) sustained inverted flight or rolling to/from inverted, (2) rolls, loops, spins, stall turns, hammerheads
Must be intentional
Subject to restrictions under CAR Part 91
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerodrome control service. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Aerodrome control service means an air traffic control service provided for the control of aerodrome traffic.
exam_tip: This is defined in the CA Act 2023, not CAR Part 1 directly. It's the ATC service provided to aerodrome traffic.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
ATC service provided to aerodrome traffic
Provided by an aerodrome control tower
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerodrome traffic circuit. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Aerodrome traffic circuit means the pattern flown by aircraft operating in the vicinity of an aerodrome.
exam_tip: The circuit pattern -- defined in the Act. Know the standard circuit direction (left-hand unless published otherwise) and the components: crosswind, downwind, base, final.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Standard is left-hand unless published otherwise
Components: upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aeronautical information circular. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Aeronautical Information Circular means a notice containing information that -- (1) does not qualify for the origination of a NOTAM or for inclusion in the AIPNZ; and (2) relates to flight safety, air navigation, technical, administrative or legislative matters.
exam_tip: AICs fill the gap between NOTAMs and AIP amendments. They cover information that's important but doesn't fit in the AIP or warrant a NOTAM.
key_facts: Does not qualify for NOTAM or AIP inclusion
Covers flight safety, air navigation, technical, administrative, or legislative matters
Published by the CAA as supplementary information
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: AIP supplement. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: AIP Supplement means temporary changes to the information contained in the AIPNZ that are published by means of special pages.
exam_tip: AIP Supplements cover temporary changes -- think temporary restricted areas, airspace changes during events, or seasonal variations. They're published as special pages within the AIPNZ.
key_facts: Temporary changes to AIPNZ information
Published as special pages
Different from AIP Amendments which are permanent changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aircraft category and category of aircraft. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Aircraft category and category of aircraft means any one of the following classes of aircraft: aeroplane, balloon, glider, hang glider, helicopter, or microlight.
exam_tip: Six categories in NZ: aeroplane, balloon, glider, hang glider, helicopter, microlight. Your licence and ratings are specific to a category.
key_facts: Six categories: aeroplane, balloon, glider, hang glider, helicopter, microlight
Licence privileges are category-specific
Don't confuse 'category' with 'type' -- type is more specific
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: air traffic control service. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Air traffic control service -- (a) means a service provided for the purposes of -- (i) preventing collisions between aircraft and between aircraft and obstructions on any manoeuvring area; and (ii) expediting and maintaining a safe and efficient flow of air traffic; and (b) includes -- (i) an aerodrome control service; (ii) an area control service; (iii) an approach control service.
exam_tip: ATC service -- defined in the Act. Covers aerodrome control, approach control, and area control services.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Encompasses aerodrome, approach, and area control services
Provided by certificated ATS providers under Part 172
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: air transport operation. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Air transport operation means an operation for the carriage of passengers or goods by air for hire or reward except -- (1) a commercial transport operation; (2) an adventure aviation operation; (3) a helicopter external load operation under Part 133; (4) an agricultural aircraft operation under Part 137; (5) an introductory flight carried out under rule 91.227H; (6) a cost sharing flight under rule 91.227B.
exam_tip: Air transport is the big one -- carrying passengers or goods for hire or reward. But note the six exceptions that carve out specific operation types. Know these exceptions.
key_facts: Carriage of passengers or goods for hire or reward
Six exceptions: commercial transport, adventure aviation, heli external load, agricultural, introductory flight, cost sharing
Governed primarily by CAR Parts 119 and 135
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: air operation. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Air operation means an air transport operation, a commercial transport operation, or an adventure aviation operation.
exam_tip: Air operation is the umbrella term covering the three main types of commercial flying: air transport, commercial transport, and adventure aviation.
key_facts: Three types: air transport operation, commercial transport operation, adventure aviation operation
Umbrella term for commercial operations
Each type has specific regulatory requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: airworthiness certificate. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Airworthiness certificate means -- (1) for a New Zealand registered aircraft, an airworthiness certificate issued by the Director under Part 21, Subpart H; and (2) for a foreign registered aircraft, an airworthiness certificate issued by the competent authority of the State of registry.
exam_tip: Two paths: NZ-registered aircraft get their certificate from the Director under Part 21; foreign aircraft rely on certificates from their home state.
key_facts: NZ aircraft: issued by Director under Part 21, Subpart H
Foreign aircraft: issued by competent authority of State of registry
Must be current and valid for the aircraft to be operated
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: airworthiness directive. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Airworthiness directive means a mandatory airworthiness requirement that specifies modifications, inspections, conditions, or limitations to be applied to an aircraft or aeronautical product to ensure continued safe operating conditions.
exam_tip: ADs are mandatory -- not optional. If an AD applies to your aircraft, it must be complied with. They address safety issues found after certification.
key_facts: Mandatory requirement -- not optional
Specifies modifications, inspections, conditions, or limitations
Ensures continued safe operating conditions
Non-compliance makes the aircraft unairworthy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: airworthy condition. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Airworthy condition means the condition of an aircraft, including its components, fuel, and other materials and substances essential to the manufacture and operation of the aircraft, that complies with all the requirements prescribed by the Civil Aviation Rules relating to design, manufacture, maintenance, modification, repair, and safety.
exam_tip: Airworthy condition isn't just 'the engine works.' It means the entire aircraft -- every component, all fuel, every substance -- complies with ALL the requirements in the CARs covering design, manufacture, maintenance, modification, repair, and safety.
key_facts: Covers the entire aircraft including components, fuel, and materials
Must comply with ALL CAR requirements for design, manufacture, maintenance, modification, repair, and safety
A single non-compliance can render the aircraft unairworthy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: alerting service. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Alerting service means an air traffic service provided to notify appropriate organisations regarding aircraft in need of search and rescue aid, and to assist such organisations as required.
exam_tip: Alerting service is about notifying search and rescue when an aircraft is in difficulty. Defined in the Act, section 5.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Service to notify search and rescue organisations
Activated when an aircraft is overdue or in distress
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: altitude. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Altitude means the vertical distance of a level, a point, or an object considered as a point, measured from mean sea level.
exam_tip: Altitude is measured from mean sea level (AMSL). Don't confuse it with height (measured from a ground reference) or flight level (measured from the standard datum of 1013.2 hPa).
key_facts: Measured from mean sea level (AMSL)
Different from height (above ground) and flight level (above 1013.2 hPa)
Used for terrain clearance calculations and airspace boundaries
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: area control service. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Area control service means an air traffic control service provided for controlled flights in controlled airspace.
exam_tip: Area control handles en-route traffic in controlled airspace. In NZ, this is provided by Airways from the area control centre.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
ATC service for controlled flights in controlled airspace en route
Provided from an area control centre
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ATC clearance. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: ATC clearance means authorisation for an aircraft to proceed under conditions specified by an air traffic control unit.
exam_tip: A clearance is authorisation to proceed under specific conditions. It doesn't guarantee separation from terrain or obstacles -- that's still your responsibility as PIC under VFR.
key_facts: Authorisation to proceed under specified conditions
Issued by an ATC unit
Does not relieve PIC of responsibility for terrain clearance under VFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ATC instruction. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: ATC instruction means a directive issued by ATC for the purpose of requiring a pilot to take a specific action.
exam_tip: An instruction is different from a clearance. A clearance authorises you to do something; an instruction directs you to do something specific. You must comply unless it would compromise safety.
key_facts: Directive requiring a pilot to take a specific action
Different from a clearance -- instruction is a direct command
Must be complied with unless safety would be compromised
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aviation event. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Aviation event means an event to be conducted below the minimum safe heights prescribed under Part 91 that is -- (1) an air show or practice for an air show; or (2) an air race or practice for an air race; or (3) an aerobatic competition; or (4) aerobatic training or practice.
exam_tip: Aviation events are specifically about operations below minimum safe heights -- air shows, air races, aerobatic competitions, and aerobatic training. Normal flying doesn't qualify.
key_facts: Operations below minimum safe heights
Four types: air show, air race, aerobatic competition, aerobatic training/practice
Subject to specific approval requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: AWIB service. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: AWIB Service means an automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information provided specifically for the facilitation of aviation, and for the avoidance of doubt, an AWIB service is not an air traffic service.
exam_tip: AWIB is an automatic broadcast -- you listen, it doesn't talk back. Importantly, it's NOT an air traffic service. Common at uncontrolled aerodromes in NZ. Don't confuse with ATIS (which is at controlled aerodromes).
key_facts: Automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information
NOT an air traffic service
Common at uncontrolled NZ aerodromes
Different from ATIS which is at controlled aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: basic weather report. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Basic weather report means a verbal comment, in support of aviation, describing any of the following current weather conditions observed at a particular place or airspace: (1) wind direction and strength; (2) mean sea level air pressure; (3) air temperature; (4) weather conditions and cloud cover.
exam_tip: A BWR is a verbal report of observed weather -- not a forecast. It covers wind, pressure, temperature, and cloud/weather. Typically provided by UNICOM or AFRU operators at uncontrolled aerodromes.
key_facts: Verbal comment on current observed weather
Covers: wind, MSL pressure, temperature, weather/cloud
Not a forecast -- it's what's happening right now
Typically provided at uncontrolled aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ceiling. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Ceiling means the height above ground or water of the base of the lowest layer of cloud below 20 000 feet covering more than half the sky.
exam_tip: Ceiling requires more than half the sky to be covered -- that's BKN (5-7 oktas) or OVC (8 oktas). SCT cloud doesn't constitute a ceiling. And it's measured from the ground, not sea level.
key_facts: Base of lowest cloud layer below 20,000 ft covering more than half the sky
BKN or OVC -- not FEW or SCT
Measured as height above ground or water, not altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: certificated organisation. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Certificated organisation means an organisation issued with a certificate under rules made under the Act.
exam_tip: Any organisation holding a certificate under the CARs -- maintenance organisations, air operators, training organisations, ATS providers, etc.
key_facts: Organisation issued a certificate under the Civil Aviation Rules
Includes maintenance orgs, air operators, training orgs, ATS providers
Must comply with conditions of their certificate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Class 3.1A Flammable liquid. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Class 3.1A -- Flammable liquid means a liquid that has a flash point of below 23 degrees C and has an initial boiling point (IBP) of a maximum 35 degrees C, and includes petrol based products.
exam_tip: Class 3.1A is the most volatile -- flash point below 23 degrees C with a low boiling point. Think petrol and avgas. Know the three flammable liquid classes for dangerous goods questions.
key_facts: Flash point below 23 degrees C, IBP maximum 35 degrees C
Includes petrol-based products (avgas)
Most volatile of the three flammable liquid classes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Class 3.1C Flammable liquid. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Class 3.1C -- Flammable liquid means a liquid that has a flash point at or above 23 degrees C, but at or below 60 degrees C, and includes kerosene based products.
exam_tip: Class 3.1C covers the middle range -- flash point 23-60 degrees C. This includes kerosene-based products like Jet A-1. Most relevant for turbine aircraft fuel.
key_facts: Flash point at or above 23 degrees C, at or below 60 degrees C
Includes kerosene-based products (Jet A-1)
Middle volatility class
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Class 3.1D Flammable liquid. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Class 3.1D -- Flammable liquid means a liquid that has a flash point of above 60 degrees C, but at or below 93 degrees C, and includes diesel products.
exam_tip: Class 3.1D is the least volatile of the three -- flash point 60-93 degrees C. Diesel products. Less common in aviation but still classified as dangerous goods.
key_facts: Flash point above 60 degrees C, at or below 93 degrees C
Includes diesel products
Least volatile of the three flammable liquid classes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Class B cargo or baggage compartment. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Class B cargo or baggage compartment means a cargo or baggage compartment in which -- (1) there is sufficient access in flight to enable a crew member to effectively reach any part of the compartment with the contents of a hand fire extinguisher; and (2) when the access provisions are being used, no hazardous quantity of smoke, flames, or extinguishing agent will enter any compartment occupied by the crew or passengers; and (3) there is a separate approved smoke detector or fire detector system to give warning at the pilot or flight engineer station.
exam_tip: Class B compartments are accessible in flight -- you can physically get to a fire with a hand extinguisher. Three requirements: access, no smoke/flames entering occupied areas, and a smoke/fire detection system.
key_facts: Accessible in flight with hand fire extinguisher
No hazardous smoke or flames entering crew/passenger areas during access
Must have approved smoke or fire detection system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: clearance limit. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Clearance limit means the point to which an aircraft is granted an ATC clearance.
exam_tip: Simple but important -- the clearance limit is where your clearance ends. You can't proceed beyond it without a new clearance.
key_facts: The point to which an ATC clearance is valid
Cannot proceed beyond without further clearance
Often an aerodrome, fix, or reporting point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: clearway. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Clearway means a defined rectangular area on the ground or water, at the departure end of the runway -- (1) under the control of the aerodrome operator; or (2) with the agreement of the authority controlling the clearway -- selected or prepared as a suitable area over which an aeroplane may make a portion of its initial climb to a specified height.
exam_tip: A clearway extends beyond the runway end and provides obstacle-free area for the initial climb. It adds to take-off distance available (TODA) but NOT to accelerate-stop distance. Don't confuse with stopway.
key_facts: Defined rectangular area beyond departure end of runway
Under aerodrome operator control or authority agreement
Used for initial climb portion
Adds to TODA, not ASDA -- don't confuse with stopway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: command practice. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Command practice means the performance by a co-pilot of the duties and functions of a pilot-in-command during a flight under the supervision of a pilot-in-command designated for the purpose by the operator.
exam_tip: Command practice lets a co-pilot act as PIC under supervision. The real PIC is still designated by the operator and remains responsible. This is how co-pilots build PIC experience in multi-crew operations.
key_facts: Co-pilot performing PIC duties under supervision
Supervising PIC is designated by the operator
Used for building co-pilot PIC experience in multi-crew operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: commercial transport operation. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Commercial transport operation means an operation for the carriage of passengers or goods by air for hire or reward -- (1) where each passenger is performing, or undergoing training to perform, a task or duty on the operation; or the passengers or goods are carried to or from a remote aerodrome -- (2) except those operations in paragraph (1) that are air transport operations, adventure aviation operations, helicopter external load operations under Part 133, agricultural aircraft operations under Part 137, introductory flights under rule 91.227H, or cost sharing flights under rule 91.227B.
exam_tip: Commercial transport is a specific subset of hire-or-reward operations where passengers are working (performing or training for a task) or being carried to/from remote aerodromes. It's distinct from air transport operations.
key_facts: Hire or reward operation with specific conditions
Passengers must be performing/training for a duty, OR carried to/from remote aerodrome
Distinct from air transport operations
Subject to specific exceptions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: controlled airspace. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Controlled airspace means an airspace of defined dimensions within which an air traffic control service is provided in accordance with airspace classification.
exam_tip: Controlled airspace in NZ includes Classes A, C, and D. You need an ATC clearance to enter. The specific boundaries and classifications are published in the AIP ENR.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Includes Class A, C, and D airspace in NZ
ATC clearance required to enter
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: controlled flight. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Controlled flight means any flight that is subject to an air traffic control clearance under the rules.
exam_tip: A controlled flight is any flight operating under an ATC clearance in controlled airspace. All IFR flights in controlled airspace are controlled flights; VFR flights become controlled flights when they receive a clearance to enter controlled airspace.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Flight subject to an ATC clearance
All IFR in controlled airspace; VFR when cleared into controlled airspace
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: co-pilot. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Co-pilot means a licensed pilot, serving in any piloting capacity other than as pilot-in-command; but does not include a pilot receiving flight instruction from a pilot on board the aircraft.
exam_tip: A co-pilot is a licensed pilot who isn't the PIC on that flight. Key exclusion: a student receiving instruction doesn't count as a co-pilot, even if they hold a licence.
key_facts: Licensed pilot serving in piloting capacity other than PIC
Excludes pilots receiving flight instruction from an on-board pilot
Must hold an appropriate pilot licence
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: crew member. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Crew member means a person carried by an aircraft who is -- (1) assigned by the operator as a flight crew member or flight attendant to perform a duty associated with the operation of the flight; or to perform a duty associated with the operation of the aircraft during flight time; or (2) a person carried for the sole purpose of undergoing or giving instruction in the control and navigation of the aircraft; or undergoing instruction as a flight engineer or flight attendant; or (3) authorised by the Director to exercise a function associated with the operation of the aircraft during flight time; or (4) a flight examiner; or (5) a person carried to perform a specified function necessary for the purpose of an operation under circumstances and conditions determined by the Director as specified in a notice under rule 91.227E.
exam_tip: Crew member has five categories -- it's broader than just pilots. Instructors, flight examiners, and Director-authorised persons all count. Know that passengers are NOT crew members.
key_facts: Five categories of crew member defined
Includes flight crew, cabin crew, instructors, examiners, Director-authorised persons
Passengers are specifically NOT crew members
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: dangerous goods. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Dangerous goods means articles or substances that are capable of posing a risk to health, safety, property, or the environment and -- (a) are listed in, or classified in accordance with, the ICAO's Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air; or (b) have properties that would result in the articles or substances being classified as dangerous goods under the ICAO's Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air.
exam_tip: Dangerous goods covers articles or substances that are hazardous to health, safety, property, or the environment. Regulated under CAR Part 92 and the ICAO Technical Instructions.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Hazardous to health, safety, property, or environment
Regulated under CAR Part 92 and ICAO Technical Instructions
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: day. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Day means the hours between -- (1) the beginning of morning civil twilight, which is when the centre of the rising sun's disc is 6 degrees below the horizon; and (2) the end of evening civil twilight, which is when the centre of the setting sun's disc is 6 degrees below the horizon.
exam_tip: Day isn't sunrise to sunset -- it's civil twilight to civil twilight. That's when the sun's centre is 6 degrees below the horizon. This gives you roughly 30 minutes of extra 'day' either side of sunrise/sunset, depending on latitude and time of year.
key_facts: Civil twilight to civil twilight
Sun's centre 6 degrees below the horizon
Approximately 30 minutes before sunrise to 30 minutes after sunset
Everything outside this period is 'night'
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: dual flight time. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Dual flight time means flight time during which a person is receiving flight instruction from an appropriately licensed and rated pilot on board a dual control aircraft.
exam_tip: Dual time requires three things: you're receiving instruction, the instructor is appropriately licensed and rated, and the aircraft has dual controls. Solo practice with an instructor observing from the ground doesn't count.
key_facts: Person receiving flight instruction
Instructor must be appropriately licensed and rated
Must be on board a dual control aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: emergency locator transmitter. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Emergency locator transmitter means an equipment that broadcasts a distinctive signal on a designated radio frequency to facilitate a search and rescue operation.
exam_tip: An ELT broadcasts on 406 MHz (and 121.5 MHz) to help search and rescue find you after an accident. There are two types you need to know: ELT(AF) -- automatic fixed, permanently attached; and ELT(S) -- survival, portable and manually activated.
key_facts: Broadcasts distinctive signal on designated frequency
Facilitates search and rescue operations
Two main types: ELT(AF) automatic fixed, ELT(S) survival/portable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: enhanced visual imaging system. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Enhanced vision imaging system refers to technology which uses imaging sensors to see in front and along the flight path of aircraft to display an image of the external scene topography to the flight deck.
exam_tip: EVIS uses sensors (infrared, millimetre wave) to display the external scene on the flight deck. It shows what's actually there in real time -- unlike synthetic vision which uses databases.
key_facts: Uses imaging sensors to see along flight path
Displays external scene topography to flight deck
Shows real-time actual conditions
Different from synthetic vision system which uses databases
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: final reserve fuel. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Final reserve fuel means the minimum quantity of fuel required to provide a margin to secure the safe completion of a flight in the event of any unplanned manoeuvring in the vicinity of the destination or alternate or a suitable aerodrome, as the case may be, and in ordinary circumstances remains on board until completion of the landing.
exam_tip: Final reserve is your last-resort fuel -- it's there for unplanned manoeuvring near your destination or alternate. You should never plan to use it. Under CAR 91, it's 30 minutes for day VFR (aeroplane) and 45 minutes for night VFR.
key_facts: Minimum fuel for unplanned manoeuvring near destination/alternate
Should remain on board until landing
30 min day VFR / 45 min night VFR for aeroplanes under Part 91
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: fit and proper person. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Fit and proper person means a person who satisfies the fit and proper person test specified in the Act.
exam_tip: The fit and proper person test is in the CA Act 2023 (section 80). It covers competence, trustworthiness, and fitness. The Director uses this test when deciding whether to issue or continue an aviation document.
key_facts: Satisfies the test specified in the CA Act 2023
Test covers competence, trustworthiness, and fitness
Applied by the Director for aviation document decisions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight examiner. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight examiner means a person who holds a flight examiner rating issued in accordance with Part 61 or is approved by the Director to conduct specific flight tests.
exam_tip: Flight examiners either hold a Part 61 flight examiner rating or have specific Director approval. They conduct flight tests on behalf of the Director.
key_facts: Holds flight examiner rating under Part 61, or Director-approved
Conducts flight tests on behalf of the Director
Must maintain currency and competency requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight attendant. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight attendant means an appropriately trained person assigned by the operator to be responsible to the pilot-in-command for passenger safety on an aircraft.
exam_tip: A flight attendant's primary role is passenger safety, and they report to the PIC. They must be appropriately trained -- not just anyone sitting in the cabin.
key_facts: Appropriately trained person
Assigned by the operator
Responsible to the PIC for passenger safety
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight crew member. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight crew member means an appropriately qualified person assigned by the operator for duty in an aircraft during flight time as a pilot or flight engineer.
exam_tip: Flight crew members are pilots and flight engineers -- people qualified and assigned to operate the aircraft. Cabin crew are crew members but not flight crew members.
key_facts: Qualified person assigned for duty during flight time
Pilots and flight engineers
Cabin crew are crew members but NOT flight crew members
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight information service. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Flight information service means an air traffic service provided for the purpose of giving advice and information intended for the safe and efficient conduct of flights.
exam_tip: FIS provides information useful for the safe and efficient conduct of flights. In NZ, this includes weather updates, traffic information, and NOTAM advice. It's an information service, not a control service.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Provides information for safe and efficient flight conduct
Information service -- not a control service
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight level. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight level means a surface of constant atmospheric pressure expressed in terms of hundreds of feet that -- (1) is related to a specific datum 1013.2 hPa; and (2) is separated from other such surfaces by specific pressure intervals.
exam_tip: Flight levels are pressure altitudes based on the standard datum of 1013.2 hPa. FL150 = 15,000 ft on 1013.2 hPa. In NZ, the transition altitude is 11,000 ft -- above that, you set 1013.2 and use flight levels.
key_facts: Surface of constant atmospheric pressure in hundreds of feet
Referenced to standard datum 1013.2 hPa
NZ transition altitude is 11,000 ft
Separated by specific pressure intervals
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight manual. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight manual means a manual, associated with the certificate of airworthiness, containing limitations within which the aircraft may be considered airworthy, and instructions and information necessary to the flight crew members for the safe operation of the aircraft.
exam_tip: The flight manual is tied to the C of A and contains the limitations and information you need to operate safely. Operating outside flight manual limitations means operating an unairworthy aircraft.
key_facts: Associated with the certificate of airworthiness
Contains aircraft limitations
Contains instructions and information for safe operation
Operating outside its limitations = unairworthy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight plan. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight plan means specified information that is required under the rules to be provided to an ATS unit or to a flight following service regarding an intended flight, or portion of a flight, of an aircraft.
exam_tip: A flight plan provides ATS with your planned flight details. Under Part 91 and Part 135, certain flights require a flight plan -- particularly IFR flights, flights into controlled airspace, and flights over water beyond gliding distance.
key_facts: Specified information provided to ATS or flight following service
Covers intended flight or portion of flight
Required for IFR, controlled airspace, and certain other flights
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight time. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Flight time means -- (1) for an aircraft other than a balloon, the total time from the moment an aircraft first moves for the purpose of flight until the moment it comes to rest at the end of the flight including any associated push back, taxiing and subsequent holding time; and (2) for a balloon, the total time from burner on for the purpose of inflation for flight until the envelope is completely deflated at the end of a flight.
exam_tip: Flight time starts when the aircraft first moves for flight (including pushback and taxi) and ends when it comes to rest. For balloons, it's burner-on to envelope deflated. This is what goes in your logbook.
key_facts: Starts when aircraft first moves for purpose of flight (including pushback/taxi)
Ends when aircraft comes to rest at end of flight
Balloons: burner on to envelope completely deflated
This is what you log in your pilot logbook
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: height. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Height means the vertical distance of a level, a point, or an object considered as a point, measured from a specified datum and includes the vertical dimension of an object.
exam_tip: Height is measured from a specified datum -- usually the ground or an aerodrome elevation. Don't confuse with altitude (measured from MSL) or elevation (a fixed point's height above MSL).
key_facts: Vertical distance from a specified datum
Includes vertical dimension of an object
Usually measured from ground level or aerodrome elevation
Different from altitude (MSL) and elevation (fixed point above MSL)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: heliport (Helicopter candidates only). CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Heliport means any defined area of land or water, and any defined area on a structure, intended or designed to be used either wholly or partly for the landing, departure, and surface movement of helicopters.
exam_tip: A heliport is specifically for helicopter operations -- on land, water, or a structure (like a building rooftop or offshore platform).
key_facts: Defined area for helicopter landing, departure, and surface movement
Can be on land, water, or a structure
Helicopter candidates only
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: introductory flight. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Introductory flight means a flight of short duration offered by a flight training organisation to a prospective trainee where the dominant purpose of the flight is to encourage the trainee to become a pilot.
exam_tip: An introductory flight is a taster -- offered by a training organisation to encourage someone to learn to fly. It's specifically exempted from air transport operation requirements.
key_facts: Short duration flight by a training organisation
Purpose is to encourage the person to become a pilot
Offered to prospective trainees
Exempted from air transport operation requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: incident. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Incident means any occurrence, other than an accident, that is associated with the operation of an aircraft and affects, or could affect, the safety of operation.
exam_tip: An incident is an occurrence, other than an accident, associated with the operation of an aircraft that affects or could affect safety. Less severe than an accident but still reportable under Part 12.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Occurrence other than an accident that affects or could affect safety
Reportable under CAR Part 12
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: landing area. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Landing area means that part of a movement area intended for the landing or take-off of aircraft.
exam_tip: The landing area is specifically the part of the movement area used for landing and take-off -- typically the runway(s). The movement area also includes taxiways and aprons.
key_facts: Part of the movement area for landing or take-off
Typically the runway(s)
Movement area also includes taxiways and aprons
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: landing distance available. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Landing distance available means the length of the runway that is declared by the aerodrome operator as available and suitable for the ground run of an aeroplane landing.
exam_tip: LDA is what the aerodrome operator declares as available for your landing roll. It may be less than the physical runway length if there's a displaced threshold.
key_facts: Length of runway declared available for landing ground run
Declared by aerodrome operator
May be less than physical runway length due to displaced threshold
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: night. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Night means the hours between -- (1) the end of evening civil twilight, which is when the centre of the setting sun's disc is 6 degrees below the horizon; and (2) the beginning of morning civil twilight, which is when the centre of the rising sun's disc is 6 degrees below the horizon.
exam_tip: Night is the inverse of day -- from end of evening civil twilight to beginning of morning civil twilight. Remember: sun's centre 6 degrees below the horizon. Night flying requires additional equipment, ratings, and recency.
key_facts: End of evening civil twilight to beginning of morning civil twilight
Sun's centre 6 degrees below the horizon
Night flying requires additional equipment and recency
Inverse of the 'day' definition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: night vision goggles. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Night vision goggles means a head-mounted, lightweight, and self-contained binocular appliance that amplifies ambient light used to enhance the ability to maintain visual reference to the surface at night.
exam_tip: NVGs are head-mounted binoculars that amplify ambient light. They help you see the surface at night but have limitations -- narrow field of view, no depth perception, and degraded performance in certain conditions.
key_facts: Head-mounted, lightweight, self-contained binocular appliance
Amplifies ambient light
Enhances visual reference to the surface at night
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: night vision imaging systems. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Night vision imaging system -- (1) refers to the integration of all the following elements for the successful and safe operation of an aircraft using NVGs: (i) operational procedures; (ii) suitable training, competency and currency requirements; (iii) NVGs and associated equipment; (iv) lighting system and associated aircraft components; (v) continuing airworthiness requirements; but (2) excludes enhanced vision imaging system and synthetic vision system.
exam_tip: NVIS is the complete system -- not just the goggles. It includes procedures, training, the NVGs themselves, aircraft lighting modifications, and ongoing airworthiness. EVIS and synthetic vision are specifically excluded.
key_facts: Complete integrated system for NVG operations
Includes: procedures, training, NVGs, lighting, airworthiness requirements
Excludes EVIS and synthetic vision system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: NOTAM. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: NOTAM means a notice distributed by means of telecommunication containing information concerning the establishment, condition or change in any aeronautical facility, service, procedure or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential to personnel concerned with flight operations.
exam_tip: NOTAMs are time-critical notices about changes that affect flight operations. Always check NOTAMs as part of your pre-flight planning -- they cover everything from runway closures to temporary restricted areas.
key_facts: Distributed by telecommunication
Covers changes to facilities, services, procedures, or hazards
Timely knowledge essential for flight operations personnel
Must be checked during pre-flight planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: NVG operation. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: NVG operation means an operation during any part of which NVGs are used to maintain visual reference to the surface in an aircraft which is NVIS equipped and approved for NVIS operations.
exam_tip: An NVG operation requires both the NVGs in use AND an NVIS-equipped and approved aircraft. You can't just strap on goggles in any aircraft -- the whole system must be approved.
key_facts: NVGs used to maintain visual reference to the surface
Aircraft must be NVIS equipped and approved
Both goggles AND aircraft approval required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: passenger. CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Passenger means any person carried on an aircraft other than a crew member. Note: The Civil Aviation Act 2023 s269(1) provides a specific definition for the context of international carriage by air (Part 8, subpart 3).
exam_tip: A passenger is anyone on board who isn't a crew member. Simple concept but important for determining regulatory requirements -- passenger numbers affect equipment requirements, flight attendant carriage, and operational category.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Anyone carried who is not a crew member
Passenger numbers affect equipment and operational requirements
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5, s269(1)

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: pilot-in-command (PIC). CA Act 2023, section 5
concept: Pilot-in-command, in relation to an aircraft -- (a) means the pilot on board the aircraft who is responsible for the operation and safety of the aircraft; and (b) in the absence of a pilot on board the aircraft, means the person who the rules specify as responsible for the operation and safety of the aircraft in accordance with the rules; and (c) in all other circumstances -- (i) means an individual nominated by the operator to be responsible for the operation and safety of the aircraft; and (ii) if no individual is nominated by the operator, means the operator.
exam_tip: The PIC has ultimate authority and responsibility for the operation and safety of the aircraft during flight time. This responsibility is non-delegable. Even with an autopilot engaged or an instructor on board, someone must be designated PIC.
key_facts: Defined in CA Act 2023, section 5
Ultimate authority and responsibility for aircraft operation and safety
Responsibility is non-delegable
Must always be designated for every flight
source: CAR Part 1, Civil Aviation Act 2023 s5

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: rating. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Rating means an authorisation entered on, or associated with, a licence, certificate, or logbook, and forming part of it, stating special conditions, privileges, or limitations relating to the licence or certificate.
exam_tip: A rating is an authorisation attached to your licence that gives you specific privileges or imposes limitations. Examples: instrument rating, type rating, instructor rating, night rating.
key_facts: Authorisation on or associated with a licence/certificate
States conditions, privileges, or limitations
Examples: instrument rating, type rating, instructor rating
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: regular air transport passenger service. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Regular air transport passenger service means a service offered by an operator consisting of four or more air transport operations for the carriage of passengers between two or more aerodromes within any consecutive 28 day period.
exam_tip: Four or more passenger operations between two or more aerodromes in any 28-day period. This is what distinguishes scheduled airline-type operations from charter work.
key_facts: Four or more air transport operations in 28 days
For carriage of passengers
Between two or more aerodromes
Distinguishes scheduled services from charter operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: SARTIME. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: SARTIME means the time nominated by a pilot for the initiation of alerting action.
exam_tip: SARTIME is the time you nominate -- if you haven't arrived or cancelled by this time, search and rescue procedures are initiated. Always set a realistic SARTIME and remember to close it on arrival.
key_facts: Time nominated by the pilot
Triggers alerting/SAR action if not cancelled
Must be realistic and must be closed on arrival
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: serious incident. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Serious incident means an incident involving circumstances indicating that an accident nearly occurred.
exam_tip: A serious incident is one where an accident nearly happened. The line between incident and serious incident matters for reporting requirements under Part 12.
key_facts: Incident where an accident nearly occurred
More severe than a standard incident
Specific reporting requirements under CAR Part 12
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: simultaneous operations -- visual conditions (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Simultaneous operations -- visual conditions means operations where simultaneous use of instrument and visual runways or approaches is conducted under specified visual meteorological conditions, as prescribed in AIP GEN.
exam_tip: SIMOPS under visual conditions allow simultaneous use of runways that would normally require sequential operations. The specific requirements are published in AIP GEN.
key_facts: Simultaneous use of instrument and visual runways/approaches
Conducted under specified VMC
Defined in AIP GEN
Specific requirements published for each aerodrome

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: synthetic vision system. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Synthetic vision system refers to a system which uses computer generated images of the external scene topography from the perspective of the flight deck, derived from aircraft attitude, high precision navigation solutions, and databases of terrain obstacles and relevant cultural features to display a synthetic vision image of the external scene topography to the flight deck.
exam_tip: SVS creates a computer-generated image of the terrain using databases and GPS position. Unlike EVIS which shows real-time sensor data, SVS shows a database-generated picture. The image is only as good as the database.
key_facts: Computer-generated images of external terrain
Uses aircraft attitude, navigation data, and terrain databases
Different from EVIS which uses real-time sensors
Image quality depends on database accuracy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: take-off distance available. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Take-off distance available means the length of the take-off run available plus the length of any clearway.
exam_tip: TODA = TORA + clearway. The clearway extends your available take-off distance beyond the runway end. Don't confuse with ASDA which adds stopway instead.
key_facts: TODA = TORA + clearway
Clearway extends available distance beyond runway end
Don't confuse with ASDA (which adds stopway)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: take-off run available. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Take-off run available means the length of the runway declared by the aerodrome operator as available and suitable for the ground run of an aeroplane taking-off.
exam_tip: TORA is the runway length available for your take-off ground run. It's the starting point for calculating TODA and ASDA.
key_facts: Length of runway declared available for take-off ground run
Declared by aerodrome operator
Base distance for calculating TODA and ASDA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: take-off weight. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Take-off weight means the weight of the aeroplane at the commencement of the take-off run and includes everything and everyone carried in or on the aeroplane at the commencement of the take-off run.
exam_tip: Take-off weight is measured at the start of the take-off run -- after taxi fuel has been burned. It includes absolutely everything: aircraft, fuel, passengers, baggage, cargo.
key_facts: Weight at commencement of take-off run
Includes everything and everyone on the aircraft
After taxi fuel burned but before take-off fuel consumed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Technical Instructions. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Technical Instructions means ICAO Document 9284 Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air approved and published by decision of the Council of the International Civil Aviation Organisation under Annex 18 of the Convention.
exam_tip: The Technical Instructions (ICAO Doc 9284) are the international standard for transporting dangerous goods by air. NZ law references these directly through CAR Part 92.
key_facts: ICAO Document 9284
Covers safe transport of dangerous goods by air
Published under ICAO Annex 18
Referenced by NZ CAR Part 92
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: threshold (rule 121.3). CAR Part 121, rule 121.3
concept: Threshold means that point where a 1:20 obstacle-free approach surface intersects the runway surface.
exam_tip: The Part 121 definition is technical -- it's the point where a 1:20 obstacle-free approach surface meets the runway. A displaced threshold means this intersection point has been moved down the runway -- you can use the area before it for take-off and roll-out but not for landing.
key_facts: Point where a 1:20 obstacle-free approach surface intersects the runway surface
Defined in CAR Part 121, rule 121.3
Displaced threshold: the intersection point is moved further down the runway
Area before a displaced threshold may still be used for take-off and roll-out

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition altitude (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Transition altitude means the altitude at or below which the vertical position of an aircraft is controlled by reference to altitudes (AMSL). In New Zealand, the transition altitude is 11,000 ft AMSL.
exam_tip: In NZ, the transition altitude is 11,000 ft. Below this, you use QNH (local altimeter setting) for altitude. At or above the transition level, you use 1013.2 hPa and flight levels.
key_facts: NZ transition altitude: 11,000 ft AMSL
Below: use QNH for altitude
At or above transition level: use 1013.2 hPa and flight levels
Defined in AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition layer (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Transition layer means the airspace between the transition altitude and the transition level.
exam_tip: The transition layer sits between the transition altitude (11,000 ft in NZ) and the transition level. You should not level off in the transition layer -- climb through it or descend through it.
key_facts: Airspace between transition altitude and transition level
Do not level off in the transition layer
Variable thickness depending on atmospheric pressure
Defined in AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition level (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Transition level means the lowest flight level available for use above the transition altitude.
exam_tip: The transition level is the lowest usable flight level above the transition altitude. In NZ with standard pressure, this is typically FL150, but it varies with atmospheric pressure.
key_facts: Lowest flight level available above transition altitude
Varies with atmospheric pressure
Typically FL150 in NZ under standard conditions
Defined in AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: type. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Type -- (1) in relation to the licensing of aviation personnel means all aircraft of the same basic design, including all modifications thereto except those modifications which result in a significant change in handling or flight characteristics; or (2) in relation to the certification of aircraft, aircraft engines, or propellers, means those aircraft, aircraft engines or propellers which are similar in design.
exam_tip: Type has two meanings depending on context. For licensing, it's all aircraft of the same basic design (so a C172M and C172P are the same type). For certification, it's aircraft similar in design.
key_facts: Two meanings depending on context
Licensing: same basic design, excluding major handling changes
Certification: aircraft similar in design
Don't confuse with 'category' which is broader (aeroplane, helicopter, etc.)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: unlawful interference. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Unlawful interference means an act or attempted act endangering a passenger, crew member, ground personnel, aircraft, or facility.
exam_tip: Unlawful interference covers hijacking, sabotage, and similar acts. Transponder code 7500 indicates unlawful interference. Report immediately.
key_facts: Act or attempted act endangering persons, aircraft, or facility
Transponder code 7500
Covers hijacking, sabotage, and similar acts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: UNICOM service. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: UNICOM Service means a ground radio communications service in the aeronautical mobile service providing local aerodrome information for the facilitation of aviation, and, for the avoidance of doubt, a UNICOM service is not an air traffic service.
exam_tip: UNICOM provides local aerodrome information by radio -- wind, QNH, traffic, runway in use. Crucially, it's NOT an air traffic service. The operator provides information; they don't give clearances or instructions.
key_facts: Ground radio service providing local aerodrome information
NOT an air traffic service
Provides information only -- no clearances or instructions
Common at smaller NZ aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: VFR flight. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: VFR flight means a flight conducted in accordance with the visual flight rules.
exam_tip: A VFR flight is conducted under the visual flight rules -- you maintain visual reference and comply with VMC minima. In NZ, VFR is governed primarily by CAR Part 91.
key_facts: Flight conducted under visual flight rules
Pilot maintains visual reference
Must comply with VMC minima
Governed by CAR Part 91 in NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: vicinity of an aerodrome (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Vicinity of an aerodrome means the airspace within a specified distance of an aerodrome where specific operating rules apply, as prescribed in AIP GEN.
exam_tip: The vicinity of an aerodrome is the area where circuit and approach operations occur and where specific rules about joining, radio calls, and separation apply. Check AIP GEN for the specific definition and distances.
key_facts: Defined in AIP GEN
Area where specific aerodrome operating rules apply
Relevant for circuit joining, radio calls, and separation

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visibility. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Visibility means the ability, as determined by atmospheric conditions and expressed in units of measurement, to see and identify prominent unlighted objects by day and prominent lighted objects by night.
exam_tip: Visibility is about what you can actually see -- unlighted objects by day, lighted objects by night. It's determined by atmospheric conditions (rain, fog, haze, dust). Different from RVR which is specific to the runway.
key_facts: Ability to see and identify objects as determined by atmospheric conditions
Unlighted objects by day, lighted objects by night
Expressed in units of measurement
Different from RVR (runway visual range)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visual meteorological conditions. CAR Part 1, rule 1.1
concept: Visual meteorological conditions means meteorological conditions expressed in terms of visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling equal to or better than specified minima.
exam_tip: VMC means conditions that meet or exceed the minimum visibility, cloud distance, and ceiling requirements for VFR flight. The specific minima vary by airspace class and are set out in CAR Part 91.
key_facts: Visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling at or better than specified minima
Minima vary by airspace class
Set out in CAR Part 91
Must be met to conduct VFR flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visual reference (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: Visual reference means the ability of the pilot to see sufficient ground features or landmarks to enable the pilot to maintain the aircraft in a desired attitude and to navigate by reference to the ground.
exam_tip: Visual reference means you can see enough of the ground to maintain attitude and position. It's the foundation of VFR flight. Losing visual reference is one of the most dangerous situations for a VFR pilot.
key_facts: Defined in AIP GEN
Ability to see sufficient ground features or landmarks
Enables pilot to maintain desired attitude and position
Loss of visual reference is a critical VFR hazard

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the following abbreviations from CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted): AD, ADF, AEDRS, AGL, AIREP (AIP GEN), AMSL, ATIS, AWS (AIP GEN), BWR (AIP GEN), CAR, CGL (AIP GEN), DME, ELT, FATO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN), GNSS, ME1, ME2, MEL, NVG, NVIS, OGE (Helicopter candidates only), PLA, QFE, QNH, TALO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN), TLOF (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN), VOR
concept: Rev 38 has significantly reduced and restructured the abbreviations list from earlier revisions. The new list contains 27 abbreviations (down from 56+ in previous versions). Many abbreviations that were previously listed here (like AIP, AIS, ATC, ATZ, AWIB, BKN, CTA, CTR, FEW, FIR, FL, ft, GAA, hPa, IFR, ILS, IMC, km, kt, LAT, LONG, MCTOW, MBZ, METAR, MOA, MSA, MTOW, NDB, NM, NOTAM, OCA/OCH, OVC, RVR, SARTIME, SCT, SKC, SPECI, SFC, TAF, TMA, UNICOM, UTC, VFR, VHZ, VMC, VNC) have been removed. New abbreviations added include AEDRS, AGL, AIREP, AMSL, AWS, BWR, CGL, FATO, GNSS, ME1, ME2, MEL, NVG, NVIS, OGE, PLA, TALO, and TLOF. Some abbreviations now reference AIP GEN as their source rather than CAR Part 1. The helicopter-specific abbreviations (FATO, OGE, TALO, TLOF) only apply to helicopter candidates.
exam_tip: The abbreviations list has been dramatically cut in Rev 38. Focus on the new additions (AEDRS, GNSS, ME1/ME2, MEL, NVG/NVIS, PLA) and note which ones are helicopter-only and which reference AIP GEN.
key_facts: 27 abbreviations in Rev 38 (down from 56+ in previous revisions)
Several abbreviations now reference AIP GEN as their source
Helicopter-specific abbreviations: FATO, OGE, TALO, TLOF
New additions include AEDRS, GNSS, ME1, ME2, MEL, NVG, NVIS, PLA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AD. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: AD means airworthiness directive.
exam_tip: AD = airworthiness directive. Mandatory compliance -- not optional. Know the difference between AD and SB (service bulletin, which is usually optional).
key_facts: AD means airworthiness directive
Mandatory compliance required
Different from service bulletins which are typically optional
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ADF. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: ADF means automatic direction-finder.
exam_tip: ADF = automatic direction-finder. The airborne receiver that works with NDB ground stations. Gives you a relative bearing to the station.
key_facts: ADF means automatic direction-finder
Airborne receiver for NDB signals
Provides relative bearing to the ground station
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AEDRS. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: AEDRS means automatic engine data recording system.
exam_tip: AEDRS = automatic engine data recording system. New addition in Rev 38. Records engine parameters from specialised sensors.
key_facts: AEDRS means automatic engine data recording system
Records engine performance parameters
New addition in Rev 38 abbreviations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
