syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AGL. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: AGL means above ground level.
exam_tip: AGL = above ground level. Used for heights, minimum altitudes, and circuit heights. Don't confuse with AMSL.
key_facts: AGL means above ground level
Used for heights and circuit altitudes
Different from AMSL (above mean sea level)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AIREP (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: AIREP (AIP GEN) means air report.
exam_tip: AIREP = air report. A report from an aircraft in flight providing position, weather, or other operational information.
key_facts: AIREP means air report
Report from aircraft in flight
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AMSL. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: AMSL means above mean sea level.
exam_tip: AMSL = above mean sea level. This is what altitudes are referenced to. Your altimeter reads AMSL when set to QNH.
key_facts: AMSL means above mean sea level
Reference datum for altitudes
Altimeter reads AMSL when set to QNH
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ATIS. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: ATIS means automatic terminal information service.
exam_tip: ATIS = automatic terminal information service. Continuous broadcast of routine aerodrome information at controlled aerodromes. Each update gets a new letter identifier (Alpha, Bravo, etc.).
key_facts: ATIS means automatic terminal information service
Continuous broadcast at controlled aerodromes
Each update identified by letter (Alpha, Bravo, etc.)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AWS (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: AWS (AIP GEN) means aerodrome weather station.
exam_tip: AWS = aerodrome weather station. Defined in AIP GEN. Provides automated weather observations at aerodromes.
key_facts: AWS means aerodrome weather station
Provides automated weather observations
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: BWR (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: BWR (AIP GEN) means basic weather report.
exam_tip: BWR = basic weather report. A verbal report of observed weather conditions. Defined in AIP GEN.
key_facts: BWR means basic weather report
Verbal report of current observed conditions
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: CAR. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: CAR means Civil Aviation Rules.
exam_tip: CAR = Civil Aviation Rules. The rules made under the Civil Aviation Act 2023. Covers Parts 1 through 175.
key_facts: CAR means Civil Aviation Rules
Made under the Civil Aviation Act 2023
Parts 1 through 175
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: CGL (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: CGL (AIP GEN) means circling guidance lighting.
exam_tip: CGL = circling guidance lighting. Defined in AIP GEN. Lighting system to guide aircraft during circling approaches.
key_facts: CGL means circling guidance lighting
Guides aircraft during circling approaches
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: DME. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: DME means distance measuring equipment.
exam_tip: DME = distance measuring equipment. Provides slant range distance to a ground station in nautical miles. Remember it's slant range, not ground distance.
key_facts: DME means distance measuring equipment
Provides slant range distance in nautical miles
Slant range -- not ground distance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ELT. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: ELT means emergency locator transmitter.
exam_tip: ELT = emergency locator transmitter. Broadcasts on 406 MHz (and 121.5 MHz) to assist search and rescue after an accident.
key_facts: ELT means emergency locator transmitter
Broadcasts on 406 MHz and 121.5 MHz
Facilitates search and rescue operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FATO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: FATO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN) means final approach and take-off area.
exam_tip: FATO = final approach and take-off area. Helicopter-specific. Defined in AIP GEN. The area over which the final phase of approach to hover or landing is completed.
key_facts: FATO means final approach and take-off area
Helicopter candidates only
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GNSS. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: GNSS means global navigation satellite system.
exam_tip: GNSS = global navigation satellite system. The umbrella term covering GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and other satellite navigation systems.
key_facts: GNSS means global navigation satellite system
Umbrella term for GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, etc.
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ME1. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: ME1 means Medical Examiner 1 Certificate holder.
exam_tip: ME1 = a medical examiner who holds a Medical Examiner 1 Certificate issued under the Act. ME1 can conduct Class 1 medical examinations for professional pilots.
key_facts: ME1 means Medical Examiner 1 Certificate holder
Can conduct Class 1 medical examinations
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ME2. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: ME2 means Medical Examiner 2 Certificate holder.
exam_tip: ME2 = a medical examiner who holds a Medical Examiner 2 Certificate issued under the Act. ME2 can conduct Class 2 medical examinations.
key_facts: ME2 means Medical Examiner 2 Certificate holder
Can conduct Class 2 medical examinations
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: MEL. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: MEL means minimum equipment list.
exam_tip: MEL = minimum equipment list. Specifies which equipment can be inoperative for dispatch while maintaining airworthiness. Approved by the Director for each aircraft type.
key_facts: MEL means minimum equipment list
Lists equipment that can be inoperative for dispatch
Must be approved by the Director
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: NVG. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: NVG means night vision goggles.
exam_tip: NVG = night vision goggles. Head-mounted binoculars that amplify ambient light for night operations.
key_facts: NVG means night vision goggles
Head-mounted light-amplifying binoculars
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: NVIS. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: NVIS means night vision imaging system.
exam_tip: NVIS = night vision imaging system. The complete integrated system for NVG operations -- not just the goggles but procedures, training, aircraft mods, and airworthiness requirements.
key_facts: NVIS means night vision imaging system
Complete integrated system for NVG operations
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: OGE (Helicopter candidates only). CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: OGE (Helicopter candidates only) means out of ground effect.
exam_tip: OGE = out of ground effect. Helicopter hovering above the height where ground effect provides additional lift. Requires more power than IGE hover.
key_facts: OGE means out of ground effect
Helicopter hovering above ground effect height
Requires more power than in ground effect (IGE)
Helicopter candidates only
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: PLA. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: PLA means parachute landing area.
exam_tip: PLA = parachute landing area. An area designated for parachute landings. Marked on charts and subject to specific operating restrictions.
key_facts: PLA means parachute landing area
Designated area for parachute landings
New addition in Rev 38
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: QFE. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: QFE means atmospheric pressure at aerodrome level or runway threshold.
exam_tip: QFE = atmospheric pressure at aerodrome level or at runway threshold. Setting QFE on your altimeter gives you height above the aerodrome, reading zero on the ground.
key_facts: QFE means atmospheric pressure at aerodrome level or runway threshold
Altimeter reads zero on the ground when set to QFE
Gives height above aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: QNH. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: QNH means altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain elevation when on the ground.
exam_tip: QNH = an altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain elevation when on the ground. Setting QNH makes your altimeter read aerodrome elevation on the ground and altitude (AMSL) in flight.
key_facts: QNH means altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain elevation on the ground
Altimeter reads aerodrome elevation on the ground
Reads altitude AMSL in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TALO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: TALO (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN) means touch-down and lift-off area.
exam_tip: TALO = touch-down and lift-off area. Helicopter-specific. Defined in AIP GEN.
key_facts: TALO means touch-down and lift-off area
Helicopter candidates only
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TLOF (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN). AIP GEN
concept: TLOF (Helicopter candidates only) (AIP GEN) means touch-down and lift-off area.
exam_tip: TLOF = touch-down and lift-off area. Helicopter-specific. Defined in AIP GEN. The load-bearing area on which a helicopter may touch down or lift off.
key_facts: TLOF means touch-down and lift-off area
Load-bearing area for helicopter touch-down/lift-off
Helicopter candidates only
Defined in AIP GEN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: VOR. CAR Part 1, rule 1.3
concept: VOR means VHF omnidirectional range.
exam_tip: VOR = VHF omnidirectional range. Ground-based radio navigation aid providing bearing information to aircraft. Operates in the VHF band.
key_facts: VOR means VHF omnidirectional range
Ground-based navigation aid
Provides bearing information in VHF band
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding a pilot licence
concept: To hold a pilot licence in New Zealand, you'll need to meet specific age, medical, and knowledge requirements set out in CAR Part 61. You must be at least 17 years old for a commercial pilot licence and hold a valid medical certificate appropriate to your licence category. You'll also need to demonstrate competency through written examinations and practical flight tests, with your training logbook showing evidence of the required flying hours and experience. Your licence will be issued by CAANZ once you've satisfied all these conditions.
exam_tip: Remember the minimum age requirement and that a valid medical certificate is non-negotiable for licence issue--you can't operate without it.
key_facts: Minimum age 17 years for CPL
Valid medical certificate required
Written examination pass required
Practical flight test (checkflight) required

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for a PIC to hold a type rating on the type of aircraft being flown
concept: When you're acting as pilot-in-command of an aircraft in New Zealand, you must hold a valid type rating for that specific aircraft type if one's required. The type rating demonstrates you've received the necessary training and passed the competency checks for that particular aeroplane's systems, handling characteristics, and operating procedures. You'll need to maintain currency on the type through regular operations or approved training, and your rating will be endorsed in your logbook. The requirement applies regardless of whether you're operating under Part 119 or 135 air operations.
exam_tip: A type rating is mandatory for PIC if the aircraft type requires one--you can't legally operate as PIC without it, even if you're experienced on similar aircraft.
key_facts: Type rating required for PIC on specified aircraft types
Rating endorsement recorded in logbook
Currency requirements must be maintained
Applies to all air operations

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for entering flight details into a pilot logbook
concept: You're required to maintain an accurate logbook of all your flight experience, recording details in a way that CAANZ can verify your experience claims. Each flight entry should include the date, aircraft type and registration, departure and arrival aerodromes, flight time, and the capacity in which you operated (PIC, co-pilot, dual instruction). Your logbook entries must be contemporaneous--written up at the time or shortly after the flight--and they'll be reviewed during licence applications, renewals, and checkflights. Keep your logbook neat and legible, as illegible or inconsistent entries can raise compliance questions.
exam_tip: Logbook entries must be contemporaneous and verifiable. CAANZ will scrutinise them during any licence application, so accuracy is critical.
key_facts: Must record date, aircraft type, registration, aerodromes
Flight time and operational capacity required
Entries must be contemporaneous
Logbook subject to CAANZ verification

syllabus_desc: Describe the allowance for a person who does not hold a current pilot licence to fly dual with a flying instructor
concept: If you don't currently hold a valid pilot licence, you're still able to receive dual instruction from an approved flying instructor. This is how you'll typically start your aviation training--flying with an instructor who can teach you the fundamentals and assess your progress. Your instructor will need to be properly qualified and hold the appropriate rating for the aircraft you're training in. The instruction must be part of an approved training programme, and you can't log this time as PIC experience, only as dual instruction. This arrangement allows you to build experience towards your first licence without yet meeting all the full licence requirements.
exam_tip: Dual instruction is permitted for unlicenced persons--this is how training starts. The key is that the instructor must be approved and the training programme must be recognised.
key_facts: Dual instruction allowed without current licence
Instructor must be approved/qualified
Must be part of approved training programme
Logged as dual instruction, not PIC time

syllabus_desc: State the solo flight requirements on a person who does not hold a current pilot licence
concept: Before you can go solo without an instructor on board, you'll need to meet specific requirements even if you don't yet hold a pilot licence. You must demonstrate to an approved instructor that you've developed sufficient skill and knowledge to operate the aircraft safely, and that instructor must then endorse your logbook authorising you to fly solo. You'll typically need to have completed a minimum number of dual instruction flights and passed written examinations relevant to solo flight. Your solo flights will count towards your licence requirements, but you remain subject to airspace restrictions and can't carry passengers. The instructor's endorsement is essential--you can't self-authorise solo flight.
exam_tip: Solo flight requires instructor endorsement in your logbook. Without that endorsement, you're not legally permitted to fly alone, regardless of your experience.
key_facts: Solo flight allowed only under specific conditions in CAR Part 61
Student must hold a current medical certificate
Must have received appropriate instruction for the flight
Solo flight limitations vary by licence type and conditions

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on a person who does not hold a current pilot licence
concept: As someone training towards a pilot licence, you're operating under specific limitations that exist to ensure safety while you build experience. You cannot act as pilot-in-command of an aircraft in normal circumstances--your flights must be either dual instruction with an approved instructor or solo flights made under an instructor's endorsement. You can't carry passengers for any purpose, whether commercial or private. Your flights are restricted to visual flight rules unless you're building towards an instrument rating under structured training. You're also limited to daytime flight in most cases, and certain airspace classes and aerodrome operations may be restricted until you've gained more experience. These limitations progressively ease once you hold your licence.
exam_tip: Remember the key limitations: no PIC operations without a licence, no passengers ever, and restricted to VFR unless training for IR. These are safety fundamentals.
key_facts: Cannot act as PIC except on endorsed solo flights
No passenger operations permitted
Restricted to VFR unless instrument training
Daytime flight restrictions in early training

syllabus_desc: State the eligibility requirements for the issue of a commercial pilot licence
concept: To qualify for your commercial pilot licence in New Zealand, you'll need to meet a clear set of eligibility criteria established by CAANZ. You must be at least 18 years old, hold a current Class 1 or Class 2 medical certificate, and have completed a minimum of 200 hours of pilot-in-command or co-pilot flying time (including specific minimums in certain areas like cross-country and night flying). You'll also need to have passed all relevant written examinations and demonstrated competency through a practical checkflight with an approved examiner. Your training must be conducted through an approved training organisation, and you'll need to demonstrate knowledge of air law, meteorology, navigation, and aircraft systems relevant to commercial operations. Once you've satisfied these requirements, CAANZ will issue your CPL.
exam_tip: The 200-hour minimum is a threshold--don't confuse this with other hour requirements for specific manoeuvres. Also, remember you need a Class 1 medical for CPL, not just any medical certificate.
key_facts: Minimum age 18 years
Class 1 medical certificate required
Minimum 200 pilot hours required
Written exams and checkflight required

syllabus_desc: State the privileges of holding a commercial pilot licence
concept: Your CPL opens up a range of privileges that you didn't have as a private pilot. You're now legally permitted to act as pilot-in-command of an aircraft for remuneration--that's the key difference. You can conduct operations under Part 119 (air transport operations) and Part 135 (air operations for hire or reward), provided you meet the specific requirements for those air operations. You can carry passengers, operate across a broader range of airspace, and your currency and recency requirements are managed differently than for private operations. However, your privileges are still bounded by your aircraft type ratings, your medical certificate, and the specific air operation regulations under which you're operating. You're also expected to maintain higher standards of professionalism and technical knowledge as a commercial operator.
exam_tip: The privilege to carry passengers for remuneration is the defining feature of a CPL. But remember, this is only a privilege--you still need to meet currency, medical, and type-rating requirements to exercise it.
key_facts: Can act as PIC for remuneration
Authorised for Part 119 and 135 operations
Can carry passengers legally
Higher standards of currency and professionalism required

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on the holder of a commercial pilot licence
concept: Even though you hold a CPL, your operations are still subject to important limitations. You can only act as PIC on aircraft types for which you hold a current type rating--you can't just fly any aircraft because you're commercially licensed. Your operations must stay within the limits of your medical certificate; if your medical lapses or is suspended, you lose the privilege to operate for remuneration immediately. Your night flying privileges depend on maintaining currency in night operations, and if you haven't flown night hours within the required recency period, you're limited to daytime operations. You're also bound by the air operation regulations under which you're operating (Part 119 or 135 have different requirements), and you must comply with the specific limitations imposed by your type ratings. Additionally, your licence doesn't permit unlimited training or testing of others unless you hold a flight instructor rating.
exam_tip: Common limitations to remember: type-rating restrictions, medical certificate currency, night-flying recency, and the fact that holding a CPL doesn't automatically make you a flight instructor. Each requires separate endorsement or currency.
key_facts: Type rating required for each aircraft operated
Valid medical certificate essential for remuneration
Night-flying currency required for night operations
Subject to air operation regulations limits

syllabus_desc: State the recent experience requirements of a PIC, by day and by night, who is the holder of a commercial pilot licence
concept: To remain current and legally able to operate as PIC under your commercial licence, you'll need to maintain regular flying experience. CAR Part 61 requires that within the 12 months preceding any flight as PIC, you must have completed at least three takeoffs and landings in the type of aircraft you're planning to operate--these can be done in day or night conditions. For daytime operations, these landings just need to be done during daylight hours. If you're planning to operate at night, you'll need to have completed at least three takeoffs and landings at night in the same aircraft type within the preceding 12 months. Night landings are defined as landing between sunset and sunrise. If you haven't maintained this recency, you'll need to complete the required number of takeoffs and landings with a qualified pilot or flight instructor before you can act as PIC again.
exam_tip: The three-landing rule is straightforward, but don't confuse day and night recency--you need separate currency for night operations. And remember, this is a 12-month rolling requirement.
key_facts: Minimum 3 takeoffs and landings in preceding 12 months
Aircraft-type specific requirement
Night operations require 3 night landings separately
Night defined as sunset to sunrise

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the completion of a biennial flight review
concept: As a commercial pilot, you're required to complete a biennial flight review--essentially a comprehensive check of your flying skills and knowledge--at least once every 24 calendar months. This isn't a test you can pass or fail in the traditional sense; rather, it's a structured evaluation with a qualified instructor or examiner who assesses your handling of the aircraft, your decision-making, and your knowledge of relevant air law and procedures. The review must cover normal, abnormal, and emergency procedures appropriate to the aircraft you're operating. Once you've successfully completed a biennial flight review, your instructor or examiner will endorse your logbook, and that endorsement resets your 24-month clock. If you allow your biennial review to lapse, you can't legally act as PIC until you've completed one. Some operations, particularly under Part 135, may have additional currency requirements beyond the basic biennial review.
exam_tip: The biennial flight review is a currency requirement, not an examination. Missing it means you can't legally operate as PIC. Keep track of your last review date to avoid inadvertently becoming non-current.
key_facts: Required every 24 calendar months
Covers normal, abnormal, and emergency procedures
Logbook endorsement required to reset currency
Lapse prevents PIC operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the use of a lower licence or rating
concept: Sometimes you might need to operate under a lower level of authority than your CPL provides, and CAR Part 61 allows you to voluntarily revert to the privileges of a lower licence like a private pilot licence. This might happen if your commercial medical certificate lapses but you still hold a Class 2 medical suitable for private operations, or if you need to operate an aircraft for which you don't hold a type rating but you could operate it under private licence privileges. When you choose to operate under a lower licence, you accept all the limitations of that lower licence--so you'd be limited to non-remunerated flights, you couldn't carry passengers for hire, and your currency requirements would change. The use of a lower licence or rating is a safety mechanism that lets you continue flying legally even if some aspect of your higher authority lapses, provided you still meet the requirements of the lower licence. It's your responsibility to clearly declare which licence or rating you're operating under for any particular flight.
exam_tip: Operating under a lower licence is a deliberate choice--you can't accidentally revert. You need to be clear about which licence authority you're exercising for each flight, and you must meet all requirements of that lower authority.
key_facts: Voluntary reversion to lower licence authority
All limitations of lower licence apply
Requires meeting lower licence medical/currency requirements
Must be explicitly declared for each operation

syllabus_desc: State the period within which a PIC of an aircraft engaged on an air operation under CAR Part 135 must have passed a check of route and aerodrome proficiency
concept: When you're operating as PIC on a Part 135 air operation (air operations for hire or reward), you're subject to enhanced currency requirements beyond the basic biennial review. You must have passed a check of route and aerodrome proficiency within the preceding 12 months. This check ensures you're familiar with the specific routes you're operating and the aerodromes you use--their layouts, procedures, terrain, and any special considerations. The check is typically conducted by an approved check and training organisation and will be recorded in your logbook. This requirement exists because commercial operations require a higher standard of proficiency than private flying, and you need to demonstrate that you're current and competent on the specific aerodromes and routes that define your operation. If you don't maintain this currency, you can't legally act as PIC on Part 135 operations until the check is re-accomplished.
exam_tip: The 12-month requirement for route and aerodrome proficiency checks is separate from your biennial flight review. Part 135 operators need to track both requirements carefully.
key_facts: Required within 12 months for Part 135 operations
Assesses route and aerodrome familiarity
Completed by approved check organisation
Logbook endorsement required

syllabus_desc: State the period within which a pilot, acting as a flight crew member of an aircraft engaged on a CAR Part 135 air operation under VFR, must have passed a check of normal, abnormal and emergency procedures in the same aircraft type
concept: If you're flying as a crew member (not necessarily as PIC) on a Part 135 VFR operation, you need to maintain currency on normal, abnormal, and emergency procedures specific to that aircraft type. This check must be passed within the preceding 12 months and ensures you can handle the aircraft competently across a range of scenarios. The check assesses your understanding of how to operate the aircraft normally, respond when things go wrong (abnormal procedures), and manage genuine emergency situations. It's type-specific, meaning you'll need separate checks for different aircraft you operate on. This requirement applies whether you're acting as PIC or as a co-pilot, because Part 135 operations demand high proficiency from all flight crew. The check is typically conducted during initial type-training and then repeated annually to maintain currency.
exam_tip: This is a 12-month requirement for Part 135 VFR crew members--it applies to all crew, not just the PIC. Don't confuse it with the PIC-specific route and aerodrome checks; they're separate.
key_facts: Required within 12 months for Part 135 VFR crew
Type-specific and covers normal, abnormal, emergency
Applies to all crew members, not just PIC
Separate from PIC route and aerodrome checks

syllabus_desc: State the period within which a pilot of an aircraft engaged on an air operation under CAR Part 135 must have completed a written or oral test of their knowledge in aircraft operating procedures, including emergency procedures and ATC procedures in relation to the areas and routes over which they are to operate
concept: For pilots engaged on Part 135 air operations, you're required to maintain current knowledge of aircraft operating procedures, emergency procedures, and the specific air traffic control procedures you'll encounter on your designated routes and in your operational areas. This knowledge check must be completed within the preceding 12 months and can be either written or oral, depending on what your operator's training programme requires. It's designed to ensure you understand not just how to fly the aircraft, but how to operate it within the specific airspace and ATC environment where your Part 135 operation takes place. Knowledge of local ATC procedures, terrain considerations, and emergency protocols for your area of operation is essential for safe commercial flying. The test results should be recorded, typically in your training file or logbook endorsement, so that your operator can verify you're current.
exam_tip: The 12-month knowledge test can be written or oral, but it's mandatory for Part 135 pilots. The focus on route and area-specific knowledge reflects the need to understand your actual operational environment, not just generic procedures.
key_facts: Required within 12 months for Part 135 pilots
Written or oral test format acceptable
Must cover aircraft operating and emergency procedures
Area and route-specific ATC knowledge required

syllabus_desc: State the period within which a pilot acting as PIC of an aircraft must have completed an instrument rating competency check or approved equivalent check
concept: When you're acting as PIC of an aircraft and you're relying on your instrument rating to conduct operations, you need to maintain currency on your instrument flying skills. You must complete an instrument rating competency check (or an approved equivalent check) within the preceding 12 months. This check ensures you can safely operate the aircraft solely by reference to instruments--a skill that's essential for IFR operations and for maintaining safe flight when VFR conditions deteriorate unexpectedly. The check covers your ability to interpret instruments, maintain attitude awareness, execute standard procedures, and respond appropriately to instrument failures or unusual situations. If you don't maintain this currency, you can't legally act as PIC for operations that rely on your instrument rating. Many operators use the competency check as part of their regular training programme, integrating it with recurrent training.
exam_tip: The 12-month instrument rating competency check is mandatory if you're using your IR for operations. Letting it lapse means you can't legally conduct IFR operations, even if your IR is still valid.
key_facts: Required within 12 months for IR operations
Tests instrument flying competency and procedures
Approved equivalent checks acceptable
Lapse prevents IFR-reliant operations

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding a medical certificate. CAR 61
concept: To exercise the privileges of any pilot licence in New Zealand, you must hold a valid medical certificate appropriate to your licence category. For a CPL holder, this means a Class 1 medical certificate issued under CAR Part 67. The requirements for holding a medical certificate are set out in CAR Part 61--you can't legally exercise licence privileges without one. The certificate must be current and appropriate to the operations you're conducting.
exam_tip: The question is about the general requirement to hold a medical certificate under CAR 61, not the specific class required. Know that holding a medical certificate is a fundamental licence requirement.
key_facts: A valid medical certificate is required to exercise licence privileges
Requirements set out in CAR Part 61
Certificate must be current and appropriate to licence category
CPL requires Class 1 medical certificate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the requirements on a person applying for a medical certificate. CAR 67
concept: When applying for a medical certificate under CAR Part 67, you need to meet specific requirements. You must apply through an approved aviation medical examiner (AME), provide accurate and complete medical history, and undergo the required examinations. The applicant has obligations to be truthful and not withhold information. CAR Part 67 sets out what the medical examination must cover and what standards you need to meet. You can't self-certify or use a standard GP medical--it must be conducted by an approved AME using the CAR Part 67 standards.
exam_tip: Focus on the applicant's obligations: apply through approved AME, provide truthful medical history, undergo required examinations. This is about the application process under CAR 67.
key_facts: Must apply through an approved aviation medical examiner (AME)
Applicant must provide complete and truthful medical history
Examination must meet CAR Part 67 standards
Self-certification or standard GP medical is not acceptable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 67

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for maintaining medical fitness following the issue of a medical certificate. CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 8
concept: Once you've got your medical certificate, the obligation doesn't stop. Under the CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 8, you must maintain your medical fitness and notify the Director of any changes. If you develop a new condition, start new medication, or experience symptoms that could affect your fitness to fly, you've got to report it. You can't just wait until your next medical exam--the requirement is ongoing. Maintaining medical fitness means actively monitoring your health and being honest about changes. This responsibility is on you, not your doctor or the CAA.
exam_tip: This is specifically about maintaining fitness AFTER issue, referenced to CA Act 2023 Schedule 2 clause 8. The ongoing notification obligation is the key point.
key_facts: Ongoing obligation to maintain medical fitness after certificate issue
Must notify Director of any changes affecting fitness
Requirement is in CA Act 2023, Schedule 2, clause 8
Cannot wait until next medical exam to report changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CA Act 2023 Schedule 2, clause 8

syllabus_desc: State the normal currency period of the Class 1 medical certificate for a CPL holder who is under the age of 40. CAR 67
concept: If you're a CPL holder under the age of 40, your Class 1 medical certificate has a normal currency period of 60 calendar months (5 years). This is set out in CAR Part 67. The longer validity period for younger pilots reflects the generally lower risk of age-related medical conditions. However, the currency period starts from the date of issue, so you need to track when your certificate was issued and plan ahead for renewal. Once you reach 40, the currency period shortens significantly.
exam_tip: 60 months for under-40 CPL holders. Remember this changes to 12 months once you turn 40.
key_facts: Class 1 medical valid for 60 calendar months if under 40
Currency period set by CAR Part 67
Period starts from date of issue
Shorter period applies once you reach age 40
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 67

syllabus_desc: State the normal currency period of the Class 1 medical certificate for a CPL holder who is 40 years of age or more on the date that the certificate is issued. CAR 67
concept: If you're a CPL holder aged 40 or over at the time your Class 1 medical certificate is issued, the normal currency period is 12 calendar months (1 year). This is set out in CAR Part 67. The shorter validity period reflects the increased risk of medical conditions that can develop with age--cardiovascular issues, vision changes, and other conditions become more likely. Annual medicals ensure these are caught early. You need to plan your renewal well before your current certificate expires to avoid a gap in your ability to exercise CPL privileges.
exam_tip: 12 months for CPL holders 40 and over. Plan your renewal early to avoid any gap.
key_facts: Class 1 medical valid for 12 calendar months if 40 or over
Age is assessed on the date the certificate is issued
Currency period set by CAR Part 67
Plan renewal before expiry to maintain CPL privileges
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 67

syllabus_desc: State the documentation that must be carried in an aircraft operating in NZ. CAR Part 91
concept: You'll need to keep certain documents onboard whenever you fly. The registration certificate, airworthiness certificate, maintenance release, and pilot operating handbook are your essentials. Think of these as your aeroplane's identity papers--they prove the aircraft is legal and safe to fly. Without them, you're grounded, mate. Each one serves a specific purpose in demonstrating compliance with NZ CARs.
exam_tip: Remember that some docs must be carried (onboard), while others just need to be available for inspection. Know the difference.
key_facts: Registration certificate must be carried at all times
Airworthiness certificate must be onboard
Maintenance release must be carried
Pilot operating handbook or flight manual required

syllabus_desc: State the documentation that must be available in an aircraft operating in NZ. CAR Part 91
concept: Beyond the docs you carry, you'll need to have others available for inspection--things like the aircraft maintenance log, weight and balance documentation, and any specific operational approvals. These stay with the aircraft and build up over time as a complete airworthiness history. You don't need them in the cockpit, but CAA inspectors will want to see them at the hangar or maintenance facility.
exam_tip: Distinguish between 'carried' (with you in flight) and 'available' (at the airfield or maintenance facility).
key_facts: Maintenance logs must be available for inspection
Weight and balance documentation required
Operational approval documents if applicable
Records of defects and repairs must be traceable

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for a maintenance release for the operation of an aircraft. CAR Part 91 & Part 43
concept: A maintenance release (sometimes called a 'Certificate of Release to Service') is your proof that maintenance work is complete and the aeroplane is fit to fly. It's issued by a maintenance engineer after they've done their work, and it's a legal document. You can't legally operate without one after any maintenance event. It's the engineer's certification that everything's been done properly according to the maintenance manual.
exam_tip: The maintenance release must identify what work was done and that the aircraft is airworthy after that work.
key_facts: Must be issued by authorised maintenance engineer
Must identify maintenance performed
Must state aircraft is airworthy and safe to fly
Required before any post-maintenance flight

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for a flight manual or pilot operating handbook. CAR Part 91
concept: You need a current flight manual or POH that's specific to your aircraft type and serial number. It tells you everything about how to operate that particular aeroplane--performance limits, emergency procedures, normal operating procedures, systems descriptions. You've got to know it backwards, and the CAA expects it to be onboard and accessible. It's written by the manufacturer and tailored to your exact airframe.
exam_tip: It must be for the specific aircraft type and kept current with any amendments issued by the manufacturer.
key_facts: Must be aircraft-type specific
Amendments must be incorporated
Contains emergency procedures
Details aircraft systems and limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the maintenance requirements of an aircraft operator. CAR 91
concept: As a pilot-owner or operator, you're responsible for keeping your aeroplane in airworthy condition--full stop. You can't just hope it stays serviceable; you've got to have a structured maintenance programme in place. Whether you fly it once a year or weekly, you must follow the manufacturer's maintenance schedule and keep comprehensive records. It's your legal responsibility under NZ CARs Part 91 to ensure every flight starts with an airworthy aircraft.
exam_tip: The operator has primary responsibility; you can delegate the work but not the accountability.
key_facts: Operator is legally responsible for airworthiness
Must follow manufacturer's maintenance schedule
Maintenance records must be complete
Maintenance programme must be appropriate to operation

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for annual and 100-hour inspections. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires aircraft to undergo annual inspections and, for aircraft used in air operations, 100-hour inspections. The annual inspection ensures your aircraft continues to meet airworthiness standards over time. The 100-hour inspection is an additional requirement for aircraft operating commercially--it's a more frequent check that catches issues before they become safety concerns. Both inspections must be performed by authorised maintenance personnel and documented properly.
exam_tip: Know the difference: annual inspections apply to all aircraft, 100-hour inspections are additional for air operation aircraft. Both are required under CAR 91.
key_facts: Annual inspections required for all aircraft
100-hour inspections required for air operation aircraft
Both must be performed by authorised personnel
Inspections must be documented in maintenance records

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for a review of airworthiness. CAR 91
concept: A review of airworthiness is a comprehensive assessment required under CAR 91 to confirm that an aircraft continues to meet its design standards and regulatory requirements. This is distinct from routine maintenance--it's a formal evaluation of the aircraft's overall airworthy condition. The review examines maintenance records, inspects the aircraft, and confirms compliance with all applicable airworthiness directives and service bulletins.
exam_tip: Defect reporting must be specific, dated, and cross-referenced to repairs in the maintenance log.
key_facts: All defects must be logged in maintenance records
Description must be detailed and specific
Defect and rectification must be linked
Records must be retained and accessible

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for maintenance records. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires aircraft operators to maintain comprehensive maintenance records. These records document all maintenance performed, including inspections, repairs, modifications, and component replacements. The records must be accurate, legible, and kept up to date. They form the aircraft's maintenance history and are essential for tracking compliance with maintenance schedules, airworthiness directives, and component life limits.
exam_tip: Maintenance records must be comprehensive, accurate, and current. They're a legal requirement under CAR 91.
key_facts: Annual inspections required
Engine overhauls on manufacturer schedule
Component replacement when limits reached
Programme must match aircraft type and usage

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the retention of maintenance records. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies how long maintenance records must be retained. Records must be kept for the life of the aircraft or component, depending on the type of record. This ensures a complete maintenance history is available for airworthiness reviews, accident investigations, and ownership transfers. You can't destroy records just because the work was done years ago--the retention requirements are strict and legally enforceable.
exam_tip: Know the retention periods for different types of maintenance records. Records must be kept for the life of the aircraft or component.
key_facts: Maintenance records must be retained per CAR 91 requirements
Retention periods vary by record type
Records must be available for airworthiness reviews
Destruction of records before required retention period is an offence

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for and contents of a technical log. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires operators to maintain a technical log for each aircraft. The technical log contains the current status of the aircraft's maintenance and airworthiness. It includes details of the last maintenance release, any outstanding defects, deferred maintenance items, and the aircraft's current flight hours and cycles. The technical log travels with the aircraft and is the primary document a pilot checks to confirm the aircraft is released for service.
exam_tip: The technical log is the aircraft's day-to-day airworthiness record. Know what it must contain and who's responsible for maintaining it.
key_facts: Technical log required for each aircraft under CAR 91
Contains current maintenance and airworthiness status
Includes maintenance release, defects, and deferred items
Travels with the aircraft and is checked by PIC before flight

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for entering defects into a technical log. CAR 91
concept: When you discover a defect during flight or pre-flight inspection, you're required to enter it into the technical log under CAR 91. The entry must be specific, dated, and describe the defect clearly enough for maintenance personnel to understand and assess it. Defects must be entered promptly--you can't leave them unreported. The technical log entry triggers the maintenance response: either the defect is rectified before the next flight, or it's assessed as acceptable for continued operation under specific conditions.
exam_tip: Defect entries must be specific, dated, and prompt. They trigger the maintenance response process.
key_facts: Pilots can perform only CAR Part 43 approved items
Includes cleaning, fluid top-ups, simple replacements
Complex work requires licensed engineer
Documentation of pilot maintenance still required

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for clearing defects from a technical log. CAR 91
concept: Once a defect has been entered in the technical log, it must be properly cleared before the aircraft can return to normal service. Clearing a defect requires either rectification of the issue by authorised maintenance personnel, or a formal assessment that the defect is acceptable for continued operation under specified conditions (a deferred defect). The clearance must be documented in the technical log with details of the work performed, the person who performed it, and their authorisation. You can't just cross out a defect entry--there's a formal process under CAR 91.
exam_tip: Clearing a defect requires either rectification or formal deferral. The clearance must be documented with details of work and authorisation.
key_facts: Defects must be formally cleared before normal service resumes
Clearance requires rectification or formal deferral assessment
Documentation must include work details and authorisation
Informal clearance (crossing out) is not acceptable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the limitations and requirements on a person undertaking 'pilot maintenance'. CAR 43
concept: CAR Part 43 allows pilots to perform a limited set of maintenance tasks known as 'pilot maintenance'. These are simple, non-critical items like cleaning, fluid top-ups, tyre pressure checks, and replacing minor components such as fuses or bulbs. The key limitation is that you can only perform tasks specifically listed in CAR Part 43--anything beyond the approved list requires a licensed aircraft maintenance engineer. You must document any pilot maintenance performed, and you're responsible for ensuring the work is done correctly. If it's not on the approved list, don't do it.
exam_tip: Pilot maintenance is limited to the CAR Part 43 approved list. If it's not on the list, you need an engineer.
key_facts: Only CAR Part 43 approved tasks can be performed by pilots
Includes simple tasks: cleaning, fluid top-ups, minor replacements
All pilot maintenance must be documented
Complex or structural work requires a licensed engineer
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 43

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for conducting an operational flight check on an aircraft. CAR 91
concept: After certain maintenance work is completed, CAR Part 91 may require an operational flight check before the aircraft returns to normal service. This flight check verifies that the maintenance work has been done correctly and that all systems are functioning as expected in actual flight conditions. The check must be conducted by an appropriately qualified pilot, and the results must be documented. Not all maintenance requires a flight check--the requirement depends on the nature of the work performed.
exam_tip: Operational flight checks are required after certain maintenance work to verify systems function correctly in flight.
key_facts: Required after certain types of maintenance work
Verifies maintenance work was done correctly
Must be conducted by qualified pilot
Results must be documented
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for acting as a test pilot. CAR 19
concept: CAR Part 19 sets out the requirements for acting as a test pilot in New Zealand. A test pilot conducts flights to evaluate aircraft performance, handling characteristics, or systems following major modifications, repairs, or during the certification process. You need specific qualifications and experience to act as a test pilot--it's not something any CPL holder can do. The requirements include appropriate training, experience on the aircraft type, and authorisation from the Director.
exam_tip: Test pilot requirements are in CAR Part 19. Specific qualifications, experience, and Director authorisation are required.
key_facts: Requirements set out in CAR Part 19
Specific qualifications and experience required
Authorisation from the Director needed
Applies to evaluation flights after modifications or during certification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 19

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for radios. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies the required inspection period for aircraft radio equipment. Radio inspections ensure your communications and navigation equipment is functioning correctly and meeting the required performance standards. The inspection must be carried out by authorised personnel and documented in the aircraft's maintenance records. Operating with overdue radio inspections means your aircraft doesn't meet the regulatory requirements for airworthiness.
exam_tip: Know the inspection period for radios under CAR 91. Operating with overdue inspections is non-compliant.
key_facts: Radio inspection period specified in CAR Part 91
Inspections ensure communications equipment meets standards
Must be performed by authorised personnel
Results documented in maintenance records
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for altimeters. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires altimeters to be inspected at specified intervals. Altimeter accuracy is critical for safe flight operations--an inaccurate altimeter can lead to altitude errors that compromise terrain clearance and traffic separation. The inspection verifies the altimeter meets the required accuracy standards across its operating range. Like all equipment inspections, this must be performed by authorised personnel and documented.
exam_tip: Know the inspection period for altimeters under CAR 91. Altimeter accuracy is critical for safe operations.
key_facts: Altimeter inspection period specified in CAR Part 91
Accuracy is critical for terrain clearance and traffic separation
Must be performed by authorised personnel
Results documented in maintenance records
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for transponders. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies the required inspection period for aircraft transponders. Transponder inspections verify that the equipment is transmitting the correct information--altitude, identification, and position data--to ATC radar systems. An inaccurate or malfunctioning transponder can create safety issues in controlled airspace and compromise traffic separation. The inspection must be carried out by authorised personnel at the specified intervals.
exam_tip: Know the inspection period for transponders under CAR 91. Transponder accuracy affects ATC radar services.
key_facts: Transponder inspection period specified in CAR Part 91
Verifies accuracy of altitude and identification transmissions
Critical for operations in controlled airspace
Must be performed by authorised personnel at specified intervals
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the normal inspection period for the ELT. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies the normal inspection period for emergency locator transmitters (ELTs). The ELT is your aircraft's emergency beacon--it activates on impact to help search and rescue locate you. Regular inspections ensure the ELT is functioning correctly, the battery has adequate charge, and the antenna and connections are serviceable. Operating with an overdue ELT inspection means you may not have a functioning emergency beacon when you need it most.
exam_tip: Know the ELT inspection period under CAR 91. The ELT is your lifeline in an emergency--it must be serviceable.
key_facts: ELT inspection period specified in CAR Part 91
Ensures beacon, battery, and antenna are serviceable
Critical for search and rescue in emergencies
Operating with overdue inspection is non-compliant
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the instrument and equipment requirements for VFR flight by day. CAR Part 91
concept: For daytime VFR, you need a solid basic set of instruments to keep you safe and legal. You'll need an airspeed indicator, an altimeter, a magnetic compass, and a source of vacuum or electrical power to run the gyro instruments if you've got them. The rules keep it straightforward for day VFR--you don't need everything, just the essentials to know your height, speed, direction, and attitude. If you're flying above 3,000 feet AGL or in controlled airspace, you might need a bit more.
exam_tip: Day VFR minimums are basic, but always check if your operation needs extra instruments.
key_facts: Airspeed indicator required
Altimeter required
Magnetic compass required
Flight and navigation instruments appropriate to operation

syllabus_desc: State the instrument and equipment requirements for VFR flight by night. CAR Part 91
concept: Night VFR ups the ante because you've lost the visual reference of the ground. You'll need everything for day flight, plus you need reliable electrical systems--a primary generator and a backup battery that can power essential instruments for at least 30 minutes. You also need flight instruments that aren't dependent on vacuum. Essentially, you're carrying a second power system as insurance. Navigation lights, cabin lighting, and landing lights are also mandatory. Night flying demands redundancy.
exam_tip: Night VFR requires electrical redundancy--generator plus battery backup for instrument power.
key_facts: All day VFR instruments required
Primary electrical power system required
Backup battery for 30 minutes minimum
Navigation, cabin, and landing lights required

syllabus_desc: State the communications and navigation equipment requirements for a VFR over-water flight. CAR 91
concept: When you're flying over water and out of gliding distance from land, the rules get tighter. You've got to have reliable communications--a VHF radio with at least two independent batteries or power sources so you can call for help if needed. Navigation equipment needs to be accurate and ideally backed up; many pilots carry a GPS plus a traditional nav system. You're also expected to have flotation equipment and survival gear because ditching is a real possibility. Over-water flying requires you to plan for emergencies seriously.
exam_tip: Redundancy is key over water. Two independent communication systems and navigation backups are standard practice.
key_facts: VHF radio with independent power required
Navigation equipment with backup recommended
Flotation equipment required
Survival gear appropriate to flight duration

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements for a night VFR flight. CAR 91
concept: Night VFR equipment goes beyond instruments. You're required to have navigation lights--red and green on the wings, white on the tail. You've got to have landing lights if you want to see the runway, and interior lighting so you can read your instruments. You also need flashlights as backup in case electrics fail. Anti-collision lights (strobes or beacons) aren't technically mandatory under Part 91, but they're sensible. Think of it as outfitting the aeroplane so others can see you and you can see what you're doing.
exam_tip: Navigation lights, landing lights, and interior lighting are mandatory for night VFR under CAR 91.
key_facts: Navigation lights (red, green, white) required
Landing lights required
Interior lighting for instruments required
Backup flashlight recommended

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements for flight over water. CAR 91 & 135
concept: Ditching over water is possible, so you've got to carry the gear to survive it. Life jackets or lifebelts for every occupant are non-negotiable. For flights more than a few minutes from land, you're looking at life rafts sized for the crew and passengers. Sea anchors, signalling equipment, first aid kits--you're building a survival kit. Under CAR Part 135, commercial operators have even stricter requirements. The further out you go, the more redundancy and survival gear you carry.
exam_tip: Life jackets are mandatory for all occupants on over-water flights.
key_facts: Life jackets for all occupants required
Life rafts for extended over-water operations
Sea anchors and signalling equipment
First aid kit required

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for indicating the time in flight. CAR 91
concept: You need a reliable clock or watch so you can track your flight time, manage your fuel endurance, and confirm your position checks. It's straightforward--a good watch onboard or a clock in the cockpit. You're also logging all your flight time, and accuracy matters for maintenance schedules and aircraft limits. It's not fancy, but it's necessary. A digital watch on your wrist works fine, but a cockpit clock keeps it visible at a glance.
exam_tip: A reliable timepiece is required; this can be as simple as a pilot's watch.
key_facts: Reliable clock or watch required onboard
Must be visible to crew in flight
Used for flight time logging
Assists in position checking and fuel management

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for emergency equipment. CAR 91 & 135
concept: Emergency equipment covers a broad range depending on your operation. You've got to carry first aid kits, fire extinguishers in the cockpit and cabin, and emergency oxygen if you're flying high. If you're doing Part 135 operations, you're carrying additional equipment like evacuation slides, emergency exits, and enhanced survival gear. Even for private flying, you should have a good first aid kit and know where your fire extinguisher is. It's about being prepared for things going sideways.
exam_tip: Emergency equipment requirements scale with your operation type and altitude.
key_facts: First aid kit required
Fire extinguisher required
Emergency oxygen if applicable
Part 135 requires enhanced emergency equipment

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for night flight. CAR 91
concept: Night flying under CAR 91 requires you to think ahead. You've got landing lights so you can see the runway, navigation lights so others see you, and interior lighting to read your instruments. You've got to have backup power--batteries as a redundancy. You're also expected to have flashlights with spare batteries for instrument backlighting if electrics fail. Many pilots carry additional gear like night-vision considerations or enhanced lighting systems. It's about redundancy and visibility in an environment where you've lost natural references.
exam_tip: Night flight equipment must support operations in zero external lighting.
key_facts: Landing lights required
Navigation lights required
Interior lighting required
Backup battery and flashlights essential

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirements for night flight.
concept: For Part 135 operators, night flight gear is more comprehensive. Beyond basic navigation and landing lights, you're required to have enhanced lighting systems, redundant power sources, and often additional safety equipment like emergency oxygen systems. You've got to meet higher standards because you're carrying paying passengers who depend on you to get them there safely at night. Your aeroplane becomes more like a mini-airline operation--everything's more thoroughly specified and documented.
exam_tip: Part 135 night requirements include enhanced lighting, redundant electrical systems, and comprehensive safety equipment.
key_facts: Enhanced navigation lighting required
Redundant electrical power systems
Emergency oxygen systems specified
Comprehensive emergency equipment

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirements for a cockpit voice recorder.
concept: Part 135 operators carrying more than a handful of passengers are required to carry a CVR--a black box that records all conversations and sounds in the cockpit. It's redundant and backed up, and it records at least the last 30 minutes of operation. If something goes wrong, investigators can review exactly what happened. It's not an invasion of your privacy; it's a safety requirement. Modern CVRs are solid-state and reliable, and they're an essential part of accident investigation.
exam_tip: CVR recording helps investigators understand crew actions and communications in the event of an incident.
key_facts: Required for Part 135 operations over certain passenger counts
Records at least 30 minutes of cockpit audio
Accident-protected recording system
Required for accident investigation

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirements for a flight data recorder.
concept: The FDR is the other black box--it records parameters like altitude, airspeed, heading, engine thrust, control surface positions, and dozens of other data points. It's continuously updated and stores at least 25 hours of flight data. Combined with the CVR, the FDR gives investigators a complete picture of what the aircraft was doing before an accident. It's your digital witness. Part 135 operators operating larger aircraft or on regular passenger routes are required to carry one. It's another safety-critical system.
exam_tip: FDR and CVR together give a complete accident investigation record; they're non-negotiable for Part 135.
key_facts: Records aircraft parameters continuously
Stores at least 25 hours of data
Required for Part 135 passenger operations
Essential for accident investigation

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for an ELT. CAR 91
concept: An Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) is your distress beacon. If you ditch or crash, the ELT automatically activates and broadcasts your location on the international emergency frequency. Search and rescue can lock onto the signal and find you quickly. You're required to carry one onboard, and it must be regularly serviced and have a current battery. It's a last resort that can save your life--literally. Modern ELTs are reliable and compact, and they've saved countless pilots and passengers over the decades.
exam_tip: ELT must be current, batteries checked regularly, and operational before every flight.
key_facts: Automatic activation on impact
Transmits on international emergency frequency 121.5 MHz
Battery must be current and serviceable
Required for all aircraft under CAR 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements of passengers to comply with instructions and commands. CAR 91
concept: Your passengers must comply with all your instructions and commands as PIC. This isn't optional--it's a legal requirement under CAR 91. You've got the authority to enforce safety procedures, and passengers need to understand that following crew instructions directly protects everyone on board. Think of it as establishing the safety culture from the moment they board.
exam_tip: Remember: passenger compliance with PIC instructions is legally mandated, not a suggestion. This applies to all passengers regardless of familiarity with the aircraft.
key_facts: Passengers must comply with all PIC instructions and commands
This is a legal requirement under CAR 91
Instructions cover safety procedures and operational requirements
PIC enforces compliance as part of safe operation responsibility

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for maintaining daily flight records. CAR 91
concept: You're required to maintain accurate daily flight records for every flight operation. These aren't just administrative--they're your legal documentation of compliance with air law requirements. Your records should capture flight details, timings, and any incidents or irregularities. Keeping proper records demonstrates your diligence as PIC and protects you if there's ever any question about your conduct.
exam_tip: Daily records must be maintained contemporaneously and accurately. They're evidence of your compliance with operational requirements.
key_facts: Daily flight records are mandatory for all operations
Records must be accurate and maintained contemporaneously
They document compliance with air law requirements
Records are legal evidence of operational diligence

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for the carriage of flight attendants. CAR 91
concept: For certain operations and aircraft types, you're required to carry trained flight attendants. The specific requirements depend on your operation type and aircraft configuration under CAR 91. Flight attendants are essential for passenger safety, emergency response, and maintaining the safety culture on your aircraft. You need to understand when they're required and their role in your overall safety management.
exam_tip: Flight attendant requirements are operation-type and aircraft-specific. Check CAR 91 for your exact classification.
key_facts: Flight attendants required for certain operations and aircraft
Requirements vary by operation type and aircraft configuration
Flight attendants provide passenger safety and emergency response
Requirements specified in CAR 91 Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for operating an aircraft in simulated instrument flight. CAR 91
concept: When you're conducting simulated instrument flight (hood work), you've got specific regulatory requirements under CAR 91. You'll need a safety pilot with appropriate qualifications, proper aircraft configuration, and you must remain outside controlled airspace. Your safety pilot's job is absolutely critical--they're your eyes on the outside world. These requirements exist because simulated instrument flight removes essential situational awareness.
exam_tip: Simulated instrument work requires a qualified safety pilot, visual confirmation of airspace, and correct aircraft setup. You cannot conduct it in controlled airspace.
key_facts: Qualified safety pilot required at all times
Must operate outside controlled airspace
Aircraft must be appropriately configured
Safety pilot maintains visual lookout and aircraft control authority

syllabus_desc: State the requirements of a PIC with respect to the safe operation of an aircraft. CAR 91
concept: As PIC, you've got the ultimate responsibility for safe aircraft operation. This isn't just a legal obligation--it's your fundamental duty. You need to ensure the aircraft is airworthy, crew members are competent, passengers are properly briefed, and you operate within limitations. You make the go/no-go decision, and you're accountable for every decision you make. This responsibility begins long before you touch the controls.
exam_tip: PIC responsibility for safe operation is absolute and non-delegable. You cannot transfer this responsibility to anyone else.
key_facts: PIC has ultimate responsibility for safe operation
Responsibility covers airworthiness, crew competency, and passenger safety
PIC makes go/no-go decisions independently
Responsibility is non-delegable and legally binding

syllabus_desc: Describe the authority of the PIC. CAR 91
concept: Your authority as PIC is extensive and necessary for safe flight operations. You can deviate from ATC instructions, procedures, and even company operations if necessary to ensure safety. You have the authority to refuse passengers, divert to alternative aerodromes, and make unilateral decisions affecting the operation. This authority isn't meant to be exercised lightly--it's there to give you the flexibility to manage unexpected situations and prioritise safety over convenience.
exam_tip: PIC authority includes the right to deviate from instructions if safety is compromised. This authority is supported by CAR 91.
key_facts: PIC can deviate from ATC instructions for safety
PIC can refuse passengers or divert aircraft
Authority extends to all operational decisions affecting safety
Authority exists to ensure safety takes precedence

topic: 16.30 -- Crew Seat Occupation and Safety Belts
syllabus_desc: State the requirements for crew occupation of seats and wearing safety belts. CAR 91
concept: Your crew members must occupy designated seats during flight operations, and they're required to wear safety belts (and shoulder harnesses where fitted) at all times. You need to enforce this requirement--it's non-negotiable under CAR 91. Everyone on the flight deck and in designated crew areas needs to be properly restrained. You set the standard by always being correctly restrained yourself.
exam_tip: All crew members must occupy designated seats and wear safety restraints continuously during flight. This applies to all phases of flight.
key_facts: Crew must occupy designated seats during flight
Safety belts (and shoulder harnesses) required at all times
Requirement applies throughout all flight phases
PIC enforces crew compliance with restraint requirements

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the occupation of seats and wearing of restraints. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies requirements for the occupation of seats and wearing of restraints by all persons on board. This extends beyond crew to include passengers. All occupants must be properly seated with restraints fastened during critical phases of flight (takeoff, landing, and whenever the PIC directs). The seat must be appropriate for the occupant, and the restraint system must be serviceable and properly adjusted.
exam_tip: This covers ALL occupants (not just crew). Know when restraints must be worn and the PIC's authority to direct restraint use.
key_facts: All occupants must occupy seats with serviceable restraints
Restraints required during takeoff, landing, and as directed by PIC
Seat must be appropriate for the occupant
PIC has authority to direct restraint use at any time

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the use of oxygen equipment. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 sets out when you must use supplemental oxygen. At cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 feet, the PIC and crew must use supplemental oxygen. For flights above 13,000 feet cabin altitude, oxygen must also be available for passengers. The specific time limits and altitude thresholds are defined in the rules. Understanding these requirements is essential for planning flights at higher altitudes, particularly in unpressurised aircraft.
exam_tip: Know the altitude thresholds for crew and passenger oxygen requirements. These are critical for high-altitude flight planning.
key_facts: Supplemental oxygen requirements specified in CAR Part 91
Crew must use oxygen above 10,000 feet cabin altitude
Passenger oxygen required above 13,000 feet cabin altitude
Requirements critical for unpressurised aircraft at altitude

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for briefing passengers prior to flight. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires that passengers are briefed before flight on safety-relevant matters. The briefing must cover the use of seat belts and restraints, emergency exits and their operation, the location and use of emergency equipment, and any specific safety procedures for the aircraft type. The briefing content and format depend on the operation type, but the requirement applies to all passenger-carrying flights. As PIC, you're responsible for ensuring the briefing is delivered.
exam_tip: Passenger briefing is mandatory for all passenger flights. Know the required content: restraints, exits, emergency equipment, and aircraft-specific procedures.
key_facts: Pre-flight safety briefing required for all passenger flights
Must cover restraints, exits, emergency equipment
Content depends on operation type and aircraft
PIC responsible for ensuring briefing is delivered

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for familiarity with operating limitations and emergency equipment. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires that the PIC and crew are familiar with the aircraft's operating limitations and emergency equipment before flight. This means you need to know the aircraft's flight manual limitations (speeds, weights, performance data), the location and operation of all emergency equipment (fire extinguishers, first aid kit, ELT, life jackets where required), and the emergency procedures for the aircraft type. This isn't about memorising everything--it's about being operationally ready to handle normal and emergency situations.
exam_tip: You must be familiar with BOTH operating limitations AND emergency equipment. This is a pre-flight requirement under CAR 91.
key_facts: PIC must be familiar with operating limitations before flight
Must know location and operation of emergency equipment
Includes fire extinguishers, first aid, ELT, life jackets
Emergency procedures for aircraft type must be known

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for carrying appropriate aeronautical publications and charts in flight. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires that you carry current and appropriate aeronautical publications and charts for your planned flight. This includes relevant AIP volumes, VNCs (Visual Navigation Charts), VPCs (Visual Planning Charts), and any other charts needed for your route. The publications must be current--using outdated charts with superseded information is a safety risk and a regulatory breach. This requirement ensures you have the information you need to navigate safely and comply with airspace requirements.
exam_tip: Publications and charts must be current and appropriate to your flight. Using outdated charts is non-compliant.
key_facts: Current aeronautical publications must be carried
Charts appropriate to route must be on board
Publications must be current versions
Requirement applies to all flights under CAR 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for operating on and in the vicinity of an aerodrome. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 sets out specific requirements for operating on and in the vicinity of an aerodrome. This includes compliance with aerodrome traffic circuit procedures, giving way to aircraft on final approach, maintaining a listening watch on the appropriate frequency, and making required radio calls. At uncontrolled aerodromes, you must comply with the published procedures and exercise good airmanship. At controlled aerodromes, ATC instructions apply. Understanding these requirements is essential for safe operations at busy and quiet aerodromes alike.
exam_tip: Know the requirements for both controlled and uncontrolled aerodromes. Circuit procedures, radio calls, and giving way rules are all testable.
key_facts: Must comply with aerodrome traffic circuit procedures
Give way to aircraft on final approach
Listening watch on appropriate frequency required
Different procedures for controlled vs uncontrolled aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Civil Aviation Act 2023 section 14, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the standard overhead joining procedure, and state when it should be used. AIP AD
concept: The standard overhead joining procedure is the default method for joining the aerodrome traffic circuit at uncontrolled aerodromes in New Zealand. It involves overflying the aerodrome at circuit altitude plus 500 feet (or as published), assessing the wind direction and circuit direction, then descending on the non-traffic side to join the circuit. This procedure gives you time to assess the traffic situation, determine the active runway, and plan your entry into the circuit safely. It should be used when arriving at an unfamiliar aerodrome, when there's no published straight-in or base-turn join, or whenever you need to assess the situation before committing to a circuit join. The procedure is described in AIP AD.
exam_tip: Know the overhead join height (circuit altitude + 500 ft), the descent on the non-traffic side, and when to use it vs other joining methods.
key_facts: Overfly at circuit altitude plus 500 feet
Descend on non-traffic side to join circuit
Default joining method at uncontrolled aerodromes
Procedure described in AIP AD

syllabus_desc: State and describe the application of the right of way rules. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 establishes right of way rules for aircraft operations. These rules determine priority when aircraft are converging, overtaking, or operating on the ground. In general: aircraft in distress have priority over all others; an aircraft on final approach or landing has right of way over aircraft on the ground or in the circuit; when two aircraft are converging at the same altitude, the one on the right has right of way; and when overtaking, the overtaking aircraft must give way. On the ground, aircraft must give way to those taking off or landing. These rules apply to all aircraft, regardless of type or operation.
exam_tip: Right of way rules follow a priority hierarchy. Know the converging rule (give way to the right), overtaking rule, and landing/takeoff priority.
key_facts: Aircraft in distress have absolute priority
Landing aircraft have right of way over those on the ground
When converging at same altitude, give way to the right
