Overtaking aircraft must give way
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for aircraft lighting. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 specifies the requirements for aircraft lighting. Navigation lights (red on port, green on starboard, white on tail) must be displayed during night operations and in reduced visibility. Anti-collision lights must be displayed whenever the aircraft's engines are running. Landing lights are required for takeoff and landing at night. The lighting requirements ensure your aircraft is visible to other traffic and help establish right of way at night. Operating without required lighting is a safety hazard and a regulatory breach.
exam_tip: Know which lights are required and when: navigation lights at night, anti-collision lights when engines running, landing lights for night takeoff/landing.
key_facts: Navigation lights required during night and reduced visibility
Anti-collision lights required whenever engines are running
Landing lights required for night takeoff and landing
Lighting requirements specified in CAR Part 91
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the pilot of an aircraft, being flown for the purpose of demonstrating eligibility for the issue of an airworthiness certificate. CAR 91
concept: CAR Part 91 sets out specific requirements for pilots conducting demonstration flights for airworthiness certification. These flights test whether an aircraft meets the standards for the issue of an airworthiness certificate. The pilot must hold appropriate qualifications, the flight must be conducted in accordance with approved procedures, and specific safety measures must be in place. These flights carry additional risk because the aircraft may not yet have full airworthiness certification.
exam_tip: Demonstration flights for airworthiness certification have specific pilot requirements and safety measures under CAR 91.
key_facts: Specific pilot qualifications required
Flight must follow approved procedures
Additional safety measures must be in place
Aircraft may not yet hold full airworthiness certification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for wearing/holding identity documentation in certain areas. CAR Part 139
concept: CAR Part 139 requires persons in certain areas of aerodromes to wear or hold identity documentation. These areas include the manoeuvring area, apron areas, and security-restricted areas. The requirement ensures that only authorised persons are present in areas where aircraft operations occur. As a pilot, you need to carry appropriate identification when operating at aerodromes with these requirements. Non-compliance can result in being denied access and potential enforcement action.
exam_tip: Identity documentation requirements apply in specific aerodrome areas under CAR Part 139. Carry appropriate ID when operating at aerodromes.
key_facts: Identity documentation required in certain aerodrome areas
Applies to manoeuvring area, apron, and security-restricted areas
Requirement under CAR Part 139
Non-compliance may result in denied access
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibility of the PIC for a pre-flight inspection
concept: You're responsible for ensuring a thorough pre-flight inspection happens before every flight. This isn't something you can delegate away entirely--you need to understand what's being checked and satisfy yourself that the aircraft is airworthy. You might conduct the inspection yourself or oversee crew performing it, but the responsibility sits with you. Your pre-flight inspection is your critical gatekeeper to ensure you're flying an airworthy aircraft.
exam_tip: PIC is responsible for pre-flight inspection. Even if crew perform it, you must satisfy yourself the aircraft is airworthy.
key_facts: PIC is responsible for pre-flight inspection
Inspection verifies aircraft airworthiness
Responsibility cannot be fully delegated
Documentation of inspection may be required

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the use of aerodromes and landing areas
concept: You can only use aerodromes and landing areas that meet the requirements for your aircraft and operation under CAR Part 91. This means checking aerodrome availability, runway conditions, facilities, and whether they're approved for your operation type. Before you plan a flight, you need to confirm your destination and any alternates are suitable. The AIP NZ gives you the information--you need to use it correctly to make safe aerodrome selections.
exam_tip: Aerodromes must be suitable for your aircraft type and operation. Check AIP NZ for aerodrome information and approval status.
key_facts: Only approved aerodromes can be used
Aerodromes must be suitable for aircraft type and operation
Information available in AIP NZ
PIC must verify aerodrome suitability before flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for operating in uncontrolled airspace
concept: Operating in uncontrolled airspace gives you more flexibility, but you've still got responsibilities under CAR Part 91. You need to maintain visual lookout, comply with VFR minimums appropriate to uncontrolled airspace, and exercise caution knowing you might encounter other traffic without ATC separation. Uncontrolled airspace doesn't mean unmonitored--you're still bound by air law and responsible for avoiding other aircraft.
exam_tip: Uncontrolled airspace operations require heightened vigilance. VFR minimums apply, and you must maintain continuous lookout.
key_facts: VFR minimums apply in uncontrolled airspace
No ATC separation services provided
Visual lookout is mandatory
PIC remains responsible for traffic awareness and avoidance

syllabus_desc: Describe the right-of-way rules for aircraft operating on the ground and in the air
concept: Right-of-way rules under CAR Part 91 establish priority when aircraft meet. In the air, rules consider aircraft type, direction of flight, and configuration. On the ground, rules cover taxiing and runway use. You need to know these rules thoroughly because they're fundamental to collision avoidance. The principle is simple: the aircraft with the right-of-way has priority, and everyone else gives way. You need to recognise situations where you have the right-of-way and situations where you must give way.
exam_tip: Learn right-of-way rules for all aircraft combinations. Giving way when required is a safety imperative, regardless of who 'should' have priority.
key_facts: Right-of-way rules prioritise certain aircraft in specific situations
Aircraft type, direction, and configuration determine precedence
Rules apply on ground and in air
Pilot must always give way if required, regardless of right-of-way

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on minimum height for aircraft operating over congested and non-congested areas
concept: CAR Part 91 sets minimum heights you must maintain over congested and non-congested areas. Over congested areas (towns and cities), the minimum is typically 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle. Over non-congested areas, it's 500 feet. These minimums exist to protect people on the ground. You're responsible for knowing the terrain under your flight path and ensuring you maintain legal minimums. Visual flight planning includes identifying terrain and obstacle heights.
exam_tip: Congested vs non-congested areas have different minimums. Know the 1,000 ft and 500 ft rules. Check terrain when planning routes.
key_facts: 1,000 feet minimum over congested areas
500 feet minimum over non-congested areas
Measured above highest obstacle
PIC is responsible for maintaining minimums

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the operation of aircraft at or near aerodromes
concept: Operating at or near aerodromes requires compliance with specific procedures under CAR Part 91. You need to use designated circuits, follow established procedures, coordinate with ATC at controlled aerodromes, and maintain awareness of other traffic. Aerodrome environments are high-workload, high-traffic areas. You're responsible for following procedures and maintaining situational awareness. Understanding circuit requirements and local procedures is essential before every flight.
exam_tip: Aerodrome operations require strict procedure compliance. Always follow published circuits and coordinate with ATC or use standard patterns.
key_facts: Designated circuits must be used at aerodromes
ATC coordination required at controlled aerodromes
Standard patterns apply at uncontrolled aerodromes
Situational awareness and procedure compliance are mandatory

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions applicable to aircraft wake turbulence
concept: Wake turbulence from larger aircraft can be hazardous to smaller aircraft following behind. CAR Part 91 establishes separation requirements to protect you from wake encounters. You need to understand wake generation (heavier aircraft create stronger vortices), positions vulnerable to wake (directly behind, slightly below), and separation distances required. If you're following a larger aircraft, you need appropriate spacing or you should plan to use different runways or routing to avoid wake entirely.
exam_tip: Larger aircraft generate hazardous wake. Maintain spacing or diverge from the flight path of large aircraft ahead of you.
key_facts: Heavy aircraft generate hazardous wake vortices
Wake most dangerous directly behind and below source aircraft
Separation distances specified in CAR Part 91
Light aircraft vulnerable to wake from heavy aircraft ahead

topic: 16.32 -- VFR Speed Restrictions in Control Zones and Uncontrolled Aerodromes
syllabus_desc: State the speed restrictions for aircraft operating VFR within a control zone and at uncontrolled aerodromes
concept: Speed restrictions in control zones and at uncontrolled aerodromes exist to improve safety in congested airspace and traffic areas. You're required to operate at reduced speeds--typically 250 knots or below in control zones and around uncontrolled aerodromes. These restrictions give you better situational awareness and more time to react to other traffic. Slower speeds are particularly important near high-traffic aerodromes where you're mixing with other aircraft.
exam_tip: Speed restrictions apply in control zones and near uncontrolled aerodromes. Know the specific speeds and when they're mandated.
key_facts: Speed restrictions apply in control zones
Speed restrictions apply near uncontrolled aerodromes
250 knots is typical maximum speed
Restrictions enhance traffic separation and situational awareness

syllabus_desc: State the speed restriction below 10,000 ft in controlled airspace
concept: In controlled airspace below 10,000 feet, you're required to maintain speed restrictions under CAR Part 91. The typical limit is 250 knots, though ATC can permit higher speeds. This restriction applies even in controlled airspace because the lower altitudes have higher air traffic density. You might be cleared to exceed this restriction by ATC, but you must comply with the restriction unless specifically cleared otherwise. Always confirm clearance if you want to exceed this speed limit.
exam_tip: 250 knots maximum below 10,000 feet in controlled airspace unless ATC permits faster. Always confirm clearance before exceeding.
key_facts: 250 knots maximum below 10,000 feet in controlled airspace
Restriction applies throughout controlled airspace
ATC may permit higher speeds by clearance
Pilot must request clearance to exceed restriction

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for joining a controlled aerodrome traffic circuit
concept: At controlled aerodromes, you cannot just join the circuit--you need ATC clearance and you must follow their instructions. ATC will sequence you into the circuit, provide vectors, and coordinate your entry with other traffic. You need to listen carefully to your clearance and follow published circuit procedures. Joining a controlled aerodrome circuit requires two-way communication with ATC and strict adherence to their instructions.
exam_tip: Controlled aerodromes require ATC clearance before joining circuit. Follow ATC instructions and published procedures exactly.
key_facts: ATC clearance required before joining circuit
ATC sequences aircraft and provides vectors
Published circuit procedures must be followed
Two-way communication mandatory

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for joining an uncontrolled aerodrome traffic circuit
concept: At uncontrolled aerodromes, you're responsible for sequencing yourself into the circuit safely. You don't have ATC to help, so you need to monitor the uncontrolled airfield frequency, announce your intentions, and observe other aircraft activity. You'll join at a standard position (often downwind) and follow the standard circuit pattern. Joining uncontrolled circuits requires heightened situational awareness and communication. Always announce your position and intentions clearly.
exam_tip: Uncontrolled circuits require self-sequencing. Monitor frequency, announce your position, and follow standard circuit patterns.
key_facts: No ATC available at uncontrolled aerodromes
Pilot responsible for safe sequencing
Standard circuit positions and patterns used
Frequency monitoring and clear position broadcasts required

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions applicable to operating an aircraft in aerobatic flight
concept: Aerobatic flight is heavily restricted under CAR Part 91. You can only perform aerobatics in designated areas at specified altitudes, and your aircraft must be certified for aerobatic flight. You need appropriate training and ratings, and you must ensure the aircraft's equipment and maintenance meet aerobatic requirements. Aerobatics aren't casual flying--they're specialised operations with specific regulatory requirements.
exam_tip: Aerobatic flight requires approved areas, minimum altitudes, and aircraft certification. You need appropriate training and clearance.
key_facts: Only in designated aerobatic areas permitted
Minimum altitude restrictions apply
Aircraft must be aerobatic certified
Pilot requires aerobatic training and endorsement

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions applicable to parachute-drop operations
concept: Parachute-drop operations are specialised activities with specific restrictions under CAR Part 91. You need designated drop zones, proper coordination with ATC if in controlled airspace, and specific procedures for conducting drops safely. You're responsible for ensuring adequate altitude, proper spacing from other traffic, and that personnel conducting drops are properly trained. Parachute operations create unique hazards for other aircraft, so restrictions protect the broader aviation community.
exam_tip: Parachute drops require designated zones, ATC coordination in controlled airspace, and proper altitude and spacing procedures.
key_facts: Designated drop zones required
ATC coordination mandatory in controlled airspace
Minimum altitude and spacing requirements apply
Personnel must be properly trained and equipped

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions applicable to aircraft towing gliders
concept: Towing gliders is a specialised operation with restrictions under CAR Part 91. Your aircraft must be equipped and certified for towing, the tow rope and attachments must meet specifications, and you need appropriate training. You can only tow in designated areas at appropriate altitudes. Glider towing creates unique handling characteristics and you need to understand stall behaviour with a glider on tow, rope management, and release procedures.
exam_tip: Glider towing requires certified aircraft, proper equipment, designated areas, and specific pilot training.
key_facts: Aircraft must be glider-tow certified
Tow equipment and rope must meet specifications
Designated towing areas required
Pilot requires glider-towing training and endorsement

topic: 16.32 -- Aircraft Towing Objects Other Than Gliders
syllabus_desc: State the restrictions applicable to aircraft towing objects other than gliders
concept: Towing objects like banners, target sleeves, or advertising signs is a specialised operation with restrictions under CAR Part 91. Your aircraft must be certified for towing, equipment must be properly designed and maintained, and you need appropriate training. You can only tow in designated areas and at safe altitudes. Towing operations create unique performance and handling effects--your aircraft's performance is degraded with a tow, and you need to plan accordingly.
exam_tip: Banner/object towing requires certified aircraft, proper equipment, and specific areas. Know your aircraft's performance limitations with tow.
key_facts: Aircraft must be certified for towing operations
Tow equipment must meet specifications
Designated towing areas required
Pilot requires towing endorsement and understanding of performance penalties

syllabus_desc: State the met minima for VFR flight (A) or (H) in various airspace
concept: VFR minimums define the visibility and cloud clearance you need to fly legally under visual flight rules. Different airspace categories have different minimums. In Class A airspace you can't fly VFR at all--that's instrument-only airspace. In other classes, minimums vary by altitude and airspace type. You need to understand these minimums for your operation. Before every VFR flight, you check weather and confirm conditions meet or exceed minimums for your planned route.
exam_tip: VFR minimums vary by airspace class and altitude. Know minimums for your typical operating areas. Weather must meet or exceed minimums.
key_facts: VFR minimums specify visibility and cloud clearance
Different airspace has different minimums
Class A airspace is IFR only
Pilot must verify weather meets minimums before VFR flight

topic: 16.34 -- Special VFR Flight Restrictions and Minimums
syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and met minima for Special VFR flight (A) or (H)
concept: Special VFR (SVFR) is a procedure that allows VFR flight in controlled airspace when standard VFR minimums can't be met. You need ATC clearance to conduct SVFR, and you must operate below 1,000 feet. Weather minimums for SVFR are reduced from standard VFR, but you're still restricted to flight in visual conditions. SVFR is useful when weather's marginal but improving, or when you need to depart from a busy aerodrome in slightly degraded conditions.
exam_tip: SVFR requires ATC clearance and operates below 1,000 feet. Minimums are lower than standard VFR but still require visual conditions.
key_facts: Special VFR requires ATC clearance
Must operate below 1,000 feet AGL
Reduced visibility and cloud clearance minimums apply
Pilot must maintain visual contact with surface

topic: 16.36 -- CAR Part 92 Limitations with Police Members
syllabus_desc: Describe the limitation of CAR Part 92 with respect to members of the Police
concept: CAR Part 92 governs dangerous goods carriage, but there are specific limitations regarding police operations. Police members operating under official duties may have exemptions or different requirements compared to general operators. You need to understand these exemptions if you're involved in police operations, as they affect how you handle dangerous goods carriage in those contexts.
exam_tip: Police operations under CAR Part 92 may have specific exemptions. Understand limitations if you conduct police flight operations.
key_facts: Police operations have specific limitations under CAR Part 92
Exemptions may apply to police members on official duties
Requirements differ from general dangerous goods carriage
Documentation of police authority may be required

topic: 16.36 -- Limitations on Recreational Dangerous Goods Carriage
syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations on the carriage of dangerous good for the recreational use of passengers
concept: Dangerous goods for recreational use (like firearms, ammunition, sporting goods with certain materials) have strict limitations under CAR Part 92. You might be able to carry some items under specific conditions, but generally recreational items are heavily restricted or prohibited. You need to understand what constitutes dangerous goods and what exemptions exist. When passengers ask about carrying potentially dangerous items, you need clear knowledge of what's permissible.
exam_tip: Recreational dangerous goods have strict limitations. Know what items are prohibited or require special handling.
key_facts: Recreational dangerous goods subject to CAR Part 92 restrictions
Many items prohibited outright
Some items may be carried under specific conditions
Carriage documentation required

syllabus_desc: State the general requirements for the carriage of dangerous goods
concept: Dangerous goods carriage is heavily regulated under CAR Part 92. You need proper documentation, packaging, labelling, and handling procedures. Not all dangerous goods can be carried--some are prohibited entirely. You must verify goods are properly declared and packaged before accepting them. Your responsibility includes ensuring crew understand dangerous goods they're carrying and know emergency procedures.
exam_tip: Dangerous goods require documentation, proper packaging, and labelling. You cannot carry undeclared or improperly packaged goods.
key_facts: Dangerous goods must be properly declared
Packaging must meet specifications
Labelling and documentation required
Some goods prohibited on aircraft

topic: 16.36 -- PIC Procedures for Undeclared Dangerous Goods
syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures for the PIC of an aircraft who has been informed that dangerous goods are on board, or suspects that undeclared or misdeclared dangerous goods are on board, the aircraft
concept: If you discover dangerous goods are on board--whether declared, undeclared, or suspected misdeclared--you've got specific procedures to follow under CAR Part 92. You need to immediately assess the risk, inform ATC if necessary, and take appropriate action. For undeclared goods, you have authority to refuse the carriage or divert if already airborne. You'll also need to report the incident to authorities. Your safety responsibility takes absolute precedence.
exam_tip: Undeclared or suspected dangerous goods require immediate action. You can refuse carriage or divert if necessary. Report to authorities.
key_facts: Undeclared goods must trigger immediate assessment
PIC can refuse carriage or divert
ATC must be informed if appropriate
Incident reporting to authorities required

syllabus_desc: State the actions required if there is an in-flight emergency involving dangerous goods
concept: If a dangerous goods emergency occurs in flight--like a package leaking, fire, or suspected contamination--you've got specific response requirements under CAR Part 92. You need to immediately inform ATC, plan an emergency landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome, and protect the aircraft and crew. Your crew needs to know procedures for isolating dangerous goods emergencies. You'll need to coordinate with emergency services on the ground.
exam_tip: Dangerous goods emergencies require immediate ATC notification and diversion to nearest suitable aerodrome. Isolate the emergency if safe to do so.
key_facts: ATC notification immediate and mandatory
Divert to nearest suitable aerodrome
Isolate emergency if safe
Ground emergency services coordination required

syllabus_desc: State the requirements relating to the condition of dangerous goods or their packaging
concept: Dangerous goods must be in good condition, and their packaging must meet specifications under CAR Part 92. You're responsible for checking that goods you accept for carriage are properly packaged--no leaking, no damaged containers, no compromised labelling. If packaging looks questionable, you refuse the goods. You also need to verify that packaging meets the standards required for air transport.
exam_tip: Inspect dangerous goods packaging before accepting. Refuse goods with damaged or questionable packaging.
key_facts: Packaging must meet regulatory specifications
Goods must be in sound condition
No leaking or damaged packages acceptable
PIC responsible for pre-carriage inspection

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for dangerous goods information to be provided to the PIC of an aircraft
concept: Before you accept dangerous goods for carriage, you must receive proper documentation and information under CAR Part 92. This includes what the goods are, hazard classification, packaging details, and emergency procedures. You cannot operate with goods on board if you don't have complete information about what you're carrying. Information must be clear and available--you need to understand exactly what's in your aircraft.
exam_tip: Complete dangerous goods information required before accepting goods. Information must be clear and you must understand the hazards.
key_facts: Complete documentation required
Hazard classification and emergency procedures specified
PIC must understand goods characteristics
Information must be clear and immediately accessible

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the PIC and the operator to inform the ATS unit when carrying dangerous goods
concept: When you're carrying dangerous goods, both you and your operator need to inform ATS appropriately under CAR Part 92. This notification ensures ATC understands your aircraft is carrying hazardous materials and can coordinate appropriately with ground services if needed. Notification might happen through NOTAM systems or direct ATC coordination depending on goods type and operation.
exam_tip: Dangerous goods carriage requires ATS notification. Ensure both your operator and you inform ATC appropriately.
key_facts: ATS notification mandatory for dangerous goods
Operator and PIC share notification responsibility
Notification through appropriate channels
ATC coordination essential for emergency response

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: (a) helicopter external load operation; (b) helicopter external load towing operation; (c) helicopter sling load operation; and, (d) OGE. CAR 133.
concept: For helicopter operations, you need to understand specific terminology. An external load operation is any carriage of loads outside the helicopter's fuselage. External load towing involves towing loads from a long line, while sling load operations have the load suspended directly. OGE (Out of Ground Effect) describes helicopter performance when hovering well above terrain or water--performance is significantly degraded. These distinctions matter for crew competency, equipment requirements, and operational procedures.
exam_tip: Know the distinction between towing, sling load, and OGE operations. Each has different requirements and performance characteristics.
key_facts: External load operation: load outside fuselage
Towing operation: load suspended from long line
Sling load operation: load suspended directly
OGE: hovering out of ground effect

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: helicopter external load operation. CAR Part 133
concept: A helicopter external load operation is any operation in which a helicopter carries a load external to the aircraft's fuselage. This covers all types of external carriage -- sling loads, towing, winching, and any other method of carrying loads outside the helicopter.
exam_tip: External load operation is the umbrella term -- it covers all external carriage methods. The sub-types (towing, sling, etc.) fall under this.
key_facts: Any operation carrying a load external to the fuselage
Umbrella term for all external load types
Regulated under CAR Part 133
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 133

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: helicopter external load towing operation. CAR Part 133
concept: A helicopter external load towing operation involves the helicopter towing a load that is suspended from the aircraft by a long line or cable during flight. The load follows the helicopter and is typically at some distance below and behind the aircraft.
exam_tip: Towing means a load on a long line -- think of a bambi bucket for firefighting or equipment being repositioned. The load is towed below and behind.
key_facts: Load towed on a long line or cable
Load follows at distance below and behind
Example: bambi bucket for firefighting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 133

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: helicopter sling load operation. CAR Part 133
concept: A helicopter sling load operation involves the helicopter carrying a load suspended directly beneath the aircraft on a sling or cargo hook. The load hangs directly below the helicopter during flight.
exam_tip: Sling load = load hanging directly below on a cargo hook or sling. More direct connection than towing. Common for construction, forestry, and supply operations.
key_facts: Load suspended directly beneath the helicopter
Uses sling or cargo hook
More direct connection than towing operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 133

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: OGE. CAR Part 133
concept: OGE means out of ground effect. When a helicopter hovers above the height where ground effect provides additional lift (typically above approximately one rotor diameter height), it's hovering OGE. This requires more power than an in ground effect (IGE) hover because the rotor downwash doesn't get the benefit of the ground cushion.
exam_tip: OGE hover requires more power than IGE. The height at which ground effect is lost varies but is typically about one rotor diameter above the surface.
key_facts: Out of ground effect
Above approximately one rotor diameter height
Requires more power than IGE hover
No ground cushion benefit from rotor downwash
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, CAR Part 133

syllabus_desc: State the pilot licence requirements for performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter external load operations require specific pilot qualifications under CAR Part 133. You need appropriate training, endorsements, and currency. Not all helicopter pilots are qualified to conduct external load operations. The operations are complex and demand specialist knowledge about weight and balance with external loads, performance limitations, emergency procedures, and crew coordination.
exam_tip: External load operations require specific pilot endorsement. Check your licence to confirm authorisation.
key_facts: Specific licence endorsement required
Specialist training required before endorsement
Currency requirements apply
Not all helicopter pilots qualified to conduct these operations

syllabus_desc: Describe the minimum height requirements when performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter external load operations have minimum height restrictions under CAR Part 133. These restrictions are typically higher than normal helicopter minimums because the added complexity and risk of external load operations demand higher safety margins. You need adequate height for recovery options if something goes wrong with the load or aircraft. Minimum heights vary depending on the specific operation type (towing vs sling load) and the terrain.
exam_tip: External load operations have minimum height requirements higher than standard operations. Know requirements for your specific operation type.
key_facts: Minimum heights specified in CAR Part 133
Heights higher than standard helicopter operations
Varies by operation type and terrain
Adequate recovery height essential

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on the carriage of persons inside a helicopter on a helicopter external load towing operation. CAR 133
concept: When you're conducting external load towing operations, you've got restrictions on how many persons you can carry inside the helicopter under CAR Part 133. Generally, you can only carry persons essential to the operation--pilots, observer, and those absolutely necessary for safety. You cannot carry passengers or excess crew. The reasoning is simple: external load towing is a complex operation demanding your full attention, and every person on board affects weight, balance, and workload.
exam_tip: Towing operations restrict non-essential personnel. Only essential crew for the operation can be carried.
key_facts: Only essential personnel permissible
Passenger carriage not permitted
Crew limited to operation requirements
Every person affects weight and balance

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on the carriage of persons inside a helicopter on a helicopter sling load operation. CAR 133
concept: Sling load operations also have restrictions on person carriage under CAR Part 133. Like towing operations, only essential personnel can be carried--typically pilots and essential crew. The operation's complexity demands crew focus, and carrying additional persons affects the helicopter's performance and your options for managing emergencies. Non-essential passengers are never permissible during sling load operations.
exam_tip: Sling load operations restrict personnel to essential crew only. No passengers or non-essential crew permitted.
key_facts: Only essential crew for operation can be carried
Passengers prohibited
Non-essential personnel not permitted
Crew selection critical for operation success

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on the carriage of persons inside a helicopter on a winching, rappelling, or human sling load operation. CAR 133
concept: Winching, rappelling, and human sling load operations (operations involving personnel being hoisted or deployed from the helicopter) have strict restrictions on additional person carriage under CAR Part 133. These operations are extremely complex and dangerous, demanding all crew attention. You can only carry persons absolutely essential to the operation itself. The persons being winched or deployed are your primary focus--you cannot distract crew or degrade capacity with non-essential personnel.
exam_tip: Personnel operations restrict carriage to essential crew. Typically only pilots and necessary support crew can be aboard.
key_facts: Only essential crew for operation permitted
Personnel deployment operations extremely complex
Crew attention fully committed to operation
No non-essential personnel carriage

syllabus_desc: State the third-party risk restrictions when carrying a load suspended beneath a helicopter. CAR 133
concept: Suspended loads create significant third-party risk--there's a possibility of load drop or equipment failure affecting people on the ground. CAR Part 133 restricts where you can conduct these operations. Generally, you cannot carry loads over congested areas, populated areas, or areas with third parties who haven't consented to the risk. The restrictions protect people on the ground from potential load drop hazards. You need to plan routes and timing to minimise third-party exposure.
exam_tip: Suspended loads cannot be carried over congested or populated areas. Route planning must avoid third-party risk areas.
key_facts: Load drop risk restricts routing
Congested areas prohibited
Populated areas without consent restricted
Route planning critical to third-party safety

syllabus_desc: State the weight limitation for a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter external load operations have weight limitations under CAR Part 133. You cannot exceed the helicopter's certified maximum external load weight. These limits are determined by structural capability, flight dynamics, and recovery characteristics. Exceeding weight limits degrades performance, compromises control authority, and increases accident risk. You're responsible for verifying load weight before accepting it and ensuring you stay within limits.
exam_tip: Know your helicopter's maximum external load certification. Verify load weight before operations and refuse overweight loads.
key_facts: Maximum load weight specified in aircraft documentation
Structural and performance limits apply
Weight affects aircraft control and recovery
PIC responsible for weight verification

syllabus_desc: State the flight rules restriction for a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: If you're conducting helicopter external load operations under a commercial air transport operation (Part 135), you've got additional requirements beyond Part 133. These cover operator certification, crew qualifications, maintenance standards, and operational procedures. Part 135 operations are more highly regulated than private external load operations. You need to understand how Part 135 requirements interact with Part 133 requirements for your specific operation.
exam_tip: Part 135 operators have additional requirements to Part 133. Know your operator's specific requirements and approvals.
key_facts: Part 135 operators subject to enhanced requirements
Operator certification required
Crew qualifications must meet Part 135 standards
Maintenance and operational procedures specified

syllabus_desc: Describe the restrictions on helicopter external load operations at night. CAR 133
concept: Carrying dangerous goods on helicopter external load operations introduces additional hazards under CAR Part 133. Dangerous goods carried externally create unique risks--load drop could scatter hazardous materials, and the external location limits crew's ability to respond to emergencies. Requirements cover proper packaging, documentation, crew briefing, and emergency procedures. You need to understand what dangerous goods can be carried externally and what restrictions apply.
exam_tip: Dangerous goods on external loads require special consideration. Know what goods are permissible and required procedures.
key_facts: Dangerous goods carriage externally restricted
Packaging must contain spill risks
Documentation and crew briefing essential
Emergency procedures required

syllabus_desc: Describe the flight characteristics requirements for a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: You're responsible for ensuring the load is properly secured, balanced, and the release mechanism functions correctly before flight under CAR Part 133. You can't just trust that someone else has checked it--you need to verify the load yourself or directly supervise someone doing the checking. Understanding release mechanism function is critical. If you're not confident in load security or release mechanism operation, you don't fly. Your responsibility is absolute.
exam_tip: Verify load security and release mechanism function yourself. Do not fly if you're uncertain about load integrity.
key_facts: Load must be properly secured and balanced
Release mechanism must function correctly
PIC verification mandatory
Pre-flight inspection required before load operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for performing a helicopter external load operation over congested areas. CAR 133
concept: Operating with external loads over congested areas is generally prohibited or severely restricted under CAR Part 133. The risk to third parties from load drop is too significant. If you absolutely need to conduct external load operations over populated areas, you'll need special authorisation from CAANZ and must meet specific risk mitigation requirements. Generally, you'll avoid congested areas entirely when carrying external loads. The principle is protecting people on the ground.
exam_tip: External loads over congested areas generally prohibited. Special authorisation needed if essential. Route planning must avoid populated areas.
key_facts: Congested areas generally prohibited for external loads
Special CAANZ approval required if necessary
Risk mitigation measures required
Alternative routing preferred

syllabus_desc: Describe the general requirements for performing an operation involving the suspension of a person beneath a helicopter. CAR 133
concept: Suspending personnel beneath a helicopter (winching, rappelling, rescue operations) requires strict compliance under CAR Part 133. You need specialised equipment, highly trained crew, documented procedures, and emergency recovery plans. Every person must have appropriate harnesses, attachments, and training. You need an experienced observer, ground coordination, and clear communication. Personnel suspension operations are among the most complex and dangerous helicopter operations--they demand meticulous preparation and execution.
exam_tip: Personnel suspension requires specialist training, equipment, and procedures. Multiple crew coordination essential. Emergency plans required.
key_facts: Specialist crew training required
Specialised equipment required
Observer and ground coordination essential
Emergency procedures and recovery plans mandatory

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for performing a helicopter winch operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter winch operations (hoisting personnel or cargo) have specific requirements under CAR Part 133. Your helicopter must have appropriate winch equipment, properly maintained and certified. You need trained crew--pilots and winch operators with specialist knowledge. You need detailed procedures, weight limits, and height limitations. Winch operations demand smooth helicopter handling, precise positioning, and excellent crew coordination. Every person involved needs training specific to winching operations.
exam_tip: Winch operations require certified equipment, trained crew, and documented procedures. Smooth helicopter handling is essential.
key_facts: Certified winch equipment required
Crew must be trained in winch operations
Weight and height limitations apply
Detailed procedures required for each operation

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the carriage of an injured person beneath a helicopter in a harness or stretcher. CAR 133
concept: Carrying injured persons in harnesses or stretchers beneath a helicopter is a rescue/medical operation with specific requirements under CAR Part 133. The person's condition must be stable enough for suspension. Appropriate harnesses or stretchers certified for flight must be used. Medical personnel might be required depending on the person's condition. Crew must have rescue/medical training appropriate to the operation. Coordination with ground medical services is essential.
exam_tip: Injured person suspension requires appropriate equipment, crew training, and coordination. Medical assessment may be required.
key_facts: Certified harnesses or stretchers required
Person's condition must be suitable for suspension
Rescue-trained crew required
Medical coordination may be necessary

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for performing a helicopter rappelling operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter rappelling (personnel abseiling from the helicopter) requires strict compliance under CAR Part 133. You need appropriately trained personnel, certified rappelling equipment, and detailed operational procedures. Not all helicopter pilots or crews are trained for rappelling operations--it's a specialist skill. Your helicopter must be configured appropriately, with suitable attachment points. You need clear communication systems, observer coverage, and emergency procedures. Every rappeller needs specialist training and current currency.
exam_tip: Rappelling requires specialist crew training, certified equipment, and detailed procedures. Observer and communication essential.
key_facts: Specialist crew training required
Certified rappelling equipment required
Observer required during operations
Communication systems and procedures essential

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for the carriage of a supplementary crew member on a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: Helicopter external load operations may require supplementary crew members (observers, load masters) to manage the operation safely under CAR Part 133. These crew members have specific responsibilities for load management, crew coordination, and external load monitoring. Supplementary crew must be appropriately trained and competent. Their presence improves safety by providing dedicated load management while you focus on flying. You need to understand when supplementary crew is required and what their responsibilities entail.
exam_tip: Supplementary crew requirements depend on operation type. Know when observers or load masters are required.
key_facts: Supplementary crew may be required depending on operation
Observers or load masters provide load management
Crew must be trained and competent
Clear role definition required for each operation

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for ensuring crew member competency to carryout winching, rappelling, or human sling load operations. CAR 133
concept: Crew conducting winching, rappelling, or human sling load operations must be demonstrably competent under CAR Part 133. Competency isn't assumed from general helicopter experience--it requires specialist training specific to each operation type. You need documented training records, proficiency checks, and currency requirements. As PIC, you're responsible for ensuring your crew has appropriate competency before you conduct these operations. Incompetent crew conducting complex operations is a significant accident risk.
exam_tip: Crew competency is non-negotiable. Ensure documented training and currency before attempting specialist operations.
key_facts: Specialist training required for each operation type
Competency must be documented and verified
Currency requirements apply
PIC responsible for crew competency assessment

syllabus_desc: Describe the external load equipment requirements on a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: External load operations require specialised equipment under CAR Part 133. This includes load suspension systems, hooks, bridles, release mechanisms, and cargo nets--all must meet certification standards. Equipment must be appropriate for the load type and weight. Maintenance and inspection standards are rigorous. You need to ensure all equipment is certified, properly maintained, and inspected before each operation. Faulty or inappropriate equipment compromises flight safety.
exam_tip: All external load equipment must be certified and properly maintained. Inspect before each operation. Replace any suspect equipment.
key_facts: All equipment must be certified for flight use
Maintenance and inspection standards rigorous
Equipment appropriate for load type and weight
PIC responsible for equipment verification

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for quick release devices on a helicopter performing a helicopter external load operation. CAR 133
concept: Quick release devices (emergency load drop systems) are critical safety equipment for helicopter external load operations under CAR Part 133. These devices must be certified, properly functioning, and immediately accessible. The quick release allows you to jettison a load in an emergency (engine failure, control problems, etc.). Your helicopter's quick release must meet certification standards and you need crew training in its operation. Regular checks ensure the device functions correctly--a failed quick release in an emergency could be catastrophic.
exam_tip: Quick release devices must function reliably. Check operation before each flight. Crew must be trained in deployment.
key_facts: Certified quick release device required
Must function reliably in emergencies
Crew training essential
Pre-flight functional check required

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for the maintenance of external load equipment. CAR 133
concept: External load equipment maintenance is strictly regulated under CAR Part 133. You need documented maintenance schedules, inspection intervals, and maintenance records for all equipment. Some items require periodic replacement. Maintenance must be performed by appropriately trained personnel. You're responsible for ensuring maintenance is completed and documented. Poorly maintained equipment creates significant risk--a failure during operation could result in load drop or aircraft damage.
exam_tip: Maintain detailed maintenance records for all external load equipment. Schedule regular inspections and replacements as required.
key_facts: Maintenance schedules specified for all equipment
Inspection intervals established
Maintenance records required
PIC responsible for ensuring maintenance compliance

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 crew qualification and experience requirements.
concept: When you're operating under Part 135, you need to meet specific qualification and experience standards set out in the CARs. Your pilot-in-command needs to have the appropriate aircraft type rating and meet the minimum flight hours specified. You'll also need to ensure any second-in-command or flight crew members hold the required licences and have the experience CAANZ expects for that operation. It's not just about having a licence--you need the right endorsements and hours logged on type or similar aircraft.
exam_tip: Remember Part 135 is tougher than Part 91. Know the PIC minimums and what type ratings or endorsements are required.
key_facts: PIC must hold appropriate aircraft type rating
Minimum flight hours specified in CAR Part 135
SIC requirements depend on operation type
Recent experience requirements apply

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 flight and duty time limitations on flight crew members.
concept: CAR Part 135 flight and duty time limits are your workload safety net. You've got maximum flight hours you can do in a day, and there are cumulative limits over 7 and 28 days. Your duty time--that's when you're on call or preparing for flight--can't exceed what's reasonable for safety. These aren't suggestions; they're regulatory requirements. You'll need to manage your rostering carefully to stay compliant, and if you're approaching limits, you've got to recognize when you need a break or when another crew member needs to step in.
exam_tip: Know the daily maximums and the 7-day and 28-day limits. Duty time is wider than just flight time.
key_facts: Daily flight time limits apply
7-day cumulative limits in CAR Part 135
28-day cumulative limits in CAR Part 135
Duty time limits include non-flying duties

syllabus_desc: State the normal minimum rest period required following any duty period. AC119-2
concept: After you've finished a duty period, you need a solid rest break before you can be legal again. That's not just a quick nap--it's a properly defined minimum rest period set out in AC 119-2. The length depends on how long your duty was and what your flight duties involved. As PIC, you've got to respect these requirements and ensure your crew gets proper rest. Ignoring fatigue management is one of the quickest ways to compromise flight safety, and CAANZ takes this seriously.
exam_tip: Rest periods are absolute minimums. Know the baseline requirement and any variations based on duty length.
key_facts: Minimum rest specified in AC 119-2
Rest period follows end of duty
Fatigue management is regulatory requirement
Variations based on duty period length

syllabus_desc: State the maximum number of flight hours that a pilot may fly as crew in an aircraft which carries two pilots on an internal air operation. AC119-2
concept: When you're the second pilot on a two-crew operation under Part 135, there's a specific ceiling on how many hours you can log. AC 119-2 sets this out to prevent fatigue and ensure you're sharp when you're needed. This is different from your PIC maximums, so don't mix them up. It's all about managing workload and ensuring both pilots stay fit and alert throughout the operation.
exam_tip: This is the two-pilot crew maximum, not the PIC maximum. Look for the specific figure in AC 119-2.
key_facts: Applies to two-pilot operations under Part 135
Separate from PIC flight time limits
Specified in AC 119-2
Prevents fatigue in multi-crew environment

syllabus_desc: State the airworthiness requirements for aircraft used on air operations. CAR 135
concept: Your aircraft has got to be airworthy before you can operate it under Part 135. That means a current Certificate of Airworthiness, regular maintenance inspections, and compliance with any applicable airworthiness directives. You can't just assume the maintenance team's got it sorted--as PIC, you've got the responsibility to satisfy yourself the aircraft is airworthy. CAR Part 135 spells out what that means, and it's tighter than Part 91 because you're carrying passengers for remuneration.
exam_tip: Part 135 airworthiness standards are stricter than Part 91. Know what makes an aircraft unfit for commercial ops.
key_facts: Current Certificate of Airworthiness required
Maintenance inspection compliance mandatory
Airworthiness directives must be complied with
PIC has final airworthiness responsibility

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 minimum heights for VFR flights.
concept: When you're operating VFR under Part 135, you've got to maintain specific minimum heights above terrain and built-up areas. You can't just cruise at whatever altitude feels right. These minimums exist to give you reaction time if something goes wrong and to keep you clear of terrain. They'll vary depending on the type of airspace and what's underneath--built-up areas have stricter requirements than rural zones. Know these figures cold; they're non-negotiable for Part 135.
exam_tip: Part 135 VFR minimums are higher than Part 91. Learn the specific heights for different terrain types.
key_facts: Minimum heights vary by terrain type
Built-up areas require higher minimums
Stricter than Part 91 requirements
Measured above ground level

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 operating restriction on single-engine air operations under IFR (SEIFR).
concept: Single-engine aircraft operations under IFR in a commercial Part 135 environment are heavily restricted or prohibited outright. CAR Part 135 recognises the safety risk of single-engine flight in instrument conditions--if your engine fails, you've got no backup. Most Part 135 operators won't touch SEIFR, and the rules reflect that. You'll find either strict limitations on routes and altitudes, or an outright ban depending on the specific operation. Know what the CARs say about whether it's allowed and, if it is, what the constraints are.
exam_tip: SEIFR under Part 135 is a critical constraint. Know whether it's permitted and any associated restrictions.
key_facts: Heavily restricted or prohibited under Part 135
Safety concern with single-engine IFR
Route and altitude limitations may apply
Specific rules in CAR Part 135

syllabus_desc: State the requirement to keep a daily flight record. CAR 135
concept: As PIC, you're required to familiarise yourself with the current and forecast meteorological conditions relevant to your flight before you depart. That's not just a quick glance at the TAF--you need to properly understand what the weather is doing and what it's likely to do along your route and at your destination. CAR Part 135 makes this your legal responsibility. You've got to use the available information--METAR, TAF, significant weather, SIGMET--and make a sound decision about whether it's safe to proceed. If you don't understand the weather picture, you shouldn't go.
exam_tip: PIC must obtain and understand current and forecast weather. Non-compliance with this is a serious breach.
key_facts: PIC responsibility to obtain weather information
Current conditions via METAR required
Forecast conditions via TAF required
Must be understood before flight

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirement for a maintenance review.
concept: You've got to brief your passengers before you depart. That includes safety information about seatbelts, emergency exits, oxygen (if applicable), and what to do in an emergency. It's not just reading a checklist--you're ensuring passengers understand the basics so they can look after themselves if something unexpected happens. CAR Part 135 specifies what's got to be covered. You might do this yourself or delegate it to a cabin attendant, but the responsibility is yours as PIC. Treat it seriously; it's a safety requirement, not a regulatory tick-box.
exam_tip: Passenger briefing is mandatory before flight. Know what topics must be covered under Part 135.
key_facts: Briefing required before every flight
Safety procedures must be explained
Seatbelts and emergency exits covered
PIC responsibility to ensure completion

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirement for passenger safety and the carriage of certain passengers.
concept: CAR Part 135 sets out specific requirements for keeping passengers safe on air operations. The operator's got to ensure passengers are properly briefed, seated, and restrained during critical phases of flight. There are also restrictions on carrying certain categories of passengers -- for example, stretcher patients, prisoners under escort, or deportees. These aren't optional niceties; they're legal requirements designed to manage risk when you're carrying people commercially. The PIC needs to be satisfied that the appropriate safety measures are in place before accepting passengers on board.
exam_tip: Watch for questions about who qualifies as a 'certain passenger' and what extra precautions are required for stretcher patients or escorted persons.
key_facts: CAR Part 135 mandates passenger safety briefings and restraint requirements
Specific provisions apply to stretcher patients, prisoners, and deportees
PIC must be satisfied passenger safety measures are in place
Operator must ensure compliance with all passenger carriage requirements

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 restrictions when refuelling.
concept: Refuelling an aircraft on an air operation comes with specific restrictions under CAR Part 135. You can't just top up while passengers are milling about without precautions. If passengers are on board, embarking, or disembarking during refuelling, there are strict safety requirements -- fire extinguishers must be immediately available, a clear escape route must be maintained, and in some cases a crew member must be stationed at the door. For helicopter operations, rotor-running refuelling has its own set of rules. The core principle is simple: fuel is flammable, so the rules exist to minimise ignition risk and ensure a quick evacuation path if something goes wrong.
exam_tip: Know the specific conditions under which refuelling with passengers on board is permitted -- fire extinguisher positioning and escape routes are common exam topics.
key_facts: CAR Part 135 restricts refuelling operations when passengers are present
Fire extinguishers must be immediately available during refuelling
Clear escape routes must be maintained at all times
Rotor-running refuelling has additional specific requirements for helicopters

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 restrictions on the manipulation of an aircraft's controls.
concept: CAR Part 135 restricts who can manipulate the controls of an aircraft on an air operation. Only qualified and authorised flight crew members can handle the controls -- you can't let a passenger have a go, no matter how enthusiastic they are. There's also the training element: if a trainee pilot is manipulating controls under supervision, the operation must comply with the relevant training provisions. This rule exists because commercial operations carry paying passengers who are trusting their safety to qualified professionals. The PIC remains responsible for the aircraft at all times, regardless of who's physically handling the controls.
exam_tip: Remember: on an air operation under Part 135, only authorised flight crew may manipulate the controls. The PIC's overall responsibility doesn't change even during supervised training.
key_facts: Only qualified flight crew may manipulate controls on a Part 135 operation
Passengers are not permitted to handle aircraft controls
Trainee pilots must comply with specific training provisions
PIC retains ultimate responsibility regardless of who handles the controls

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirement for helicopter operations over congested areas. (Helicopter candidates only)
concept: Helicopters operating over congested areas on Part 135 air operations face additional requirements beyond the standard minimum height rules. The key concern is third-party risk -- if you lose your engine over a crowded town centre, where do you go? CAR Part 135 requires that helicopter operations over congested areas must be conducted at a height that allows a safe forced landing or autorotation to a suitable area in the event of an engine failure. This typically means you need a clear area within autorotative glide range. For NZ operations, think about flying over places like central Hamilton or Queenstown's built-up areas -- you've got to plan your route to maintain escape options.
exam_tip: The key point is autorotative capability over congested areas -- you must be able to reach a safe landing area in the event of engine failure.
key_facts: Helicopter operations over congested areas require compliance with specific Part 135 provisions
Must maintain a height allowing safe autorotation to a suitable landing area
Third-party risk on the ground is the primary safety concern
Route planning must account for engine failure escape options over built-up areas

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on helicopter sling loads on an air operation. CAR 135 (Helicopter candidates only)
concept: When you're carrying a sling load on a Part 135 air operation, there are specific restrictions beyond those in CAR Part 133. The sling load operation must be conducted in accordance with the operator's exposition, and the PIC must be satisfied that the load is properly rigged and within the helicopter's weight and balance limits. You can't carry a sling load over congested areas unless specific conditions are met. The load must be jettisoned if it poses a hazard to the helicopter's safe flight -- your quick-release has to work, every time. For Part 135 operations, the operator must also have procedures for sling load operations documented in their exposition.
exam_tip: Know the difference between CAR 133 (external load rules generally) and CAR 135 (additional requirements when it's an air operation). They work together.
key_facts: Sling load operations on Part 135 air ops must comply with both CAR 133 and CAR 135
The operator's exposition must include sling load procedures
Loads over congested areas face additional restrictions
Quick-release capability must be maintained for emergency jettison
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Parts 133 and 135

topic: 16.44 -- Air Operations Meteorological Requirements and Restrictions
syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 meteorological conditions and requirements for an air operation under VFR.
concept: VFR operations under Part 135 need you to maintain minimum cloud heights and visibility. These are stricter than Part 91 because you're carrying passengers commercially. You need a certain flight visibility and your cloud base needs to be at a specified height so you can see and avoid other traffic and terrain. These minima apply whether you're planning to fly VFR or you're considering an IFR flight but want to operate VFR instead. Know the figures and know that if weather is forecast to drop below minima, you've either got to file IFR or delay the flight.
exam_tip: Part 135 VFR weather minima are tougher than Part 91. Memorise the visibility and cloud base requirements.
key_facts: Flight visibility minima specified
Cloud base minima specified
Stricter than Part 91 requirements
Applied before departure and enroute

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 performance requirements for takeoff distance.
concept: Your aircraft's take-off and landing performance is critical under Part 135. You've got to ensure you can take off safely from your departure aerodrome with your payload and fuel, and you've got to be able to land at your destination and your planned alternate within safe performance margins. CAR Part 135 specifies how you calculate this--you'll use aircraft performance data and factor in conditions like runway length, temperature, and elevation. You can't just assume you'll make it; you've got to prove it with calculations before you go. If the numbers don't work, you adjust your payload or find another aerodrome.
exam_tip: Part 135 requires documented performance calculations. Know that runway length, temperature, and weight all factor in.
key_facts: Performance calculations required before flight
Take-off distance must not exceed available runway
Landing distance must not exceed available runway
Safety margins specified in CAR Part 135

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 performance requirements for landing distance.
concept: Multi-engine aircraft have enroute performance requirements that you've got to meet under Part 135. You need to be able to maintain your planned altitude and route even if you lose an engine. This is where your climb performance becomes critical--you've got to prove you can continue climbing or at least maintain altitude on one engine. The rules ensure you're not flying a route where an engine failure would force you into terrain or unprepared areas. You'll calculate this using your aircraft's single-engine performance data and factor in temperature and elevation along your planned route.
exam_tip: Know the single-engine climb requirements. You must be able to clear terrain on one engine.
key_facts: Single-engine climb performance checked
Terrain clearance on one engine required
Route must be feasible with engine failure
Calculated using aircraft performance data

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 performance requirements for landing on wet and contaminated runways.
concept: Single-engine aircraft operating under Part 135 have their own performance rules. You've got to ensure you can take off, climb out, and maintain altitude with your planned weight. The performance margins are typically tighter than multi-engine requirements because there's no engine backup. You can't do single-engine IFR, so your route planning is constrained to areas where you can always reach a suitable landing site. CAR Part 135 specifies climb gradients and performance margins you've got to meet. It's less about redundancy and more about ensuring the aircraft's got the oomph to get you where you're going safely.
exam_tip: Single-engine performance limits are about climb capability and terrain clearance. Know the specific gradient requirements.
key_facts: Climb performance margins required
Take-off distance calculated and checked
Terrain clearance on original engine only
Route planning constrained by performance

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of a performance-class 1 helicopter. CAR Part 1 (Helicopter candidates only)
concept: A performance-class 1 helicopter is one that, in the event of a critical engine failure, can either continue the flight safely or abort the takeoff and stop within the available distance. Think of it as the helicopter equivalent of multi-engine aeroplane performance -- you've got enough power from the remaining engine(s) to handle the failure at any point during the flight. CAR Part 1 defines performance-class 1 specifically: the helicopter must be able to land safely with one engine inoperative at any point after the takeoff decision point. This is the highest performance class and is typically required for operations into challenging environments or carrying large numbers of passengers.
exam_tip: Performance-class 1 means the helicopter can safely continue or stop after a critical engine failure at any point -- it's the 'gold standard' of helicopter performance classification.
key_facts: Performance-class 1 is defined in CAR Part 1
Must be able to continue flight or abort safely after critical engine failure
Applies from the takeoff decision point onwards
Highest performance classification -- typically required for multi-engine helicopter operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for the obtaining and considering relevant information prior to flight. CAR 91
concept: Before you fly, you've got to gather all the relevant information you need to conduct the flight safely. CAR Part 91 requires you to obtain current aeronautical information including weather, NOTAM, aerodrome information, and navigation data. You can't just launch without checking what's changed since the last flight. This is fundamental preflight planning. You'll use sources like the AIP, NOTAM lists, TAF, and METAR. The extent of information you need depends on the flight--a local VFR flight needs less than a cross-country IFR operation, but the principle is the same: you've got to know what you're flying into.
exam_tip: Information gathering is a legal requirement. Know the main sources: AIP, NOTAM, weather, and aerodrome status.
key_facts: NOTAM information must be obtained
Weather information required (METAR, TAF)
Aerodrome information must be current
Navigation data must be up-to-date

syllabus_desc: Describe the publications and their content that provide operational route and aerodrome information.
concept: You've got several key publications that give you the route and aerodrome information you need. The Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP) is your primary source--it contains permanent aerodrome data, approach procedures, and enroute information. The AIP includes sections on airports, navigation aids, and airspace restrictions. You'll also use the NOTAM system for temporary information and changes. Some operators produce their own operations manuals with detailed information for their regular routes and aerodromes. These publications are structured to give you quick access to critical information--runway lengths, lighting, services available, and procedure details.
exam_tip: Know the structure of the AIP and what sections cover route versus aerodrome information. NOTAM covers temporary changes.
key_facts: AIP is primary permanent source
AIP divided into sections (airports, airways, etc.)
NOTAM provides temporary information
Operator manuals may supplement AIP

syllabus_desc: Derive operational information from charts and publications that provide route and aerodrome information.
concept: You need to be able to read and interpret the charts and publications to extract the information you actually need for your flight. That means understanding how the AIP is organised, knowing how to read approach plates, being able to interpret NOTAMs, and being able to extract critical data like runway dimensions, elevation, and lighting from aerodrome information. This is practical stuff--you might need to figure out whether your aircraft can use a particular runway given its length and surface, or whether an approach is available under the conditions you're expecting. It's not just knowing where to look; it's extracting the right data and applying it to your specific situation.
exam_tip: This is about practical application. Can you work out go/no-go decisions from AIP and NOTAM data?
key_facts: Must interpret runway data and limitations
NOTAM information affects aerodrome usability
Approach plate interpretation required
Performance planning uses published data

syllabus_desc: State the fuel reserve (A) or (H) required for a day VFR flight. CAR 91
concept: For day VFR flights, you've got to carry fuel reserves to cover you if your plan goes sideways. CAR Part 91 requires you to carry enough fuel to reach your destination plus a reserve. That reserve gives you time to find an alternate if weather's worse than forecast or you've got a headwind you didn't expect. The reserve size depends on the fuel type you're carrying--reserves are often expressed as Reserve (A) for avgas or Reserve (H) for Jet-A, with specific time minimums. It's not a huge reserve for day VFR, but it's enough to keep you out of trouble if things don't go exactly to plan.
exam_tip: Day VFR fuel reserve is straightforward. Know the specific time requirement for your fuel type.
key_facts: Reserve required beyond destination requirement
Reserve (A) for avgas specified
Reserve (H) for Jet-A specified
Expressed as time minimum

syllabus_desc: State the fuel reserve (A) or (H) required for a night VFR flight. CAR 91
concept: Night VFR is trickier than day VFR because your options are more limited if something goes wrong. You can't just land anywhere if fuel gets tight--you need to land at a lit aerodrome, and that might not be close. CAR Part 91 requires you to carry a bigger fuel reserve for night VFR than day. That extra reserve gives you a buffer to find a suitable lit aerodrome if your plan changes or winds are worse than forecast. Again, the reserve is expressed as Reserve (A) or Reserve (H) depending on your fuel type. The time requirement is longer than day VFR because at night you're more constrained.
exam_tip: Night VFR reserves are bigger than day. Know the specific time requirement for each fuel type.
key_facts: Larger reserve than day VFR
Reserve (A) for avgas specified
Reserve (H) for Jet-A specified
Limited landing options at night increase requirement

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 requirements for the filing of a flight plan.
concept: When you're operating Part 135, you've got to file a flight plan for your flight. That's not optional--it's a mandatory requirement. You file it before you depart, and it goes into the system so search and rescue knows where you're supposed to be. The flight plan includes your route, cruising altitude, estimate times, and other critical details. CAR Part 135 is specific about this because you're operating commercially and your operation needs to be trackable. You can't just take off without formally notifying the aviation system of your intentions.
exam_tip: Part 135 flight plan filing is mandatory. Know the timing requirement--typically before departure.
key_facts: Flight plan filing mandatory for Part 135
Filed before departure
Contains route and estimate times
Required for search and rescue coordination

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the notification of changes to the filed flight plan. CAR 91
concept: If things change after you've filed your flight plan, you've got to notify the aviation system. Maybe you're going to be later than your estimate time, or you're diverting to a different destination, or your cruising level's changed. CAR Part 91 requires you to amend your flight plan so the information in the system matches your actual flight. This is search and rescue stuff--if they're looking for you and the system says you're going to one destination but you've actually diverted somewhere else, that's a problem. You notify through ATS (Air Traffic Services), and the timing depends on what you're changing.
exam_tip: Know when and how to amend flight plans. Significant changes need to be notified promptly.
key_facts: Changes must be notified to ATS
Estimate time changes notify ATS
Route changes must be cleared
Destination changes notified promptly

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for terminating a flight plan. CAR 91
concept: When you land, you've got to tell ATS you've arrived. That's terminating your flight plan, and it's critical for search and rescue. If you file a flight plan and then land at your destination without closing it, the system still thinks you're airborne. If you're long overdue and haven't closed the plan, search and rescue starts getting concerned and might launch a search. You close the flight plan with ATS when you land, or sometimes with the tower if you're landing at a controlled aerodrome. It's simple but essential--don't forget this step.
exam_tip: Flight plan termination is as important as filing. Always close your plan on landing.
key_facts: Notified to ATS on arrival
Usually done with ATC at landing
Prevents unnecessary SAR activation
Required by CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the difference between ETA and SARTIME. CAR 91
concept: Your Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) is when you reckon you'll land at your destination--it's in your flight plan and it's what you're aiming for. SARTIME is different; it's the time when search and rescue starts to get concerned if you haven't closed your flight plan. SARTIME is usually your ETA plus a buffer--typically 30 minutes. So if your ETA is 15:00 and SARTIME is 15:30, you've got a 30-minute window after your estimated arrival before SAR starts thinking something's wrong. If you're running late, you need to amend your ETA so SARTIME shifts forward. It's a safety mechanism to catch problems early.
exam_tip: ETA is your estimate, SARTIME is when ATS gets concerned. Know the typical SARTIME buffer.
key_facts: ETA is estimated time of arrival
SARTIME is when search and rescue action starts
SARTIME typically 30 minutes after ETA
Amending ETA shifts SARTIME forward

syllabus_desc: State the time search and rescue action would be initiated if a flight plan is not terminated before SARTIME. AIP ENR
concept: If you land and don't close your flight plan before SARTIME arrives, search and rescue action kicks off automatically. AIP ENR gives you the details, but basically, if you've reached SARTIME and ATS hasn't heard from you that you've landed, they treat you as overdue. They'll try to contact you through ATS or your operator, and if they can't get a response, they'll escalate to actual search and rescue coordination. This is why closing your flight plan is so critical--every time you don't do it, you're potentially triggering unnecessary SAR effort. The system exists to find missing aircraft, so respect it.
exam_tip: SARTIME is a fixed trigger. Know that SAR action begins automatically at SARTIME if flight plan isn't closed.
key_facts: SAR action initiated at SARTIME
Automatic process if plan not closed
Search and rescue resources activated
Can be expensive and disruptive if unnecessary

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 135 enroute limitations for two engine aeroplanes.
concept: Twin-engine aircraft operating under Part 135 have enroute limitations about how far they can go from a suitable aerodrome. You can't just cruise down the middle of nowhere if you've only got two engines--there's got to be an escape route. CAR Part 135 specifies limitations often referred to as ETOPS-style rules (even if you're not doing actual ETOPS). Basically, you can't be further from a suitable aerodrome than your aircraft can fly on one engine. This creates a corridor of safety--you're never so far out that an engine failure leaves you without options. The exact limitations depend on your aircraft performance and the terrain you're flying over.
exam_tip: Twin-engine enroute limitations are about distance from diversion aerodromes. Know the concept and constraints.
key_facts: Distance from suitable aerodrome limited
Single-engine performance determines max distance
Terrain and aerodrome availability affects planning
Part of overall flight safety envelope

syllabus_desc: Derive from operational publications, the required radio frequency for communicating with specified ATC units.
concept: You'll need to be across the AIP ENR tables and the sectional chart--they've got all the frequencies you need for talking to Auckland Centre, approach units, and tower frequencies at your destination. It's not something you memorise; instead, you develop the skill of quickly finding and using the right publication. If you're heading to Christchurch, you'll check the AIP AD for their specific frequencies. This forms the foundation of your radio work at the commercial level.
exam_tip: Know where to find frequencies in AIP ENR and AIP AD. Examiners want to see you can navigate publications, not recite numbers.
