key_facts: AIP ENR contains Centre and approach frequencies
AIP AD lists tower and ground frequencies for each aerodrome
Sectional charts include frequency information
Publications must be current and accessible in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, AIP ENR, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for making position reports to an ATS unit. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: When you're operating in controlled airspace or as directed by ATC, you need to give position reports at specified intervals or waypoints. The standard format includes your aircraft callsign, position, altitude, time, and destination. You're expected to deliver this information clearly and concisely on the frequency assigned--there's no padding or unnecessary chatter. In New Zealand's airspace, you'll typically report over visual reporting points or geographical features that are clearly marked on your chart.
exam_tip: Remember the standard position report format and when you're required to make them. CAR Part 91 outlines the obligations.
key_facts: Position reports required in controlled airspace as directed
Must include callsign, position, altitude, time, destination
Given over VRPs or geographical features
Maintain specified reporting intervals
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the content of a VFR position report. AIP ENR
concept: A VFR position report contains specific information in a standard order: your callsign, position (using a VRP or geographical feature), altitude, heading, and destination or intentions. You're not throwing information at ATC randomly--there's a format that controllers expect and understand. When you're cruising from Hamilton to Ardmore under VFR, you'd report your position over a readily identified landmark, your flight level, where you're heading. This discipline keeps you and everyone else organised on the frequency.
exam_tip: The order matters. Callsign, position, altitude, heading, destination--learn it in sequence.
key_facts: VFR position reports follow a standard format
Include callsign, position, altitude, heading, destination
Position identified using VRP or geographical feature
Reports maintain ATS situational awareness

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of Universal Communications Services (UNICOM). AIP GEN
concept: UNICOM provides a broadcast service at uncontrolled aerodromes where there's no ATC facility. It's typically staffed by the aerodrome operator and used for traffic information, weather updates, and aerodrome conditions. You'll use UNICOM to get the wind direction and strength, which runway's in use, and any hazards before you land. At places like Ardmore, UNICOM keeps everyone in the circuit informed and working safely, even though there's no formal air traffic control. It's your gateway to coordinating with other aircraft in the vicinity.
exam_tip: UNICOM = information service at uncontrolled aerodromes. It's not ATC, but it does coordinate traffic safely.
key_facts: UNICOM operates at uncontrolled aerodromes
Provides wind, runway, hazard information
Staffed by aerodrome operator
Improves safety through coordinated traffic information

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of an Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit (AFRU). AIP GEN
concept: An AFRU is an automated system at aerodromes that responds with pre-recorded messages containing aerodrome information when you key your radio. You might call up an AFRU to get the wind, QNH, and runway information without needing someone to answer. It's efficient and available 24/7 at participating aerodromes, reducing frequency congestion and giving you the information you need to plan your arrival safely. Think of it as an automated UNICOM that doesn't require a person on the ground.
exam_tip: AFRU = automated information service. You transmit, it responds automatically with pre-recorded info.
key_facts: AFRU provides automated aerodrome information
Pre-recorded responses include wind, QNH, runway
Available 24/7 at participating aerodromes
Reduces frequency congestion

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of Aerodrome and Weather Information Broadcasts (AWIB). AIP GEN
concept: AWIB is a continuous broadcast of aerodrome and weather information that you can tune into at participating aerodromes. Rather than asking for information, you listen to the periodic broadcast which includes wind, temperature, QNH, visibility, and significant weather. It's a passive service that keeps you informed without cluttering the frequency. At major aerodromes like Christchurch or Auckland, AWIB keeps everyone updated on conditions, freeing up radio time for operational traffic.
exam_tip: AWIB = continuous broadcast, you listen rather than ask. It's passive but timely information.
key_facts: AWIB broadcasts aerodrome and weather continuously
Includes wind, temperature, QNH, visibility
Operates at major aerodromes
Reduces radio congestion

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the various light signals from a control tower. CAR 91 & AIP AD
concept: If your radio fails, you need to understand tower light signals. A steady green light clears you to land; a flashing green means clear to taxi; a steady red means 'give way and continue circling'; a flashing red is an emergency signal and you need to land immediately without delay. These are standardised internationally but you'll find the complete table in the AIP GEN. You might never need them in normal operations, but every pilot needs to know them cold. They're your safety net when your comms go down on approach.
exam_tip: Memorise the light signal colours and patterns--steady green, flashing green, red lights. They're essential emergency knowledge.
key_facts: Steady green = clear to land
Flashing green = clear to taxi
Steady red = give way and continue circling
Flashing red = emergency landing required immediately
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the communications requirements when TIBA procedures are in force. AIP ENR
concept: TIBA stands for Traffic Information Broadcast by Aircraft -- it's a procedure used in areas where ATC services aren't available or have been disrupted. When TIBA procedures are in force (you'll find NOTAMs about it), you're required to broadcast your position, altitude, and intentions on a designated frequency at specified intervals. The idea is that without ATC providing separation, pilots need to build their own traffic picture by listening to and making these broadcasts. You'll typically encounter TIBA when flying through oceanic airspace or in areas where ATC coverage has been temporarily withdrawn. In New Zealand, TIBA procedures are detailed in AIP ENR.
exam_tip: TIBA = Traffic Information Broadcast by Aircraft. Remember it's used when ATC isn't available, and broadcasts include position, altitude, and intentions.
key_facts: TIBA stands for Traffic Information Broadcast by Aircraft
Used when ATC services are unavailable or disrupted
Pilots must broadcast position, altitude, and intentions at specified intervals
Details of TIBA procedures are published in AIP ENR and via NOTAM

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for complying with ATC clearances and instructions. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: You need an ATC clearance before you enter controlled airspace, and you need to understand what that clearance covers. It includes your route, altitude, and any restrictions the controller needs to impose. You must read back the clearance accurately--misheard details can create conflicts. The clearance is specific to your flight, your aircraft type, and your planned routing. You can't interpret a clearance creatively; you follow it exactly. When conditions change, you ask for an amended clearance. It's the contract between you and ATC that keeps the airspace safe.
exam_tip: Clearances aren't suggestions--they're mandatory. Know the procedure for obtaining and reading back clearances.
key_facts: Clearance required before entering controlled airspace
Includes route, altitude, restrictions
Must be read back accurately
Cannot be amended without requesting revision
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for coordinating with an aerodrome flight information service. CAR 91
concept: Controlled airspace exists to manage traffic density and safety. Before you enter a CTR (control zone) or CTA (control area), you must have an explicit clearance from ATC. You can't just wander in because you're VFR or because you think it's safe. The controller needs to know you're coming and where you're heading. Your clearance defines your path and altitude within that airspace. In New Zealand, Auckland's CTR and CTA are busy, and you'll get a specific routing. The clearance is the key to safe operations in this airspace--it's the controller's way of managing separation and flow.
exam_tip: No controlled airspace entry without ATC clearance, regardless of weather or aircraft type.
key_facts: Clearance mandatory before CTR/CTA entry
Controller specifies route and altitude
VFR clearance differs from IFR requirements
Read back must be accurate and complete
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for receiving an ATC clearance prior to entering various types of airspace, and ground manoeuvring area. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Before you enter controlled airspace or a ground manoeuvring area, you need an ATC clearance -- no exceptions. Under CAR 91, you must obtain a clearance before entering Class A, C, or D airspace. For the ground manoeuvring area at a controlled aerodrome, you need clearance before taxiing on any active runway or entering the manoeuvring area. The type of clearance depends on the airspace: Class C and D require a specific clearance with assigned conditions, while entering a CTR might come with altitude and routing restrictions. At places like Christchurch or Auckland, you'll get detailed clearances; at smaller controlled aerodromes, the process is simpler but the requirement is the same. No clearance, no entry.
exam_tip: Know which airspace classes require a clearance before entry (A, C, D) and remember that ground manoeuvring areas at controlled aerodromes also require clearance.
key_facts: ATC clearance required before entering Class A, C, or D controlled airspace
Clearance also required before entering the ground manoeuvring area at controlled aerodromes
Clearance conditions may include altitude, routing, and speed restrictions
CAR 91 and AIP ENR detail the specific requirements for each airspace class
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for receiving an ATC clearance prior to re-entering controlled airspace. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: If you've left controlled airspace -- whether intentionally or because your clearance limit was reached -- you need a fresh ATC clearance before re-entering. You can't just turn around and fly back in. This catches people out sometimes: you exit a CTR to the west, do some work in uncontrolled airspace, then want to come back through -- you've got to call ATC and get a new clearance before crossing that boundary again. The same principle applies if you've been cancelled out of controlled airspace due to traffic or weather. Contact ATC well before the boundary, state your intentions, and wait for the clearance. It's a legal requirement under CAR 91, not just good airmanship.
exam_tip: A previous clearance doesn't cover re-entry -- you need a new clearance every time you want to enter or re-enter controlled airspace.
key_facts: A new ATC clearance is required before re-entering controlled airspace
Previous clearances do not automatically cover re-entry
Contact ATC well before the airspace boundary to request re-entry
CAR 91 makes this a legal requirement, not optional
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the method of passing traffic information using the clock code.
concept: When you're VFR in controlled airspace, ATC provides traffic information but doesn't guarantee separation the way they do for IFR. You remain responsible for seeing and avoiding other traffic--it's still 'see and avoid'. ATC will tell you where traffic is, but you're still looking out the window. This is fundamental to VFR operations: you've got some help from ATC, but you're not handed over to autopilot mode. It's the hybrid arrangement that keeps VFR affordable but still safe when you stay alert and look properly.
exam_tip: VFR = traffic information only, not separation guarantee. See and avoid is your responsibility.
key_facts: ATC provides traffic information for VFR aircraft
Separation guarantee not provided
Pilot remains responsible for see and avoid
VFR in controlled airspace is a workload consideration

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where Air Traffic Control is responsible for the provision of separation between VFR, SVFR and IFR traffic. AIP ENR
concept: ATC's responsibility for separation depends on the airspace class and the type of traffic. In Class A airspace, ATC separates all traffic (though VFR isn't normally permitted there). In Class C, ATC provides separation between IFR and IFR, IFR and VFR, and IFR and SVFR -- but NOT between VFR and VFR. In Class D, ATC separates IFR from IFR and provides traffic information for VFR. SVFR flights in a CTR get separation from other SVFR and IFR traffic. The key thing to understand is that as a VFR pilot in Class C or D, you're responsible for your own separation from other VFR traffic -- ATC will give you traffic information, but you're the one who has to see and avoid.
exam_tip: This is a classic exam topic. Remember: in Class C, ATC separates IFR/IFR and IFR/VFR but NOT VFR/VFR. In Class D, ATC only separates IFR/IFR.
key_facts: Class C: ATC separates IFR/IFR, IFR/VFR, and IFR/SVFR -- not VFR/VFR
Class D: ATC separates IFR/IFR only; traffic info provided for VFR
SVFR in a CTR receives separation from other SVFR and IFR
VFR pilots are responsible for their own separation from other VFR traffic in C and D

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where the pilot-in-command is responsible for maintaining separation from other traffic. AIP ENR
concept: As PIC, you're responsible for maintaining separation from other traffic in several situations. In Class G (uncontrolled) airspace, it's entirely on you -- there's no ATC separation service. In Class C and D, you're responsible for separation from other VFR traffic (ATC provides traffic information, but the see-and-avoid responsibility is yours). In Class E, VFR pilots are responsible for separation from all other traffic. Even when ATC is providing a separation service, if you see conflicting traffic, you've still got a duty to take avoiding action if needed. The bottom line: unless ATC is explicitly providing separation between you and another aircraft, assume you're responsible. Keep your eyes outside.
exam_tip: In uncontrolled airspace (Class G), ALL separation is the pilot's responsibility. In controlled airspace, VFR/VFR separation is typically the pilot's job.
key_facts: PIC responsible for all separation in Class G uncontrolled airspace
In Class C and D, PIC responsible for VFR-to-VFR separation
In Class E, VFR pilots responsible for separation from all traffic
See-and-avoid responsibility applies even when ATC is providing traffic information

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal separation standards applied by ATC. AIP ENR
concept: ATC applies specific separation standards to keep aircraft safely apart. The normal radar separation standard in New Zealand is 5 NM laterally or 1,000 ft vertically below FL 245 (3 NM in certain terminal areas with appropriate radar capability). Non-radar separation uses time-based or distance-based standards -- for example, 10 minutes longitudinal separation for aircraft on the same track at the same level. Vertical separation below FL 245 is 1,000 ft; above FL 245, it's 2,000 ft (unless RVSM applies). These aren't arbitrary numbers -- they're calculated to account for navigation accuracy, radar precision, and aircraft performance. When ATC gives you a clearance, these are the minimum standards they're working to.
exam_tip: Key numbers: 5 NM radar separation (3 NM in some terminal areas), 1,000 ft vertical below FL 245, 2,000 ft above FL 245.
key_facts: Normal radar separation is 5 NM laterally (3 NM in some terminal areas)
Vertical separation: 1,000 ft below FL 245, 2,000 ft above FL 245
Non-radar longitudinal separation is typically 10 minutes on the same track
Standards are based on navigation accuracy and radar precision

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where the normal separation may be reduced. AIP ENR
concept: Normal separation standards can be reduced in certain situations. In terminal areas with precision radar (like Auckland or Christchurch), lateral separation may be reduced to 3 NM. Wake turbulence separation may be applied instead of standard distance-based separation when a light aircraft follows a heavy one. Visual separation can be applied when ATC can see both aircraft, or when one pilot reports the other in sight and can maintain separation -- ATC may then reduce below normal standards. Reduced separation may also apply during parallel runway operations. The point is that ATC doesn't reduce separation arbitrarily -- there's always an alternative safety mechanism in place.
exam_tip: Reduced separation typically applies in terminal areas with better radar, during visual approaches, or when wake turbulence spacing is used instead of standard separation.
key_facts: Terminal radar separation can be reduced to 3 NM in areas with precision radar
Visual separation may be applied when pilots have traffic in sight
Wake turbulence separation replaces standard separation behind heavy aircraft
ATC must have an alternative safety mechanism before reducing normal separation

syllabus_desc: State the wake turbulence separation requirements for light aircraft in non-radar environment. AIP AD
concept: Wake turbulence is a serious hazard, especially for light aircraft following heavier ones. In a non-radar environment (which covers most of New Zealand's uncontrolled aerodromes), you need to apply time-based wake turbulence separation yourself. A light aircraft following a medium aircraft should allow at least 2 minutes; following a heavy aircraft, at least 3 minutes. These minimums apply for takeoff from the same runway or a parallel runway less than 760 m apart. On approach, similar time spacing applies. At places like Ardmore where light trainers share the circuit with larger aircraft, being aware of wake turbulence timing is a practical daily skill, not just exam knowledge.
exam_tip: For non-radar operations: 2 minutes behind medium, 3 minutes behind heavy. These are MINIMUM times -- more is better, especially in calm conditions where vortices persist longer.
key_facts: Light behind medium: minimum 2 minutes separation in non-radar environment
Light behind heavy: minimum 3 minutes separation
Applies for takeoff from same or closely spaced parallel runways
Wake vortices persist longer in calm conditions -- extra spacing recommended

syllabus_desc: Describe the radar services available to VFR flights. AIP ENR
concept: If you're VFR in radar coverage, ATC can provide you with radar information--calling out traffic they can see on their screens. Some units offer radar vectors for planning purposes, but you're still VFR, which means weather minimums and see-and-avoid remain your responsibility. Radar doesn't replace your eyeballs. It's a tool that helps ATC guide you through airspace and warn you of traffic. In areas around Auckland and Christchurch where radar coverage exists, this service is valuable, but it doesn't change your VFR obligations.
exam_tip: Radar information is advisory. You still navigate and maintain VFR.
key_facts: Radar information available in coverage areas
Controllers call out identified traffic
Radar vectors can assist with routing
VFR minimums and see-and-avoid unchanged

syllabus_desc: Explain the altimeter setting requirements for flight under VFR. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Your altimeter must be set to the QNH (atmospheric pressure corrected to mean sea level) of the QNH zone you're in. New Zealand's divided into zones, and you must use the current QNH for your zone. Get it from ATIS, AWIB, or UNICOM before takeoff. If you cross zone boundaries, you update to the new zone's QNH. This is non-negotiable--wrong QNH means wrong altitude, which creates separation problems and potential conflicts. It's a basic airmanship skill that you'll do routinely, and the rules are clear in CAR Part 91.
exam_tip: Set QNH before takeoff, update when crossing zones. You'll get QNH from aerodrome information services.
key_facts: QNH must match your operating zone
Obtain from ATIS, AWIB, or UNICOM
Update when crossing zone boundaries
CAR Part 91 makes this mandatory
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the procedure to use to obtain an altimeter setting when QNH is not available prior to takeoff and the requirement to obtain a QNH once in flight. AIP ENR
concept: If QNH isn't available before takeoff--say you're at a remote strip--you can set your altimeter using the field elevation as a reference. You'll set the altimeter to read the known aerodrome elevation. Once airborne, you'll request QNH from the nearest ATS unit or UNICOM. You can't just guess; you need an official QNH. In practical terms, most NZ aerodromes have some form of information service, but this procedure covers those occasions when you're at a smaller strip without regular broadcasts. Get the official QNH as soon as practical after departure.
exam_tip: Set to field elevation pre-flight if QNH unavailable. Get official QNH once airborne ASAP.
key_facts: Altimeter set to field elevation if QNH unavailable
Request official QNH from ATS once airborne
Cannot operate indefinitely without proper QNH
Procedure maintains altitude awareness safety

syllabus_desc: Describe QNH zones and state when zone QNH should be used. AIP ENR
concept: New Zealand's airspace is divided into QNH zones, each with its own reference pressure setting. The zones follow geographical boundaries and are shown on your sectional charts and in the AIP ENR. You use the QNH for whichever zone your aircraft is in. The idea is that within a zone, all aircraft set the same QNH, so your altitude reading corresponds to everyone else's. When you cross a zone boundary, you update your altimeter to the new zone's QNH. It's straightforward in practice--you check the chart, see which zone you're in, and set accordingly. The AIP ENR gives you the current QNH for each zone.
exam_tip: Zones are geographic, shown on charts. Update your QNH when you change zones.
key_facts: QNH zones divide New Zealand airspace
Each zone has its assigned QNH
Zones shown on sectional charts and AIP ENR
Update QNH when crossing zone boundaries

syllabus_desc: Describe the transition altitude, layer and level. AIP ENR
concept: The transition altitude is the altitude below which you set your altimeter to QNH (local pressure) and above which you set it to 1013 hPa (the standard pressure setting). In New Zealand, the transition altitude varies by location but is typically around 5,000 feet. The transition layer is the airspace between the transition altitude and the transition level--you're in this space during climb or descent. The transition level is the flight level associated with the transition altitude when you've switched to 1013. Think of it as the handover point: below it, you're using local pressure; above it, you're using standard pressure. It prevents altitude errors during climb and descent through busy airspace.
exam_tip: Transition altitude uses QNH; transition level uses 1013 hPa. Know the NZ transition altitude for your area.
key_facts: Transition altitude = QNH setting boundary
Transition level = flight level at transition altitude
Transition layer = airspace between them
Prevents altitude errors in climb/descent

syllabus_desc: State the altitude requirements when cruising VFR within the New Zealand FIR. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: VFR cruising altitudes in the New Zealand FIR follow the cruising level table in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR. The rule is simple: if you're flying on a magnetic heading of 0-179 degrees, you cruise at odd thousands (1,500, 3,500, 5,500 feet); if you're flying 180-359 degrees, you cruise at even thousands (2,000, 4,000, 6,000 feet). This table minimises conflicts between aircraft flying in opposite directions. It's not a suggestion--it's a legal requirement unless ATC assigns something different for traffic management. The whole system relies on everyone following the table, so separation works without constant radio calls.
exam_tip: Know the cruising level table: odd for 0-179°, even for 180-359°. Unless ATC assigns otherwise.
key_facts: Odd thousands for magnetic heading 0-179°
Even thousands for magnetic heading 180-359°
Applies to all VFR cruising in NZ FIR
ATC can assign alternatives for traffic management
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe situations where ATC may assign cruising altitudes not in accordance with the VFR table of cruising altitudes. AIP ENR
concept: ATC has the authority to assign you a cruising altitude outside the standard VFR table when traffic demands it. If there's congestion, weather, or separation issues, they'll vector you up or down to manage the flow. They might assign you 2,500 feet when the table says 1,500 because other traffic's in your normal slot. When ATC issues an altitude clearance, it supersedes the standard table. You need to understand that the table is the default, but ATC's tactical needs sometimes require variation. It's always in your clearance, and you read it back to confirm.
exam_tip: ATC can assign non-standard altitudes. Follow your clearance, not the table, if they differ.
key_facts: ATC can assign altitudes outside VFR table
Traffic management reason for variations
Clearance supersedes the standard table
Always confirm assigned altitude in readback

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the operation of transponders within the New Zealand FIR. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Your transponder requirements depend on where you're flying. In transponder mandatory airspace (usually controlled airspace), you must have an operating Mode C transponder (altitude-reporting). In some areas, Mode A is acceptable. Below certain altitudes or in uncontrolled airspace, transponders might be optional or not required at all. The AIP ENR spells out the specific requirements for different airspace classes and areas around New Zealand. If you're crossing into Auckland's airspace, you'll need a transponder. If you're cruising at 2,000 feet over rural airspace away from major aerodromes, you might not. Check the specific requirements for your planned route.
exam_tip: Transponder requirements vary by airspace. Know the AIP ENR classification for your route.
key_facts: Transponder mandatory in controlled airspace
Mode C required where specified
Requirements vary by altitude and location
AIP ENR details specific airspace requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures required of pilots operating transponders. AIP ENR
concept: When you've not been assigned a specific transponder code by ATC, you squawk 7700 in an emergency, 7600 for communications failure, or 7500 for unlawful interference--but during normal VFR flight when no code's been assigned, you use 7000. Code 7000 is the default VFR code. ATC can see you on their screens without needing an individual code, but 7000 tells them you're a VFR aircraft at an unassigned code. Once you've been assigned a specific code--say 3421--you use that instead. The AIP ENR confirms that 7000 is your default when no assignment's been made.
exam_tip: 7000 is your default VFR code. Emergency codes (7700, 7600, 7500) are used only in those situations.
key_facts: 7000 = default VFR code unassigned
7700 = emergency
7600 = communications failure
7500 = unlawful interference

syllabus_desc: State the requirements and limitations on an aircraft operating under VFR in transponder mandatory airspace without an operating transponder. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: You cannot legally operate VFR in transponder mandatory airspace if your transponder isn't working. You'll need ATC clearance to deviate from this rule, and that clearance is rarely granted unless you're positioning the aircraft for maintenance or have a genuine operational reason. CAR Part 91 is clear: no transponder, no entry into the controlled airspace that requires it. Your alternatives are to fly around the airspace, climb above it if conditions allow, or route through a gap in the coverage. Equipment serviceability is your responsibility, and you can't just ignore a broken transponder and hope for the best.
exam_tip: No transponder = no entry to transponder mandatory airspace. Plan routes accordingly or get maintenance done.
key_facts: Transponder mandatory airspace requires working transponder
CAR Part 91 prohibits entry without it
ATC clearance rarely granted for deviation
Plan routes or schedule maintenance accordingly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the rules pertaining to operating VFR in the various classes of airspace. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: New Zealand airspace is classified into categories (Class A through G), and each class has specific VFR operating rules. Class A is IFR only--you can't fly VFR there. Classes B through D require varying degrees of ATC clearance and contact. Class E is controlled but you can operate VFR with appropriate separation. Classes F and G are uncontrolled airspace where VFR is the standard. The AIP ENR maps out which classes apply where, and you check this before flight planning. Your clearance request and procedures change depending on the class you're entering. Queenstown's airspace differs from Hamilton's, so you need to know the local classification.
exam_tip: Know the VFR operating rules for each airspace class. AIP ENR shows local classifications.
key_facts: Class A = IFR only
Classes B-D = progressively less restrictive
Class E = controlled, VFR permitted
Classes F-G = uncontrolled, VFR standard
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the vertical limits and purpose of control zones (CTR). CAR 71
concept: A control zone (CTR) is the airspace immediately surrounding a controlled aerodrome. Its purpose is to manage traffic safely around the aerodrome during approaches and departures. CTRs are Class D airspace in New Zealand, extending typically from the surface to around 3,000 feet above aerodrome elevation, though the exact limits are shown in the AIP AD for each aerodrome. You need an ATC clearance to enter a CTR, whether you're landing or just transiting. Auckland's CTR, for example, has specific boundaries shown on the sectional chart and AIP AD. The vertical and horizontal limits are defined precisely to ensure separation and orderly flow around the busy aerodrome.
exam_tip: CTRs are defined in AIP AD with exact vertical and horizontal boundaries. Know them for your destination aerodrome.
key_facts: CTR = controlled zone around aerodrome
Usually Class D airspace
Requires ATC clearance to enter
Vertical limits shown in AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the vertical limits and purpose of control areas (CTA). CAR 71
concept: A control area (CTA) is the controlled airspace above and around a CTR, extending higher and wider. It's Class C or D airspace and serves to manage traffic at altitude approaching or departing the aerodrome. Auckland CTA, for instance, extends from above the CTR up to considerable altitude. CTAs protect the approach and departure corridors, ensuring safe separation for aircraft climbing or descending into the busy aerodrome. You'll need clearance to enter a CTA, though the requirements vary slightly depending on the specific class. The AIP ENR and CAR Part 71 define the exact boundaries, and they're shown on sectional charts. Understanding CTAs is essential for flight planning around major centres like Auckland, Christchurch, and Wellington.
exam_tip: CTAs extend above CTRs. Both require clearance. Check AIP ENR for boundaries and class.
key_facts: CTA = controlled airspace above CTR
Extends higher and wider than CTR
Usually Class C or D
Requires ATC clearance for entry

syllabus_desc: State the status and conditions relating to flight in VFR transit lanes. AIP ENR
concept: VFR transit lanes are designated corridors that allow VFR aircraft to transit through controlled airspace without a full ATC clearance. They're typically created around busy aerodromes like Auckland to allow VFR traffic to pass through safely without clogging approach frequencies. When you fly through a VFR transit lane, you're still in controlled airspace, but you follow the specific routing, altitude, and procedures for that lane. You maintain VFR separation and alert lookout. The lane's boundaries and procedures are published in the AIP ENR. It's a practical compromise: VFR pilots get through the airspace, and ATC's workload is reduced because they're not handling every VFR clearance individually.
exam_tip: VFR transit lanes allow VFR flight through controlled airspace. Follow published procedures and altitudes.
key_facts: VFR transit lanes designated around major aerodromes
Allow VFR passage through controlled airspace
Specific routing and altitude requirements apply
Published in AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the status and purpose of a general aviation area (GAA). CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: A general aviation area (GAA) is a designated airspace reserved for general aviation operations, typically around smaller aerodromes or training areas. The purpose is to give GA operators a protected space where they can operate without competing with commercial or military traffic. Ardmore, for example, is a centre of general aviation. GAAs are usually uncontrolled or lightly controlled, and the rules are more relaxed than in busy CTRs. They're shown on your chart and described in the AIP ENR and CAR Part 91. Operating in a GAA lets you train, conduct local flights, and build experience in a relatively straightforward environment, though you still need to follow the basic VFR rules and be aware of other traffic.
exam_tip: GAAs protect general aviation from commercial congestion. Rules are lighter than CTRs.
key_facts: GAA = airspace reserved for general aviation
Usually uncontrolled or lightly controlled
Examples include Ardmore
Published in AIP ENR and CAR Part 91
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe visual reporting points.
concept: Visual reporting points are easily identifiable landmarks on the ground--distinctive terrain features, towns, roads, or buildings--that pilots and ATC use as position references. Instead of giving a lat/long coordinate, you'd say 'two miles north of Christchurch' or 'overhead the railway junction at Hamilton'. VRPs are chosen because they're unmistakable from the air and shown on sectional charts. They make position reports efficient and memorable. When you're filing a flight plan or reporting to ATC, you reference VRPs. They're essential to navigation in NZ's airspace because they're quick to spot, unambiguous, and don't require technology--just good chart work and observation. Learning to identify VRPs develops your situational awareness and gives you reliable position references.
exam_tip: VRPs are chart-marked landmarks for position reference. Learn them for your regular areas.
key_facts: VRPs are distinctive ground landmarks
Used for position reporting and navigation
Shown on sectional charts
Reduce ambiguity in position reporting

syllabus_desc: Describe the status of controlled airspace when ATC go off duty. AIP GEN
concept: When ATC go off duty at an aerodrome, the controlled airspace--the CTR and CTA--doesn't disappear; it changes status. The airspace reverts to uncontrolled status, often becoming Class G. You can still operate in it, but without ATC separation or clearance requirements. You're back to see-and-avoid in uncontrolled airspace. The change happens at a published time, and you'll find it in the AIP GEN. This is important for your flight planning: if you're arriving at a smaller controlled aerodrome late in the day, check whether ATC will be closed. You might need to plan for uncontrolled arrival or wait until ATC reopens. It affects your procedures and planning, so knowing the status is essential.
exam_tip: When ATC closes, controlled airspace becomes uncontrolled. Check operating hours in flight planning.
key_facts: CTR/CTA becomes uncontrolled when ATC off duty
Published times in AIP GEN
No clearance required in uncontrolled status
Affects flight planning for late arrivals

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on operating an aircraft in a restricted area. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Restricted areas are airspace where operations are restricted for specific reasons--often military training, weapons ranges, or sensitive activities. You cannot enter a restricted area without explicit permission from the controlling authority, which is usually Defence Force or the relevant agency. The areas are clearly marked on your chart and listed in the AIP ENR. Trying to wander into a restricted area without clearance is illegal and potentially dangerous. Before flight planning through an area where you suspect restriction, check the AIP ENR. If there's a restricted area between you and your destination, you plan a route around it or request access. It's about respecting national security and safety protocols.
exam_tip: Restricted areas require explicit permission to enter. Plan around them or request clearance well in advance.
key_facts: Restricted areas require explicit permission
Marked on chart and listed in AIP ENR
Usually Defence Force or military operations
Plan routes to avoid or request clearance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on operating an aircraft in a military operating area (MOA). CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Military operating areas are airspace where military training and exercises take place. They're less restrictive than restricted areas--you can usually enter an MOA, but you need to be aware of the activity and use caution. Military traffic operating there isn't bound to civilian VFR rules, so they might be manoeuvring aggressively. The AIP ENR shows which MOAs are active and their activation times. Before routing through an MOA, check whether it's active. If it is, expect fast military traffic and be especially vigilant. You might request clearance through, but you're entering airspace where military operations take priority. It's about situational awareness: you know what you're flying into and can take appropriate precautions.
exam_tip: MOAs are for military training. Check activation status in AIP ENR and be extra vigilant for fast traffic.
key_facts: MOAs allow military training and exercises
Usually permeable but active when in use
Military traffic not bound to VFR rules
Check activation status in AIP ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a mandatory broadcast zone (MBZ). CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: A mandatory broadcast zone is uncontrolled airspace where you're required to broadcast your position and intentions regularly to alert other traffic. You're not talking to ATC; you're broadcasting on a nominated frequency (typically UNICOM) so that other pilots in the area know you're there. There's no clearance requirement, but the broadcast obligation is non-negotiable. It's a safety mechanism in busy uncontrolled areas. The frequency and procedures are in the AIP ENR. Think of it as organised see-and-avoid: you tell everyone where you are, they tell you where they are, and everyone's situational awareness improves. It's common around training areas and smaller aerodromes where several operators converge.
exam_tip: MBZ requires position broadcasts on nominated frequency. No ATC, but broadcasting is mandatory.
key_facts: MBZ = uncontrolled airspace with broadcast requirement
Position broadcasts on nominated frequency
Usually UNICOM or CTAF frequency
Improves situational awareness for all traffic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a volcanic hazard zone (VHZ). CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Volcanic hazard zones are airspace above or near volcanoes where volcanic ash and gases pose a risk to aircraft. New Zealand has several active volcanoes, and the airspace around them is designated as VHZ. You're not prohibited from flying through, but you need awareness of the hazard. If volcanic activity's occurring, ash clouds can damage engines and reduce visibility dramatically. The AIP ENR will indicate active hazards and recommended altitudes to avoid. Ash can extend to high altitude, so even if you're climbing through the zone, you're conscious of the risk. It's about informed decision-making: you know the hazard exists, you check the current status, and you take appropriate action--either routing around or accepting the risk if conditions are benign.
exam_tip: VHZ around active volcanoes. Check AIP ENR for current activity and hazard information.
key_facts: VHZ mark airspace above/near volcanoes
Volcanic ash and gases are hazards
Not prohibited but awareness required
Check current activity status in AIP ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a danger area. CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Danger areas are designated airspace where hazardous activities take place--explosions, weapon testing, parachute drops--creating risk to transiting aircraft. You can usually fly through a danger area, but you need to know it's active and exercise caution. The AIP ENR shows danger areas, their extent, and activation times. Before routing through one, check the status. If it's active, you might choose to route around or, if the hazard's acceptable, transit at your own risk with heightened awareness. It's another example of informed decision-making: you know the hazard, you know when it's active, and you make your decision accordingly. Danger areas are taken seriously--they exist because there's genuine hazard.
exam_tip: Danger areas mark hazardous activities. Check activation in AIP ENR before routing through.
key_facts: Danger areas mark explosions, tests, drops
Usually passable but hazardous when active
Shown in AIP ENR with activation times
Requires situational awareness and decision-making
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a designated low flying zone (LFZ). CAR 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Low flying zones are designated airspace where military and specialised operators conduct low-altitude training and operations. Civil aircraft can usually operate in them, but you're aware that fast military jets and helicopters conducting tactical manoeuvres are present. The operating considerations are straightforward: expect aggressive manoeuvring, maintain excellent lookout, and be prepared to give way. LFZs are marked on your chart and described in the AIP ENR. They're not restricted in the sense you can't enter, but they're busy, hazardous places where military operations dominate. Your approach is cautious and aware--you're in military training airspace, and you respect that.
exam_tip: LFZ = low-altitude military training airspace. Expect aggressive manoeuvring. Stay alert and clear if needed.
key_facts: LFZ for military low-altitude training
Civil aircraft can usually operate
Expect fast, tactical manoeuvring
Marked on chart and in AIP ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a common frequency zone (CFZ). AIP ENR
concept: A Common Frequency Zone (CFZ) is an area of uncontrolled airspace where all aircraft are required to monitor and broadcast on a common frequency. You'll find CFZs around many NZ aerodromes that don't have a control tower -- places like Tauranga, Nelson, or Whanganui during certain hours. The operating considerations are straightforward: you must monitor the CFZ frequency, make appropriate broadcasts (joining, circuit calls, departing), and maintain a listening watch for other traffic. The frequency is published in AIP NZ Volume 4 for each aerodrome. The whole point is traffic awareness -- without ATC providing a service, everyone needs to talk and listen to build a shared picture of who's where.
exam_tip: CFZs require monitoring AND broadcasting on the published frequency. Know where to find the frequency (AIP NZ Volume 4) and what broadcasts are expected.
key_facts: CFZ requires all aircraft to monitor and broadcast on a common frequency
Found around uncontrolled aerodromes throughout New Zealand
Published frequencies are in AIP NZ Volume 4
Broadcasts include joining, circuit, and departure calls for traffic awareness

syllabus_desc: State the operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft over or close to temporary hazards/airspace. AIP ENR
concept: Temporary hazards and temporary restricted airspace pop up regularly through NOTAMs. These include things like firefighting operations, military exercises, search and rescue zones, air shows, or even volcanic activity. When operating near these areas, you need to check the current NOTAMs before flight and plan your route accordingly. If a temporary restricted area is active, you must not enter without authorisation. For temporary hazard areas, you may be permitted to transit but need to exercise additional caution and maintain communications. In New Zealand, temporary restrictions are common around volcanic zones (like the Tongariro area) and during events like the Warbirds Over Wanaka show.
exam_tip: Always check NOTAMs for temporary hazards before flight. Temporary restricted areas have the same legal force as permanent ones while they're active.
key_facts: Temporary hazards and airspace restrictions are published via NOTAM
Must check NOTAMs as part of pre-flight planning
Temporary restricted airspace has the same legal force as permanent restricted areas
Common NZ examples include volcanic zones, firefighting, and air show areas

syllabus_desc: Interpret airspace information on aeronautical charts used for VFR flights.
concept: Being able to read airspace information on VFR charts is a fundamental flying skill. On NZ Visual Navigation Charts (VNCs), controlled airspace is depicted with blue boundaries for CTRs and magenta for CTAs, with vertical limits shown at the boundary. Restricted areas are marked with red hatching, MOAs with green, and danger areas with orange. You'll also see MBZ boundaries, GAA areas, CFZ rings, and transit lanes. The key is understanding the symbology: each colour and boundary style means something specific. Check the chart legend, and remember that charts get updated -- an out-of-date chart could show airspace that no longer exists or miss new restrictions.
exam_tip: Know the colour coding: blue for CTR, magenta for CTA, red for restricted, green for MOA. Always check the chart effective date before use.
key_facts: VNC uses blue for CTR boundaries and magenta for CTA boundaries
Restricted areas shown with red hatching, MOAs with green
Vertical limits are displayed at airspace boundaries
Charts must be current -- always check the effective date
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, VNC legend, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations on the use of a place as an aerodrome or heliport. CAR 91
concept: AIP NZAD (Volume 4 of the Aeronautical Information Publication) is your encyclopaedia for aerodrome information. It lists every controlled and licensed aerodrome in New Zealand with runway dimensions, approaches, services, frequencies, and procedures. You derive runway lengths, surface types, available services (fuel, maintenance, accommodation), and operating limitations. For example, Christchurch's entry shows multiple runways, each with specific dimensions and surface types. Auckland lists extensive approach procedures. Smaller aerodromes like Ardmore or Hamilton have simpler entries but still include essential information. You consult AIP AD for every aerodrome you intend to use--there's no guessing. It's your primary source for operationally relevant aerodrome data.
exam_tip: AIP AD is your aerodrome bible. Know how to find runway info, services, and operational notes quickly.
key_facts: AIP AD (Vol 4) contains all NZ aerodromes
Includes runway dimensions, surfaces, approaches
Lists services and operating limitations
Essential preflight planning reference

syllabus_desc: Describe the method of runway designation. AIP AD
concept: Aerodrome reference codes describe the aerodrome's physical characteristics--runway length, width, and pavement strength--relative to aircraft categories they can accommodate. A code like '4E' indicates capability for larger, heavier aircraft; code '2A' is smaller. The code's significance is that it tells you immediately whether your aircraft can use that aerodrome. A light training aircraft like a Cessna can operate from a 1A-coded strip; a regional jet needs at least code 3C. The AIP AD gives the code for each aerodrome. It's a shorthand system that lets you quickly assess aerodrome suitability during flight planning. Once you understand the code, you can match your aircraft type to aerodrome capabilities without reading lengthy technical descriptions.
exam_tip: Aerodrome codes indicate pavement capability and runway length. Match your aircraft to the code.
key_facts: Codes describe runway capacity and pavement strength
First character = runway length category
Second character = pavement strength
Codes allow quick aerodrome suitability assessment

syllabus_desc: Describe the movement area of an aerodrome. CAR 1
concept: Aerodrome signage keeps you oriented and safe on the ground. Runway designators show magnetic heading (e.g., runway 20 aligns roughly on 200 degrees magnetic). Taxiway letters identify each route (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie). Directional signs show which taxiway you're on and where others lead. Distance and turning signs assist with navigation from runway to apron. The AIP AD includes signage descriptions for each aerodrome, and the visual approach slope indicators (PAPIs, VASIS) guide your descent. You need to identify these during taxi, particularly at unfamiliar aerodromes. Poor signage awareness leads to wrong turns and runway confusions. At Christchurch or Auckland, signage is comprehensive; at smaller strips, it's minimal but still present. Learning to identify signage quickly becomes second nature but requires conscious effort initially.
exam_tip: Runway numbers = magnetic heading. Taxiway letters = Alpha, Bravo, etc. Learn standard signage patterns.
key_facts: Runway designators show magnetic heading
Taxiway letters in sequence
Directional signs aid navigation
Signage critical at unfamiliar aerodromes

syllabus_desc: Describe the meaning of the various aerodrome ground signals.
concept: Pavement markings serve multiple purposes: runway edge lines show you're aligned with the paved surface; threshold markings indicate where the runway officially begins; runway designation numbers appear at both ends; touchdown zone markings show the optimal landing area; and centre line dashing guides your track. Taxiway markings--yellow edge lines and centre dashes--keep you on taxiway surfaces. Closed runway markings (X pattern) tell you that runway's unavailable. Blast pad markings show where jet blast can reach. The AIP AD describes these for specific aerodromes. Understanding markings is essential for safe ground operations: you don't land outside the paved surface, you don't taxi across runway thresholds, you don't use closed runways. The markings are your ground-level guidance system.
exam_tip: Runway markings guide landing and alignment. Taxiway markings keep you on surface. Learn standard patterns.
key_facts: Runway edge and centre markings guide alignment
Threshold markings show runway start
Designation numbers appear at both ends
Taxiway yellow markings keep you on surface

syllabus_desc: Interpret information on aerodrome/heliport charts. AIP GEN & AIP Volume 4
concept: Aerodrome and heliport charts in AIP NZ Volume 4 contain a wealth of operational information. You'll find runway lengths, widths, and surfaces; approach and departure procedures; circuit directions and altitudes; obstacle information; communications frequencies; and lighting availability. For heliports, charts show FATO and TLOF dimensions, approach and departure paths, and any special procedures. The skill here is being able to extract the information you need quickly -- before arriving at an unfamiliar aerodrome, you should study the chart to understand the layout, identify potential hazards, and know the local procedures. At somewhere like Queenstown, the chart information about approach paths and terrain is critical for safe operations.
exam_tip: Be able to extract key information from an aerodrome chart: runway data, circuit direction, frequencies, and obstacle information. Practice with real charts.
key_facts: AIP NZ Volume 4 contains aerodrome and heliport charts for all NZ aerodromes
Charts show runway data, circuit procedures, frequencies, obstacles, and lighting
Heliport charts include FATO/TLOF dimensions and special procedures
Study unfamiliar aerodrome charts thoroughly before arrival
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, AIP GEN, AIP Volume 4

syllabus_desc: Interpret runway, taxiway, apron, and stand signs and markings.
concept: Aerodrome signs and markings follow ICAO standards, and you need to be able to read them quickly -- especially when taxiing at an unfamiliar aerodrome. Mandatory instruction signs have white text on a red background and must be obeyed (runway holding positions, no-entry signs). Information signs have yellow text on a black background (location, direction, destination). Runway markings include centrelines, threshold bars, touchdown zones, and aiming points. Taxiway centrelines are yellow, and holding position markings consist of two solid and two dashed yellow lines. Apron markings guide you to parking stands. At larger NZ aerodromes like Auckland or Christchurch, there's quite a bit of signage to navigate; at smaller strips, you might only see the basics.
exam_tip: Red background = mandatory (must obey). Yellow on black = information. Know the holding position marking: two solid lines, two dashed lines.
key_facts: Mandatory signs: white text on red background (runway holding, no entry)
Information signs: yellow text on black background (location, direction)
Taxiway centrelines are yellow; runway centrelines are white
Holding position marking: two solid and two dashed yellow lines across the taxiway

syllabus_desc: Describe the lighting intensity classifications.
concept: Visual approach slope indicators help you maintain the correct descent angle on final approach. PAPIs (Precision Approach Path Indicators) use four lights that show white if you're on the correct glideslope, red if you're low, or a mix if you're slightly off. VASIS (Visual Approach Slope Indicator System) uses a similar principle with more lights arranged in bars. T-VASIS is a simplified two-bar version used at smaller aerodromes. All three work on the same principle: looking at the colour pattern tells you your vertical position relative to the ideal descent path. Major NZ aerodromes like Auckland and Christchurch use PAPIs or VASIS; smaller fields like Hamilton might have T-VASIS or none at all. Learning to interpret these indicators quickly prevents approaches that are too steep or too shallow. They're significant safety tools, particularly in marginal visibility.
exam_tip: White over white = on slope. Know PAPI, VASIS, T-VASIS patterns. They're your descent guide.
key_facts: PAPIs use four lights, white = on slope
VASIS uses multiple lights in bars
T-VASIS simplified two-bar system
Help maintain safe descent angle on final

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting systems: (a) Runway edge lighting (REDL); (b) Runway landing threshold lighting (RTHL); (c) Runway end lighting (RENL); (d) Runway centreline lighting system (RCLL); (e) Runway end identifier lighting (REIL); (f) Circling guidance lighting (CGL); (g) Runway lead in lighting (RLLS); (h) Pilot activated lighting (PAL); and (i) Precision approach path indicators (PAPI).
concept: Aerodrome lighting systems each serve a specific purpose. REDL (runway edge lights) are white, turning yellow in the last 600 m and red at the end. RTHL (threshold lights) are green, marking where the runway begins. RENL (runway end lights) are red, visible only from the approach end. RCLL (centreline lights) help you stay on track in poor visibility. REIL (runway end identifier lights) are flashing white strobes that help you spot the threshold from a distance. CGL (circling guidance lights) assist in visual circling approaches at night. RLLS (lead-in lights) guide you from the approach path to the runway. PAL (pilot activated lighting) lets you switch on the lights at unattended aerodromes using your radio -- common at NZ regional strips. PAPIs give you glide slope guidance with red and white light combinations.
exam_tip: Know the colours: threshold = green, runway end = red, edge lights turn from white to yellow to red. PAL activation by radio is common at NZ regional aerodromes.
key_facts: REDL: white edge lights, yellow in last 600 m, red at runway end
RTHL: green threshold lights; RENL: red runway end lights
PAL: pilot activated by radio transmissions -- common at NZ regional aerodromes
PAPI: red/white light combinations for glide slope guidance

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway edge lighting (REDL).
concept: Runway edge lighting (REDL) consists of white lights along both edges of the runway. In the last 600 metres (or last third of the runway if shorter than 1800 m), the lights change to yellow as a caution that the runway end is approaching. At the runway end itself, the lights are red when viewed from the approach direction.
exam_tip: REDL colour sequence: white for most of the runway, yellow in the last 600 m, red at the end. The colour change gives you distance-remaining cues at night.
key_facts: White lights along both runway edges
Yellow in last 600 m (or last third)
Red at runway end from approach direction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway landing threshold lighting (RTHL).
concept: Runway landing threshold lighting (RTHL) consists of green lights that mark the beginning of the runway available for landing. They're located at the threshold and are visible to approaching aircraft.
exam_tip: RTHL = green lights at the threshold. Green means 'go' -- this is where your landing surface begins.
key_facts: Green lights at the runway threshold
Marks beginning of runway available for landing
Visible to approaching aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway end lighting (RENL).
concept: Runway end lighting (RENL) consists of red lights at the end of the runway. They mark the end of the runway available for use and are visible to aircraft on the runway.
exam_tip: RENL = red lights at the runway end. Red means stop -- you've run out of runway. Visible from the runway, not from the approach.
key_facts: Red lights at the runway end
Marks end of usable runway
Visible to aircraft on the runway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway centreline lighting system (RCLL).
concept: Runway centreline lighting system (RCLL) provides lights embedded in the runway surface along the centreline. The lights are white until the last 900 m, then alternate red and white until the last 300 m, where they become all red. This gives the pilot progressive distance-to-go information.
exam_tip: RCLL colour sequence: white, then alternating red/white in last 900 m, then all red in last 300 m. Typically only at major aerodromes with precision approaches.
key_facts: Lights embedded in runway centreline
White, then alternating red/white (last 900 m), then all red (last 300 m)
Provides distance-to-go information
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway end identifier lighting (REIL).
concept: Runway end identifier lighting (REIL) consists of a pair of synchronised flashing white lights, one on each side of the runway threshold. They help pilots identify the runway end during approach, particularly useful when the runway environment is difficult to distinguish from surrounding terrain or lighting.
exam_tip: REIL = pair of synchronised flashing white lights at the threshold. They help you find the runway end when the environment is visually confusing.
key_facts: Pair of synchronised flashing white lights
Located one each side of the threshold
Aids runway identification in visually confusing environments
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Circling guidance lighting (CGL).
concept: Circling guidance lighting (CGL) is a system of lights designed to provide visual guidance to pilots conducting a circling approach, particularly when the runway and aerodrome environment may be difficult to identify visually. The lights help maintain orientation during the circling manoeuvre.
exam_tip: CGL helps you keep the runway environment in sight during a circling approach -- particularly useful at night or in reduced visibility.
key_facts: Provides visual guidance during circling approaches
Helps maintain orientation during circling manoeuvre
Useful at night or reduced visibility
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway lead in lighting (RLLS).
concept: Runway lead in lighting (RLLS) is a system of lights extending outward from the runway threshold along the approach path. It guides the pilot from the approach environment to the runway, providing a visual lead-in to the threshold.
exam_tip: RLLS extends outward from the threshold along the approach path. Think of it as a light trail leading you to the runway.
key_facts: Lights extending outward from runway threshold
Along the approach path
Guides pilot from approach environment to runway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Pilot activated lighting (PAL).
concept: Pilot activated lighting (PAL) is a lighting system that pilots can activate by radio transmissions on the appropriate frequency. Common at smaller NZ regional aerodromes where it's not economical to leave lights on all night. Typically activated by a series of microphone clicks.
exam_tip: PAL = lights you turn on yourself by keying the mic. Very common at NZ regional aerodromes. Check the AIPNZ Vol 4 entry for each aerodrome for the specific activation frequency and procedure.
key_facts: Pilot-activated by radio transmissions
Common at NZ regional aerodromes
Typically activated by microphone clicks
Check AIPNZ Vol 4 for specific procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Precision approach path indicators (PAPI).
concept: Precision approach path indicators (PAPI) consist of a row of four light units positioned beside the runway, usually on the left side. Each unit shows either red or white depending on whether the aircraft is above or below the correct glide path. On the correct glide slope, you see two red and two white lights.
exam_tip: PAPI: 4 lights beside the runway. On slope = 2 red + 2 white. Too high = more white. Too low = more red. 'Red over white, you're alright' doesn't apply to PAPI -- that's VASIS. For PAPI, think 2+2.
key_facts: Four light units beside the runway
On slope: 2 red + 2 white
Too high: 3 or 4 white
Too low: 3 or 4 red
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe aerodrome beacons.
concept: Aerodrome beacons are rotating or flashing lights used to identify an aerodrome from a distance, particularly useful at night or in reduced visibility. In New Zealand, the standard aerodrome beacon flashes white and green -- the green identifies it as a land aerodrome (as opposed to a water aerodrome, which would flash white and yellow). Military aerodromes may use a different flash pattern. The beacon is typically mounted on a tall structure near the aerodrome and is visible from a considerable distance. Not all NZ aerodromes have beacons -- check AIP NZ Volume 4 for the lighting available at your destination. When you spot that flashing green and white at night, you know you've found your aerodrome.
exam_tip: Standard NZ aerodrome beacon: alternating white and green flashes. Green = land aerodrome. Not all aerodromes have beacons -- check AIP Volume 4.
key_facts: Standard aerodrome beacon flashes alternating white and green
Green identifies a land aerodrome; yellow would indicate a water aerodrome
Not all NZ aerodromes have beacons installed
Beacon availability is published in AIP NZ Volume 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the indication of above, on and below slope for: (a) PAPIs; (b) VASIS; and (c) T-VASIS.
concept: Visual approach slope indicators tell you whether you're on the correct glide path by using combinations of red and white lights. For PAPIs (the most common system in NZ), four light bars are arranged beside the runway: all white means you're too high, three white one red means slightly high, two white two red is on slope, one white three red is slightly low, and all red means too low -- get back up. VASIS uses two bars: the far bar shows white when on or above slope, and the near bar shows red when on or below -- on slope gives you red over white (red over white, you're alright). T-VASIS uses a central bar and fly-up/fly-down lights above and below. The saying 'red over white, you're alright' works for VASIS -- but for PAPIs, think '2 and 2 is where you want to be'.
exam_tip: PAPIs: 2 red + 2 white = on slope. VASIS: red over white = on slope ('red over white, you're alright'). All red = too low in both systems.
key_facts: PAPI on slope: 2 white + 2 red (reading from left to right)
PAPI too low: 3 or 4 red lights; too high: 3 or 4 white lights
VASIS on slope: red over white ('red over white, you're alright')
T-VASIS uses fly-up and fly-down indicators above and below a central bar

syllabus_desc: Describe the indication of above, on and below slope for: PAPIs.
concept: For PAPIs, the four light units show combinations of red and white to indicate your position relative to the correct glide path. On the correct slope: 2 white (far) + 2 red (near). Slightly high: 3 white + 1 red. Too high: 4 white. Slightly low: 1 white + 3 red. Too low: 4 red.
exam_tip: PAPI on slope = 2 white + 2 red. Memory aid: equal white and red means you're where you should be. More white = too high, more red = too low.
key_facts: On slope: 2 white + 2 red
Slightly high: 3 white + 1 red; too high: 4 white
Slightly low: 1 white + 3 red; too low: 4 red
Lights viewed from left to right
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the indication of above, on and below slope for: VASIS.
concept: For VASIS (Visual Approach Slope Indicator System), two bars of lights are used -- an upwind bar and a downwind bar. On the correct slope: the far (upwind) bar shows white and the near (downwind) bar shows red -- 'red over white, you're alright.' Too high: both bars show white. Too low: both bars show red.
exam_tip: VASIS: 'red over white, you're alright' -- the classic mnemonic. Near bar red, far bar white = on slope. Both white = high. Both red = low (and dangerous).
key_facts: On slope: far bar white, near bar red ('red over white, you're alright')
Too high: both bars white ('white over white, you'll fly all night')
Too low: both bars red ('red over red, you're dead')
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the indication of above, on and below slope for: T-VASIS.
concept: T-VASIS uses a central bar of lights (the wing bar) with fly-up and fly-down indicator lights above and below. On the correct slope: only the wing bar is visible. Above slope: fly-up indicators appear above the wing bar (the more lights, the further above). Below slope: fly-down indicators appear below the wing bar.
exam_tip: T-VASIS: on slope, you see only the wing bar. Lights above = you're high (fly down). Lights below = you're low (fly up). The more lights, the further off slope you are.
key_facts: On slope: only wing bar visible
Above slope: fly-up lights appear above wing bar
Below slope: fly-down lights appear below wing bar
Number of lights indicates how far off slope
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for the notification of accidents. CAR 12
concept: If you're involved in an aircraft accident, you must notify the appropriate authorities immediately--typically the NZCAR investigators or Police, depending on severity. CAR Part 12 outlines who must be notified and the timeframe. Accidents are reported promptly; there's no discretion here. The purpose is incident investigation and safety improvement. You cooperate with investigators, provide statements, and preserve evidence. The definition of 'accident' under CAR Part 12 includes incidents involving serious injury, death, or substantial aircraft damage. Even if you're shaken, you report it. Operators have specific obligations too. Your role as pilot is to notify authorities and cooperate fully with investigation.
exam_tip: Accidents reported immediately to authorities under CAR Part 12. Cooperation with investigators is mandatory.
key_facts: Accident = serious injury, death, or substantial damage
Immediate notification required
CAR Part 12 outlines obligations
Pilot must cooperate with investigation

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for the notification of incidents. CAR 12
concept: Incidents--situations that don't result in injury or significant damage but involve safety concerns--must also be reported under CAR Part 12. These include engine problems, near-misses, structural issues, or medical events. The reporting threshold is lower than accidents, but the requirement's clear. Operators file incident reports; pilots provide information. The system exists to identify trends and prevent incidents from escalating into accidents. You're encouraged to report honestly without fear of punishment--the focus is safety improvement, not blame. A confidential reporting system supports this culture. Whether it's a minor technical issue or a close call in the air, reporting it contributes to safety knowledge across the community.
exam_tip: Incidents include safety concerns without injury. Report under CAR Part 12. Reporting is safety-focused, not punitive.
key_facts: Incident = safety concern without injury or damage
Must be reported under CAR Part 12
System focuses on safety improvement
Honest reporting encouraged

syllabus_desc: State the extent to which a pilot may deviate from the CA Act or rules in an emergency situation. CA Act 2023, section 15
concept: In a genuine emergency where safety's compromised, you have authority to deviate from the Civil Aviation Act and rules. This isn't a blank cheque--it's limited to what's necessary to resolve the emergency. If your engine's failing, you might descend below the minimum safe altitude to reach the nearest aerodrome. If your communications fail, you might deviate from your cleared flight path. The law recognises that rigid rule-following in an emergency can increase risk. Section 15(1) of the CA Act 2023 and sections 16(1) and (2) spell this out. The key is that the deviation must be genuinely necessary for safety, not a convenience or preference. Once the emergency's resolved, normal rules apply. The CAA recognises this principle, and pilots act in good faith within the emergency authority.
exam_tip: Emergency authority exists but is limited to genuine safety situations. Deviation must be necessary, not convenient.
key_facts: Emergency authority in CA Act section 15(1)
Limited to genuine safety situations
Deviation only as necessary
Normal rules apply once emergency resolved
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 sections 15(1), 16(1)&(2)

syllabus_desc: State the pilot action required following deviation from the CA Act or rules in an emergency situation. CA Act 2023, section 15
concept: After you've deviated from the rules in an emergency, sections 15(2) and 16(3) of the CA Act require you to report the deviation. You notify the CAA (or CAANZ, the relevant authority) as soon as practicable after the flight--usually within 24 hours. This notification isn't about punishment; it's about record-keeping and demonstrating that the deviation was genuinely necessary. The CAA uses this information to understand emergency situations and improve rules or training as needed. Your report should explain the emergency, what you did, why it was necessary, and the outcome. Honesty and timeliness matter. The system trusts that pilots will report truthfully, and that trust is foundational to the entire safety framework.
exam_tip: Report emergency deviations to CAA within 24 hours. Explain the situation and why deviation was necessary.
key_facts: Report required within 24 hours of incident
Notification to CAA or relevant authority
Report explains emergency and necessity
System based on pilot honesty and trust
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, CA Act 2023 sections 15(2), 16(3)

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate an emergency condition. AIP ENR
concept: When you're facing a genuine emergency--engine failure, structural damage, medical event--you set your transponder to 7700. This code immediately alerts ATC that you're in distress. Controllers see the code on radar and know to prioritise your situation. You'd also declare the emergency on radio: 'Mayday, Mayday, Mayday' for life-threatening situations, or 'Pan Pan' for urgent but non-life-threatening situations. The transponder code 7700 works with your radio declaration to ensure maximum visibility and assistance. Even if you're not in radio contact or communications are degraded, the transponder code alerts ATC to your situation. It's one of the most critical transponder codes, and every pilot must know it.
exam_tip: 7700 = emergency. Set it and declare on radio simultaneously. Both actions essential.
key_facts: 7700 = emergency condition
Alerts ATC immediately on radar
Set simultaneously with radio declaration
Works with Mayday or Pan Pan call

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate a loss of communications. AIP ENR
concept: If your radio fails and you can't communicate with ATC, you set your transponder to 7600. This code tells controllers that you've got a communications problem but can hear them (or understand their intent). You'll follow standard procedures: maintain your clearance, hold your assigned altitude, and proceed to your destination if safe to do so. At your destination aerodrome, you'll watch for light signals from the tower since you can't receive radio. The 7600 code is your silent declaration of the problem, and experienced controllers understand what it means. It's a backup system that keeps you and everyone else safe when communications fail. You must declare the code promptly and maintain heightened awareness without radio guidance.
exam_tip: 7600 = communications failure. Set immediately and follow standard procedures. Watch for light signals.
key_facts: 7600 = communications failure
Alerts ATC to radio problem
Follow assigned clearance and altitude
Watch tower for light signal guidance

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate that the aircraft is being subjected to unlawful interference. AIP ENR
concept: If you're subject to unlawful interference--hijacking, threatening behaviour, or hostile action--you set your transponder to 7500. This code immediately signals to authorities that you're in a hostage situation or under threat. Combined with an appropriate radio declaration (if safe to make), it triggers emergency response protocols. You'd follow instructions from the perpetrators while the transponder code alerts authorities to mount a response. It's a rare code in most pilots' experience, but every pilot needs to know it and understand that using it triggers maximum response. The code's existence acknowledges that aircraft can become targets of unlawful interference, and the system's designed to alert authorities and protect lives.
exam_tip: 7500 = unlawful interference. Set if hijacked or threatened. Authorities respond immediately.
key_facts: 7500 = unlawful interference/hijacking
Signals hostage situation to authorities
Triggers emergency response
Rarely used but critically important

syllabus_desc: Describe the means by which ATC will verify the transmission of an emergency SSR transponder code. AIP ENR
concept: If your radio fails, you set your transponder to 7600 and follow a set sequence. First, you attempt to restore communications--check frequencies, adjust volume, try alternate transmitters. If communications won't restore, you continue your flight maintaining your last assigned altitude and heading. You proceed to your destination aerodrome if safe, or to the nearest suitable aerodrome if yours isn't reachable. At the destination, you'll use light signals from the tower for landing clearance and listen for any emergency frequency broadcasts that might give you information. You maintain heightened situational awareness without radio traffic advisories. Once on the ground, you report the communications failure to ATC. The AIP ENR gives the full procedure. It's a logical, orderly response to an emergency that tests your judgment and airmanship.
exam_tip: 7600, attempt to restore comms, maintain altitude/heading, proceed to destination, watch for light signals.
key_facts: Set 7600 transponder code immediately
Attempt to restore communications
Maintain last assigned altitude and heading
Watch tower for light signal guidance at destination

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of the speechless technique using unmodulated transmissions. AIP ENR
concept: International emergency frequency 121.5 MHz is monitored worldwide and used for declaring emergencies when normal frequencies fail. In New Zealand, you'd also be aware of your local emergency frequency and the standard air-ground frequency. These frequencies exist precisely so that when normal communications channels are congested or unavailable, you've got a clear path to get help. 121.5 is monitored by ATC facilities, pilots, and rescue services. You'd declare your emergency on this frequency if you can't reach your assigned frequency. The AIP GEN lists emergency frequencies and their monitoring arrangements. Knowing them gives you confidence that help's available even in worst-case scenarios.
exam_tip: 121.5 MHz = international emergency frequency. Monitored worldwide. Use if other frequencies fail.
key_facts: 121.5 MHz = primary emergency frequency
Monitored worldwide by ATC and rescue
Use when normal frequencies unavailable
Published in AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: Describe and interpret ground-air visual signal codes. AIP GEN
